<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Mistake 1:** Confusing **9+0 (centriole)** with **9+2 (axoneme)**. The "+0" refers to the *absence of a central microtubule pair in the centriole* — not "0 triplets". Deduct 2 marks if interchanged.
> - **Mistake 2:** Stating "centrosome = 2 centrioles" **without** mentioning the **pericentriolar material (PCM)**. The PCM is the **functional** part. Deduct 1 mark.
> - **Mistake 3:** Saying plant cells have centrosomes. They have **acentriolar MTOCs**. Deduct 1 mark.
> - **Mistake 4:** Failing to link centriole duplication to **S-phase**, not G1 or M. Deduct 1 mark.
> - **Mistake 5:** In numerical questions, **forgetting the "1 centrosome → 2 centrosomes"** numerical invariant at cytokinesis. Deduct 1 mark.

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the centrosome and list its two principal structural components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **centrosome** is the principal **microtubule-organizing center (MTOC)** of a eukaryotic animal cell, responsible for nucleating and organizing cytoplasmic microtubules.

It is composed of two structural components:
1. **A pair of centrioles** — also called the **diplosome**, comprising a mother and daughter centriole arranged orthogonally (at right angles).
2. **Pericentriolar material (PCM)** — a proteinaceous matrix surrounding the centrioles, containing **γ-tubulin ring complexes (γ-TuRCs)** that nucleate microtubule polymerization.

> **Marking split:** Definition (1m) + Diplosome (1m) + PCM (1m).

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between the 9+0 and 9+2 microtubule arrangements with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | 9+0 Arrangement | 9+2 Arrangement |
|---------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Architecture | 9 outer microtubule triplets; **no central pair** | 9 outer microtubule doublets; **2 central singlets** |
| Example | **Centriole / Basal body** | **Axoneme of motile cilium / flagellum** |
| Function | Microtubule organization (MTOC) | Motility (ciliary/flagellar beating) |

> **Marking split:** 9+0 explanation + example (1.5m) + 9+2 explanation + example (1.5m).

---

## 📕 PART B — Essay / Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Each Part B question contains sub-parts **(a) for 7 marks** and **(b) for 7 marks**. Two complete alternative choices are provided below.

---

### **🟦 QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the **ultrastructure of a centriole** with a labeled diagram. Mention its dimensions, microtubule organization, and the 9+0 arrangement. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **centrosome duplication cycle** during the cell cycle. Highlight the molecular players CDK2/Cyclin-E, Plk4, and Aurora A. (7 marks)

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Definition and shape [1 mark]:**
A centriole is a **cylindrical, barrel-shaped organelle** with a characteristic **cartwheel cross-section**. It serves as the core structural unit of the centrosome.

**Step 2 — Dimensions [1 mark]:**
- Length: **~0.4 µm (≈ 400 nm)**
- Outer diameter: **~0.2 µm (≈ 200 nm)**
- Inner diameter: ~0.1 µm

**Step 3 — Microtubule arrangement — the 9+0 pattern [3 marks]:**
The centriole wall is composed of **9 evenly spaced triplets of microtubules** arranged circumferentially, with no central microtubule pair (hence **9 outer triplets + 0 central = 9+0**).

Each triplet contains:
- **A-tubule** — complete microtubule (13 protofilaments) — serves as the template
- **B-tubule** — fused to A, sharing 3 protofilaments
- **C-tubule** — fused to B, sharing 3 protofilaments

The triplets are interconnected via **linker proteins** and connected to the central cartwheel hub via radial spokes.

**Step 4 — Functional polarity [2 marks]:**
- **Proximal end**: anchored to PCM, contains the cartwheel hub.
- **Distal end**: open, extends into the cytoplasm, can act as a basal body for cilia.

> **Marks split:** Definition (1m) + Dimensions (1m) + 9+0 architecture (3m) + Polarity (2m).

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — G1 phase [1 mark]:**
A cell contains **one mature centrosome** with a pair of engaged centrioles (mother + daughter). This is the "licensed but unduplicated" state.

**Step 2 — S phase — Duplication initiation [2 marks]:**
- Trigger: **CDK2/Cyclin-E** activation commits the cell to S-phase.
- The mother centriole templates a **procentriole** orthogonal to itself via the action of **Plk4 (Polo-like kinase 4)** — the master regulator of centriole biogenesis.
- After duplication, the cell contains **4 centrioles still tethered within one PCM**.

**Step 3 — G2 phase — Maturation and disengagement [2 marks]:**
- **Separase** cleaves the centriole-cohesion linker proteins.
- The two pairs disengage into **two independent centrosomes**.
- **Aurora A kinase** phosphorylates **Pericentrin (PCNT)** to recruit massive amounts of **γ-TuRCs** to the PCM — this is **centrosome maturation**.

**Step 4 — M phase — Bipolar spindle formation [1 mark]:**
The two centrosomes migrate to opposite poles, nucleate **astral and spindle microtubules**, and form a **bipolar mitotic spindle** essential for accurate chromosome segregation.

**Step 5 — Cytokinesis — Numerical reset [1 mark]:**
Each daughter cell inherits exactly **one centrosome = 2 centrioles**, restoring the invariant for the next cycle.

> **Marks split:** G1 (1m) + S-phase duplication (2m) + G2 maturation (2m) + M-phase spindle (1m) + Cytokinesis reset (1m).

---

### **🟥 QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **functional significance of the centrosome** in cell division. Discuss how centrosome amplification leads to aneuploidy and its role in cancer cytogenetics. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare the **structure and function of centrioles, basal bodies, and axonemes**. Use a suitable comparison table. Mention the 9+0 vs 9+2 distinction clearly. (7 marks)

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Role in spindle pole organization [2 marks]:**
The duplicated centrosomes migrate to opposite poles of the cell during prophase, where they form the two **spindle poles** of the mitotic apparatus. The **astral microtubules** they nucleate anchor the spindle to the cell cortex, while the **spindle microtubules** capture kinetochores of chromosomes.

**Step 2 — Bipolarity ensures equal segregation [1 mark]:**
Bipolarity is the **prerequisite for equational chromosome segregation**. A single pole or multipolar spindle leads to unequal division.

**Step 3 — Centrosome amplification — definition [1 mark]:**
**Centrosome amplification** = a cell with **>2 centrosomes** at mitosis. This is observed in **>80% of solid tumors** (breast, prostate, colon, lung).

**Step 4 — Mechanism of aneuploidy [2 marks]:**
With $N > 2$ centrosomes, multipolar spindles form. Chromosomes segregate to 3 or more poles, leading to:
- **Lagging chromosomes** during anaphase
- **Merotelic kinetochore attachments**
- **Chromosome mis-segregation** → daughter cells with abnormal karyotypes (**aneuploidy**)

**Step 5 — Cancer cytogenetic relevance [1 mark]:**
Aneuploidy is a **hallmark of cancer**; it is the cytogenetic bridge between centrosome dysfunction and **chromosomal instability (CIN)**, both of which drive **tumor heterogeneity** and **therapy resistance**.

> **Marks split:** Spindle pole (2m) + Bipolarity (1m) + Amplification definition (1m) + Aneuploidy mechanism (2m) + Cancer relevance (1m).

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Comparative overview [1 mark]:**
All three structures are microtubule-based, with evolutionary and functional kinship. The **centriole** is the precursor of the **basal body**, which templates the **axoneme**.

**Step 2 — Centriole [2 marks]:**
- Architecture: **9 triplets of microtubules (9+0)**
- Location: within the centrosome (centriolar)
- Function: **microtubule-organizing center (MTOC)**
- Length: ~0.4 µm

**Step 3 — Basal body [2 marks]:**
- Architecture: **same 9+0** as centriole
- Location: docked at the **apical plasma membrane**
- Function: **template for cilium assembly**; anchors the cilium
- Origin: derived from a centriole that has migrated to the membrane

**Step 4 — Axoneme [2 marks]:**
- Architecture: **9 doublets + 2 central singlets (9+2)** in motile cilia
- Location: inside the cilium shaft
- Function: **ciliary/flagellar motility** (sliding of doublets via dynein arms)
- Length: variable, 1–50 µm

> **Marks split:** Overview (1m) + Centriole (2m) + Basal body (2m) + Axoneme (2m).

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] The **centrosome = 2 centrioles (diplosome) + PCM**. PCM is the functional hub containing **γ-TuRCs**.
- [x] A **centriole** has the **9+0 architecture** — 9 triplets of microtubules, no central pair.
- [x] An **axoneme** of a motile cilium has the **9+2 architecture** — 9 outer doublets + 2 central singlets.
- [x] Centriole dimensions: **~0.4 µm long, ~0.2 µm diameter**. Each triplet has tubules A, B, C.
- [x] Centriole biogenesis is **semi-conservative and templated** — each parent centriole templates a procentriole.
- [x] **Plk4** is the master regulator of centriole duplication; its overexpression causes **centrosome amplification**.
- [x] **CDK2/Cyclin-E** couples centrosome duplication to **S-phase** (one-to-one with DNA replication).
- [x] **Aurora A** and **Pericentrin** mediate **centrosome maturation** in G2.
- [x] The numerical invariant: **1 centrosome (2 centrioles) → 2 centrosomes (4 centrioles) → 1 centrosome per daughter cell**.
- [x] **Centrosome amplification → multipolar spindle → aneuploidy** is the cytogenetic signature of cancer.
- [x] **Plant cells lack centrioles** but possess **acentriolar MTOCs** — a common viva trap.
- [x] Centrioles can convert to **basal bodies** to template cilia/flagella (ciliogenesis).
- [x] Centriole **distal vs proximal polarity**: proximal = cartwheel/PCM; distal = open end.
- [x] **Total protofilaments per centriole = 9 × 33 = 297**. (Quick numerical fact for KTU problems.)
- [x] **Failure of centrosome duplication licensing** → either acentrosomal mitosis (some flies/early embryos) or supernumerary centrosomes (cancer).
- [x] Ciliopathies (Bardet-Biedl, PKD) and microcephaly (MCPH) are **centrosome-linked clinical conditions** relevant to KTU essay questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Karyotype analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBB861 - CYTOGENETICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Karyotype analysis # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Karyotype Analysis** is the systematic characterization, arrangement, and visualization of an organism's complete set of chromosomes (the **karyotype**) from a somatic cell, ordered according to a standardized classification based on **size, centromere position, and banding pattern**. The term originates from the Greek *"karyon"* (κάρυον, meaning "kernel" or "nucleus") and *"typos"* (τύπος, meaning "general form"). In the human genome, the standard karyotype comprises **46 chromosomes**, organized as **23 homologous pairs** — **22 pairs of autosomes** and **1 pair of sex chromosomes (XX or XY)**. A **normal human karyotype** is conventionally written as: $$46, \text{XX}$$ (typical female) $$46, \text{XY}$$ (typical male) Any deviation from this standard count or structural arrangement is termed a **chromosomal aberration**, which forms the diagnostic basis of clinical cytogenetics. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Library Cataloging System" Imagine a massive library with **46 volumes of encyclopedias**, where each volume is bound in two identical copies (the homologous pair — one inherited from the mother, one from the father). - **Chromosome preparation** is like laying every book open on a long table. - **Staining (banding)** is like dipping each page in colored ink so that chapter patterns become visible. - **Photography (Karyotyping)** is taking a snapshot of all 46 books at once. - **Karyotype analysis** is when a librarian assigns each volume a specific shelf position (Group A, B, C…) based on its height, the position of its "spine" (centromere), and its unique page-pattern fingerprint (banding pattern). > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight**: Just as a missing, duplicated, or mis-shelved book disrupts the library catalog, any **aneuploidy** (wrong chromosome number), **deletion**, **duplication**, **translocation**, or **inversion** in the karyotype manifests as a clinical genetic disorder — such as **Down syndrome (Trisomy 21)**, **Turner syndrome (45,X)**, or **Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (Philadelphia translocation t(9;22))**. --- ## 1.3 Standardized Metrics Used in Karyotype Reporting | Parameter | Standard Value / Convention | |---|---| | Total chromosomes (Homo sapiens) | **46** | | Autosome pairs | **22** | | Sex chromosome pairs | **1** | | Resolution of standard banding | **~400–550 bands per haploid genome** | | Resolution of high-resolution banding | **~850 bands per haploid genome** | | Cell stage arrested for analysis | **Metaphase** of mitosis | | Conventional stain | **Giemsa (G-banding)** | --- ## 1.4 Historical Context (Syllabus Highlight) > [!NOTE] > - **1956**: Joe Hin Tjio and Albert Levan correctly determined the human chromosome number as **46**. > - **1959**: Jérôme Lejeune discovered **Trisomy 21** in Down syndrome — the first chromosomal disease identified. > - **1960**: The **Denver Conference** standardized human chromosome classification into Groups **A–G**. > - **1970s onward**: Introduction of **G-banding**, **Q-banding**, and **Fluorescence In Situ Hybridization (FISH)** dramatically improved resolution. # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Six Sequential Pillars of Karyotype Analysis A complete karyotype analysis workflow consists of six standardized stages. Each stage must be performed with clinical-grade precision. ### 🔹 Pillar 1 — Cell Culture & Synchronization - Lymphocytes from peripheral blood (or amniocytes, bone marrow, fibroblasts) are cultured in a mitogen-rich medium (e.g., **PHA — Phytohemagglutinin**). - Cells are arrested at **metaphase** using **colchicine** (a microtubule polymerization inhibitor that prevents spindle formation). ### 🔹 Pillar 2 — Hypotonic Treatment & Fixation - Cells are exposed to a hypotonic solution (typically **0.075 M KCl**) → cells swell. - Fixation in **Carnoy's fluid** (methanol : acetic acid = 3:1) preserves chromosome morphology. ### 🔹 Pillar 3 — Slide Preparation & Banding - Chromosomes are spread on a glass slide and stained. - Most common banding technique: **G-banding** (Giemsa stain after trypsin digestion). ### 🔹 Pillar 4 — Microscopy & Image Capture - Metaphase spreads are photographed under a **light microscope** at **1000× magnification**. - Multiple metaphase spreads (typically **20 cells** are counted, **5 cells** are karyotyped) are examined. ### 🔹 Pillar 5 — Chromosome Pairing & Ordering - Chromosomes are arranged in a **karyogram** by homologous pairs. - Ordering follows the **International System for Human Cytogenomic Nomenclature (ISCN)**. ### 🔹 Pillar 6 — Nomenclature & Reporting - Final report follows ISCN 2024 conventions, e.g., **46,XX,del(5p)** denotes a female with a deletion on the short arm of chromosome 5 (Cri-du-chat syndrome). --- ## 2.2 The Denver Classification System (Groups A–G) > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Denver Classification (1960)** segregates the 23 human chromosome pairs into **7 morphological groups (A–G)** based on **size** and **centromere position**. | Group | Chromosome Pairs | Size | Centromere Position | Notable Features | |---|---|---|---|---| | **A** | 1, 2, 3 | Largest | Metacentric (1, 3) / Submetacentric (2) | Easily distinguishable | | **B** | 4, 5 | Large | Submetacentric | Difficult to differentiate 4 vs 5 | | **C** | 6–12, X | Medium | Submetacentric | Largest group; X chromosome resembles group C | | **D** | 13, 14, 15 | Medium | Acrocentric | Carry nucleolar organizer regions (NORs); **Trisomy 13 = Patau syndrome** | | **E** | 16, 17, 18 | Small | Metacentric (16) / Submetacentric (17, 18) | **Trisomy 18 = Edwards syndrome** | | **F** | 19, 20 | Smaller | Metacentric | Hard to differentiate | | **G** | 21, 22, Y | Smallest | Acrocentric | **Trisomy 21 = Down syndrome**; Y is usually smaller | --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Quantitative Metrics > [!NOTE] > The following equations are **board-examination essentials** for karyotype numerical questions. ### 🔸 Equation 1 — Arm Ratio (r) The **arm ratio** quantifies centromere position and is the primary morphological classifier. $$r = \dfrac{q}{p}$$ Where: - $p$ = length of the **short arm (petit arm)** - $q$ = length of the **long arm** ### 🔸 Equation 2 — Centromeric Index (CI) The **centromeric index** expresses the short-arm length as a fraction of the **total chromosome length**. $$CI = \dfrac{p}{p+q} \times 100\,\%$$ ### 🔸 Morphological Classification by Centromeric Index | Morphology | CI Range (%) | Arm Ratio (r) | Example Chromosomes | |---|---|---|---| | **Metacentric** | $37.5 \leq CI \leq 50$ | $1.0 \leq r \leq 1.7$ | 1, 3, 16, 19, 20 | | **Submetacentric** | $25 \leq CI < 37.5$ | $1.7 < r \leq 3.0$ | 2, 4, 5, 6–12, X, 17, 18 | | **Acrocentric** | $CI < 25$ | $r > 3.0$ | 13, 14, 15, 21, 22, Y | | **Telocentric** | $CI = 0$ | $r = \infty$ | Absent in humans | ### 🔸 Equation 3 — Relative Length $$RL_i = \dfrac{L_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{22} L_j} \times 100\,\%$$ Where $L_i$ is the length of chromosome $i$, and the denominator sums the lengths of all 22 autosomes. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Clinical Utility - **Pre-natal diagnostics**: Amniocentesis and CVS (Chorionic Villus Sampling) coupled with karyotyping detect fetal aneuploidies. - **Cancer cytogenetics**: Identification of **Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22)(q34;q11)** in CML dictates **Imatinib therapy**. - **Forensic science**: DNA profiling complements karyotype-based species identification. - **Bioinformatics pipelines**: Automated karyotyping software (e.g., **MetaSystems**, **Ikaros**, **CytoVision**) uses **machine-learning image segmentation** to accelerate clinical reporting. # 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example — Determining Centromere Position from Arm Measurements > [!NOTE] > This derivation demonstrates the **exact KTU-style numerical** that examiners love to ask: *"Given the p-arm and q-arm lengths of a chromosome, classify its morphology."* ### 📋 Problem Statement A metaphase chromosome was measured using calibrated image-analysis software. The following data were recorded: - Length of short arm: $p = 3.0\,\mu\text{m}$ - Length of long arm: $q = 5.0\,\mu\text{m}$ **Task:** Calculate the (a) arm ratio, (b) centromeric index, and (c) classify the chromosome morphology. --- ### 🧮 Part (a) — Arm Ratio Derivation The arm ratio is defined as the quotient of the long arm to the short arm: $$ r = \dfrac{q}{p} $$ Substituting the measured values: $$ r = \dfrac{5.0\,\mu\text{m}}{3.0\,\mu\text{m}} $$ $$ r = 1.667 $$ $$ \boxed{r \approx 1.67} $$ **Logical interpretation:** Since $r = 1.67$ falls within the boundary interval $1.0 \leq r \leq 1.7$, the chromosome *could* be classified as **metacentric**. However, the centromeric index will give a more definitive classification. --- ### 🧮 Part (b) — Centromeric Index Derivation The centromeric index is the ratio of the short arm to the total chromosome length, expressed as a percentage. The total chromosome length is first computed: $$ L_{\text{total}} = p + q $$ $$ L_{\text{total}} = 3.0\,\mu\text{m} + 5.0\,\mu\text{m} = 8.0\,\mu\text{m} $$ The centromeric index is then: $$ CI = \dfrac{p}{p+q} \times 100\,\% $$ $$ CI = \dfrac{3.0\,\mu\text{m}}{8.0\,\mu\text{m}} \times 100\,\% $$ $$ CI = 0.375 \times 100\,\% $$ $$ \boxed{CI = 37.5\,\%} $$ **Logical interpretation:** The centromeric index of exactly **37.5 %** lies precisely on the **boundary** between metacentric and submetacentric morphology. --- ### 🧮 Part (c) — Morphological Classification Using the strict boundary rule: - **Metacentric:** $37.5 \leq CI \leq 50$ → inclusive lower bound - **Submetacentric:** $25 \leq CI < 37.5$ → exclusive lower bound Since $CI = 37.5$ satisfies the **inclusive** metacentric inequality: $$ \boxed{\text{Chromosome morphology: Metacentric}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Trick Question Alert:** Students often misclassify at the boundary. **Always use the inclusive inequality ($\leq$) for metacentric** to claim metacentricity. The boundary belongs to the metacentric category. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Six Disorders Diagnosed by Karyotyping | Disorder | Karyotype Notation | Numerical / Structural Abnormality | Group Affected | |---|---|---|---| | **Down syndrome** | $47,\text{XX},+21$ | Trisomy 21 (numerical) | G | | **Edwards syndrome** | $47,\text{XY},+18$ | Trisomy 18 (numerical) | E | | **Patau syndrome** | $47,\text{XX},+13$ | Trisomy 13 (numerical) | D | | **Turner syndrome** | $45,\text{X}$ | Monosomy X (numerical) | C (loss) | | **Klinefelter syndrome** | $47,\text{XXY}$ | Extra X (numerical) | C/G | | **Cri-du-chat** | $46,\text{XX},\text{del}(5\text{p})$ | Deletion of 5p (structural) | B | | **CML (Philadelphia)** | $46,\text{XY},\text{t}(9;22)(\text{q}34;\text{q}11)$ | Reciprocal translocation (structural) | C/G | | **Burkitt lymphoma** | $46,\text{XY},\text{t}(8;14)(\text{q}24;\text{q}32)$ | Translocation involving MYC (structural) | C/D | --- ## 3.3 Banding Resolution Equation The number of visible bands per haploid genome ($N_b$) is empirically related to the average chromosome condensation level, approximated as: $$ N_b \approx \dfrac{L_{\text{extended}}}{L_{\text{condensed}}} \times N_b^{\text{(standard)}} $$ For prometaphase high-resolution banding: $$ N_b \approx \dfrac{10\,\mu\text{m}}{6\,\mu\text{m}} \times 400 \approx 666 \text{ bands} $$ Practically, high-resolution banding reaches **~850 bands** because earlier-stage chromosomes (prometaphase) are physically more extended than metaphase chromosomes. # 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — End-to-End Karyotype Analysis Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Sample Collection
Blood / Amniotic fluid / Bone marrow] --> B[Cell Culture
with PHA mitogen 37 deg C 72h] B --> C[Colchicine Arrest
Metaphase block] C --> D[Hypotonic Treatment
0.075 M KCl swelling] D --> E[Fixation
Carnoy fluid 3 to 1] E --> F[Slide Preparation
Drop from height] F --> G[Banding Staining
Giemsa G-banding] G --> H[Microscopy
1000x magnification] H --> I{Sufficient Metaphase
Spread Quality} I -- No --> J[Repeat Slide Prep] I -- Yes --> K[Image Capture
20 cells counted] K --> L[Karyogram Assembly
Homologous pairing] L --> M[ISCN Nomenclature
Reporting] M --> N[Clinical Diagnosis] style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b style N fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style G fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style L fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Denver Classification Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR S[Human Karyotype
46 Chromosomes] --> SA[22 Autosome Pairs] S --> SB[1 Sex Chromosome Pair] SA --> SA1[Group A
Pairs 1 to 3
Largest Metacentric] SA --> SA2[Group B
Pairs 4 to 5
Large Submetacentric] SA --> SA3[Group C
Pairs 6 to 12
Medium Submetacentric] SA --> SA4[Group D
Pairs 13 to 15
Medium Acrocentric] SA --> SA5[Group E
Pairs 16 to 18
Small Meta or Submeta] SA --> SA6[Group F
Pairs 19 to 20
Smaller Metacentric] SA --> SA7[Group G
Pairs 21 to 22
Smallest Acrocentric] SB --> SB1[XX Female] SB --> SB2[XY Male] SA4 --> NOR1[Contains NOR
Nucleolar Organizer Region] SA7 --> NOR2[Contains NOR
and rRNA Genes] style S fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1 style SA fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style SB fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f style NOR1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style NOR2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` ## 4.3 Centromere Position Schematic — Text-Only Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Short Arm p] --- C1[Centromere] --- Q1[Long Arm q] P2[Short Arm p] --- C2[Centromere] --- Q2[Long Arm q] P3[Short Arm p] --- C3[Centromere] --- Q3[Long Arm q] P4[Short Arm p] --- C4[Centromere] --- Q4[Long Arm q] C1 --> META[Metacentric
CI 37.5 to 50 percent] C2 --> SUBM[Submetacentric
CI 25 to 37.5 percent] C3 --> ACRO[Acrocentric
CI below 25 percent] C4 --> TELO[Telocentric
CI equals 0] style META fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style SUBM fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style ACRO fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style TELO fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c ``` # 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define karyotype. List the morphological criteria used in the Denver classification system for arranging human chromosomes.** #### 📝 Model Answer (3 Marks): **Definition (1.5 Marks):** A **karyotype** is the complete set of chromosomes in an individual or species, visualized as a systematized **karyogram** in which homologous chromosome pairs are arranged in a standard order. **Denver Criteria (1.5 Marks):** The Denver classification arranges chromosomes using: 1. **Chromosome size** (decreasing order) 2. **Centromere position** (metacentric → submetacentric → acrocentric) 3. **Banding pattern** (under G-banding, each pair shows a unique pattern) These criteria group the 22 autosome pairs into **7 groups (A through G)** plus the sex chromosomes. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is the centromeric index? Compute the centromeric index of a chromosome whose p-arm is $2.4\,\mu\text{m}$ and q-arm is $4.8\,\mu\text{m}$. State its morphological class.** #### 📝 Model Answer (3 Marks): **Centromeric Index Definition (1 Mark):** The centromeric index is the percentage ratio of the short-arm length to the total chromosome length, used to classify centromere position. **Calculation (1.5 Marks):** $$ CI = \dfrac{p}{p+q} \times 100\,\% $$ $$ CI = \dfrac{2.4}{2.4 + 4.8} \times 100\,\% $$ $$ CI = \dfrac{2.4}{7.2} \times 100\,\% = 33.33\,\% $$ **Morphological Class (0.5 Marks):** Since $25 \leq 33.33 < 37.5$, the chromosome is **submetacentric**. --- ## 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Choice-Based) > **ESE Module 1 Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **"Karyotype analysis is the cornerstone of clinical cytogenetics."** **(a)** Describe in detail the six sequential steps involved in the preparation of a karyotype from a peripheral blood sample. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Compare and contrast G-banding, Q-banding, R-banding, and C-banding techniques in a tabular form, highlighting their stain chemistry and diagnostic applications. **(7 Marks)** #### 📝 Model Solution **Part (a) — Six Steps of Karyotype Preparation (7 Marks):** 1. **Sample Collection & Culture Setup (1 Mark):** 2–5 mL of heparinized peripheral blood is collected. Lymphocytes are cultured in **RPMI 1640 medium** supplemented with **Phytohemagglutinin (PHA)** — a T-cell mitogen that triggers mitosis. Culture is maintained at **37 °C** for **~72 hours**. 2. **Mitotic Arrest (1 Mark):** **Colchicine** (or colcemid) is added to inhibit microtubule polymerization, arresting cells at the **metaphase stage**, when chromosomes are maximally condensed and visible. 3. **Hypotonic Treatment (1 Mark):** Cells are treated with **0.075 M KCl** hypotonic solution, causing osmotic swelling that spreads chromosomes apart within the cell. 4. **Fixation (1 Mark):** Cells are fixed in **Carnoy's fluid** (methanol:glacial acetic acid = 3:1) to preserve chromosome morphology and prevent degradation. 5. **Slide Preparation & Staining (1.5 Marks):** Fixed cell suspension is dropped onto a chilled, grease-free slide from a height. Slides are stained via **G-banding** — trypsin digestion followed by **Giemsa staining** to produce characteristic light and dark bands. 6. **Microscopy, Karyogram Assembly & ISCN Reporting (1.5 Marks):** Metaphase spreads are photographed under a **1000× light microscope**. **20 cells** are counted and **5 karyotyped**. Chromosomes are paired as homologs and arranged in the karyogram using **ISCN nomenclature** (e.g., *46,XY* or *47,XX,+21*). --- **Part (b) — Comparison of Banding Techniques (7 Marks):** | Feature | G-banding | Q-banding | R-banding | C-banding | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Stain / Method** | Trypsin + Giemsa | Quinacrine mustard | Heat denaturation + Giemsa | NaOH + Giemsa | | **Pattern Produced** | AT-rich (dark) / GC-rich (light) | AT-rich (bright) / GC-rich (dim) | Reverse of G-banding | Constitutive heterochromatin only | | **Fluorescence Required?** | No (bright-field) | **Yes** (UV excitation) | No | No | | **Diagnostic Strength** | Whole-genome identification | Y chromosome / polymorphisms | Telomeres, GC-rich ends | Centromeres, Yqh, 1qh, 9qh, 16qh | | **Resolution** | ~400–550 bands | ~400–500 bands | Similar to G | Limited (heterochromatin only) | > **Synthesis (1 Mark):** G-banding is the **clinical gold standard**, R-banding is **complementary** (best at telomeres), Q-banding detects **Y-chromatin polymorphisms**, and C-banding is restricted to **centromeric heterochromatin** studies. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** With neat labeled diagrams, describe the **Denver classification** of human chromosomes. List the chromosome pairs belonging to each group. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A chromosome has p-arm = $1.5\,\mu\text{m}$ and q-arm = $6.0\,\mu\text{m}$. Compute its **arm ratio** and **centromeric index**, and classify its morphology. Mention two chromosomal disorders diagnosed using karyotype analysis. **(7 Marks)** #### 📝 Model Solution **Part (a) — Denver Classification (7 Marks):** The **Denver Classification (1960)** groups the **23 pairs (46 chromosomes)** of the human karyotype into **7 autosome groups (A–G)** plus the sex chromosomes, based on **decreasing size** and **centromere position**. | Group | Pairs | Size | Centromere | |---|---|---|---| | **A** | 1, 2, 3 | Largest | Metacentric / Submetacentric | | **B** | 4, 5 | Large | Submetacentric | | **C** | 6–12, X | Medium | Submetacentric | | **D** | 13, 14, 15 | Medium | Acrocentric | | **E** | 16, 17, 18 | Small | Metacentric / Submetacentric | | **F** | 19, 20 | Smaller | Metacentric | | **G** | 21, 22, Y | Smallest | Acrocentric | *(Include the karyogram block diagram from SECTION 4.2 as the labeled schematic — 2 Marks.)* --- **Part (b) — Numerical & Clinical Reasoning (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — Arm Ratio (2 Marks):** $$ r = \dfrac{q}{p} = \dfrac{6.0\,\mu\text{m}}{1.5\,\mu\text{m}} = 4.0 $$ **Step 2 — Centromeric Index (2 Marks):** $$ CI = \dfrac{p}{p+q} \times 100\,\% = \dfrac{1.5}{1.5 + 6.0} \times 100\,\% $$ $$ CI = \dfrac{1.5}{7.5} \times 100\,\% = 20\,\% $$ **Step 3 — Morphological Classification (1 Mark):** Since $CI = 20\,\% < 25\,\%$, the chromosome is **acrocentric**. **Step 4 — Two Karyotype-Diagnosed Disorders (2 Marks):** 1. **Down syndrome** — karyotype *47,XX,+21* (Trisomy 21; Group G). 2. **Turner syndrome** — karyotype *45,X* (Monosomy X; loss from Group C). --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Karyotype Questions:** > 1. **Boundary Blunder at CI = 37.5%**: The **inclusive** inequality belongs to **metacentric**. Writing *"submetacentric because $CI < 37.5$"* will cost 1 mark. > 2. **Forgetting to multiply by 100**: Always express the centromeric index as a **percentage**, not a decimal. > 3. **Confusing arm ratio with centromeric index**: Arm ratio = $q/p$; Centromeric index = $p/(p+q)$. Examiners specifically test whether students know the difference. > 4. **Denver Group C Confusion**: Students forget that the **X chromosome** belongs to **Group C** (size- and morphology-wise), not Group G. Only the **Y chromosome** belongs to Group G. > 5. **ISCN Notation Errors**: Comma placement matters — *46,XX,+21* has the comma **before** the abnormality. Writing *"46XX+21"* is **wrong syntax** and costs a mark. > 6. **Unit Omission**: Always specify *$\mu\text{m}$* for chromosome arm lengths. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Keep These 12 Points at Your Fingertips:** - ✅ The **human karyotype** has **46 chromosomes = 23 pairs** (22 autosomes + 1 sex pair). - ✅ **Karyotyping** = pairing + ordering + classifying chromosomes. - ✅ **Metaphase** is the optimal stage for chromosome visualization. - ✅ **Colchicine** arrests cells at metaphase. - ✅ **Carnoy's fluid** = methanol : acetic acid = **3 : 1**. - ✅ **G-banding** is the clinical gold standard. - ✅ **Denver classification** has **7 groups: A, B, C, D, E, F, G**. - ✅ Group G includes **21, 22, and Y** — *Trisomy 21 = Down syndrome*. - ✅ **Arm ratio** $r = q/p$; **Centromeric index** $CI = [p/(p+q)] \times 100\,\%$. - ✅ Morphology boundaries: **Metacentric** ($CI \geq 37.5\%$) | **Submetacentric** ($25 \leq CI < 37.5\%$) | **Acrocentric** ($CI < 25\%$). - ✅ ISCN 2024 nomenclature: *46,XX* (normal female) | *46,XY* (normal male) | *47,XX,+21* (Down syndrome) | *45,X* (Turner) | *47,XXY* (Klinefelter). - ✅ **Philadelphia chromosome** t(9;22) is the diagnostic cytogenetic hallmark of **Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML)**.

topics.title: Sex determination and sex chromosomes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBB861 - CYTOGENETICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sex determination and sex chromosomes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Sex Determination?

**Sex determination** is the biological mechanism by which the sexual fate of an organism—male, female, hermaphrodite, or other categories—is established during embryogenesis. In cytogenetics, it specifically refers to the **chromosomal basis** of sex differentiation, governed by the **sex chromosome complement** (karyotype) inherited from the parents at fertilization.

In the KTU 2024 Cytogenetics framework, sex determination is treated as a **special case of chromosomal inheritance** where the sex chromosomes (X and Y in mammals, Z and W in birds, etc.) carry the master-switch genes that trigger divergent developmental cascades in the gonads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Sex determination* refers to the genetic and chromosomal mechanisms that decide whether an individual develops as a male or a female. It encompasses chromosomal systems (XX/XY, XX/XO, ZZ/ZW), genic balance systems, environmental systems, and the molecular switch genes (e.g., SRY) that initiate sexual differentiation.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Master Switch" of Biology

Imagine a giant industrial factory (the embryo) that can produce two completely different products: a **"Blue Widget" (male)** or a **"Pink Widget" (female)**. Both products are equally complex, but the factory starts out **identical and undifferentiated**. 

Now imagine a **single master switch** on the factory floor. If the switch is set to "BLUE," the entire production line reconfigures—different machines start, different workers are hired, and the output is a Blue Widget. If the switch is "PINK," a completely different cascade begins.

In biology:
- The **factory** = the undifferentiated embryonic gonad (bipotential gonad)
- The **master switch** = the **SRY gene** on the Y chromosome (or analogous triggers in other species)
- The **production line** = the hormone-driven developmental cascade (testis vs. ovary development)
- The **workers and machines** = downstream genes like **SOX9, DAX1, WNT4, FOXL2**

This analogy explains a profound biological truth: the **default pathway** in most mammals is the **female pathway**. The male pathway is essentially an *override*—a hijacking of the default program by the SRY gene.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Examiners:** The "default is female" concept is a high-yield KTU question. In the absence of a Y chromosome (or the SRY gene), the XX embryo develops along the female pathway. This is a recurring conceptual question in cytogenetics papers.

## 1.3 What are Sex Chromosomes?

**Sex chromosomes** are the pair of chromosomes (typically designated **X and Y** in mammals, or **Z and W** in birds) that differ between the two sexes and are responsible for sex determination. Unlike **autosomes** (the other 22 pairs in humans, or 44 in total), sex chromosomes are often **heteromorphic**—they look visibly different under the microscope.

### 1.3.1 The X Chromosome

- **Size:** Approximately **155 Mb** in humans, containing roughly **1,000 genes**.
- **Composition:** Centromere is positioned such that X has a **long arm (q)** and a **short arm (p)**.
- **Gene density:** Comparatively gene-rich for a chromosome.
- **Role in dosage:** Carries many essential, non-sex-related genes (e.g., color vision, blood clotting factor VIII, dystrophin).

### 1.3.2 The Y Chromosome

- **Size:** Approximately **60 Mb** in humans—about **one-third** the size of the X.
- **Composition:** Contains the male-determining **SRY gene** at Yp11.3, the **AZF regions** (Azoospermia Factor), and the **pseudoautosomal regions (PAR1 and PAR2)**.
- **Gene content:** Comparatively **gene-poor**, with a large heterochromatic region containing repetitive sequences.
- **Critical role:** Acts as the dominant male-determining factor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric (Homo sapiens):**
> - Total chromosomes: **46** (diploid, 2n)
> - Autosomes: **22 pairs (44 chromosomes)**
> - Sex chromosomes: **1 pair (XX in female, XY in male)**
> - Total genes on X: **~1000** | Total genes on Y: **~70–200**

## 1.4 Conceptual Map of Sex Determination Mechanisms

Sex determination is not universal. Different phyla have evolved radically different strategies:

| Mechanism | Symbol | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **XY System (Male Heterogamety)** | ♀ XX, ♂ XY | Humans, most mammals, *Drosophila* |
| **ZW System (Female Heterogamety)** | ♀ ZW, ♂ ZZ | Birds, butterflies, some reptiles |
| **XO System** | ♀ XX, ♂ X0 | Grasshoppers, some insects |
| **Haplodiploidy** | ♀ 2n, ♂ n (haploid) | Honeybees, ants, wasps |
| **Environmental (Temperature-Dependent)** | No chromosome basis | Many reptiles (e.g., crocodiles, turtles) |
| **Genic Balance (X:A ratio)** | Ratio-dependent | *Drosophila melanogaster* |

> [!NOTE]
> In **mammals**, the **Y chromosome dominates**: XXY is male (Klinefelter), XO is female (Turner), and XO is **not** male. This is the opposite of *Drosophila*, where the **X:A (X-to-Autosome) ratio** is decisive, not the Y.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The XX/XY System: The Mammalian Paradigm

In humans and most mammals, the chromosomal basis of sex determination follows a clear-cut rule:

- **Female karyotype:** **46, XX** — Two X chromosomes, no Y.
- **Male karyotype:** **46, XY** — One X and one Y.

The **Y chromosome carries the dominant male-determining factor**. Mechanistically:

1. The **SRY gene** (Sex-determining Region Y) is located at **Yp11.3**.
2. SRY encodes a transcription factor of the **HMG-box family**.
3. Around week 6–7 of human embryonic development, SRY is transiently expressed in the **bipotential gonad**.
4. SRY upregulates **SOX9** (SRY-related HMG-box gene 9).
5. SOX9 drives the differentiation of **Sertoli cells** in the testis.
6. Sertoli cells produce **Anti-Müllerian Hormone (AMH)**, which regresses the Müllerian ducts.
7. Leydig cells (also induced) produce **testosterone**, which stabilizes the Wolffian ducts into male reproductive structures.

In the **absence of SRY** (the default XX pathway), the **WNT4** and **RSPO1** genes drive the bipotential gonad to become an **ovary**, and the **FOXL2** gene maintains ovarian identity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The SRY gene acts as a "master switch," but it is the **SOX9 gene** that is the actual executor of testis determination. SRY is the trigger; SOX9 is the engine. This is a frequently tested distinction.

## 2.2 The X:A Ratio Mechanism in *Drosophila*

In *Drosophila melanogaster*, sex is determined not by the presence/absence of the Y, but by the **ratio of X chromosomes to autosomes (X:A)**.

| X:A Ratio | Sex | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **≥ 1.0** (e.g., XX;AA = 1.0) | Female | *Sex-lethal (Sxl)* gene activated early |
| **= 0.5** (e.g., XY;AA = 0.5) | Male | *Sxl* remains inactive |

- The **Sex-lethal (Sxl)** gene acts as the master switch.
- When X:A ≥ 1, **activator proteins** (e.g., *sisterless-a*, *sisterless-b*, *runt*) accumulate to threshold, and **Sxl** is activated.
- Active Sxl triggers a **female-specific splicing cascade** that maintains female development.
- In males, Sxl is not activated; default male splicing gives the male phenotype.

> [!NOTE]
> The Y chromosome in *Drosophila* is **not** male-determining—it is required only for **spermatogenesis** (it carries fertility genes). A fly with XO is a sterile male, not a female.

## 2.3 The ZW System (Female Heterogamety)

In **birds, some reptiles, butterflies, and moths**, the system is reversed:
- **Male:** **ZZ** (homogametic)
- **Female:** **ZW** (heterogametic)

The **W chromosome** carries the female-determining signal, analogous to Y in mammals. The **DMRT1** gene on the Z chromosome plays a key role: in ZZ (males), DMRT1 is double-dosed and drives testis development; in ZW (females), DMRT1 is single-dosed, insufficient to override the default female pathway.

## 2.4 Haplodiploidy in Hymenoptera

In bees, ants, and wasps:
- **Females (queens, workers):** **Diploid (2n)** — develop from fertilized eggs.
- **Males (drones):** **Haploid (n)** — develop from unfertilized eggs (**arrhenotoky**).

Sex is determined by a single gene **complementary sex determiner (csd)** locus. Heterozygosity at *csd* → female; hemizygosity (haploid) → male. Homozygosity at *csd* in diploids produces sterile diploid males.

## 2.5 Environmental Sex Determination (ESD)

In **reptiles** (crocodiles, many turtles, some lizards), sex is determined by the **incubation temperature** of the egg during a critical window of embryonic development:
- **Pattern Type Ia (e.g., many turtles):** Low temp → males, High temp → females.
- **Pattern Type Ib (e.g., crocodiles, some lizards):** Low temp → females, High temp → males.

> [!WARNING]
> Climate change is skewing sex ratios in ESD species. KTU examiners may pose a question linking this to biodiversity.

## 2.6 X-Inactivation (Lyonization) and Dosage Compensation

Because females have **two X chromosomes** and males have only one, females have potentially **double the dosage** of X-linked gene products. To equalize this, **mammals employ X-inactivation**:

- Proposed by **Mary Lyon (1961)**.
- In each somatic cell of a female (XX), **one X chromosome is randomly inactivated** during early embryogenesis.
- The inactivated X condenses into a **Barr body** (visible at the nuclear periphery).
- The number of Barr bodies = **n − 1**, where *n* is the number of X chromosomes.

| Karyotype | Phenotype | Barr Bodies |
|---|---|---|
| 46, XY (normal male) | Male | 0 |
| 46, XX (normal female) | Female | 1 |
| 47, XXY (Klinefelter) | Male | 1 |
| 47, XXX (Triple X) | Female | 2 |
| 45, X (Turner) | Female | 0 |

The inactivation is **epigenetically** maintained via **XIST RNA** (X-inactive specific transcript), which coats the X chromosome and recruits silencing complexes (**PRC2**, **histone deacetylation**, **DNA methylation**).

## 2.7 Pseudoautosomal Regions (PAR)

The **PAR1 and PAR2** of the X and Y chromosomes are short regions of **sequence homology** that pair and recombine during male meiosis. They behave like autosomes genetically (hence "pseudo-autosomal") but are located on the sex chromosomes. Genes in PAR1 include **SHOX** (Short Stature Homeobox; deletions cause Léri-Weill dyschondrosteosis) and **CRLF2**.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Number of Barr bodies** | $B = n_X - 1$ where $n_X$ = number of X chromosomes | High-yield |
| 2 | **Probability of male offspring (XX/XY)** | $P(\text{male}) = \frac{1}{2}$ | High-yield |
| 3 | **Probability of sex-linked recessive (X-linked) affected child** | $P = \text{inheritance pattern via Punnett square}$ | High-yield |
| 4 | **X:A ratio threshold (Drosophila)** | $\text{Ratio} \geq 1 \Rightarrow$ female; $\text{Ratio} \leq 0.5 \Rightarrow$ male | Moderate |
| 5 | **Y-linked (holandric) transmission** | Father → all sons, never to daughters | High-yield |
| 6 | **Sex chromosome aneuploidy** | Turner (45,X), Klinefelter (47,XXY), Triple X (47,XXX), XYY (47,XYY) | High-yield |
| 7 | **Pseudoautosomal genes** | Genes in PAR1/PAR2 undergo recombination between X and Y | Moderate |
| 8 | **SRY gene locus** | Yp11.3 | High-yield |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** When asked for the number of Barr bodies, *always* state the formula first: $B = n_X - 1$. Then plug in. Examiners reward the formula and the answer.

## 2.9 Real-World Utility of Sex Determination Studies

- **Medical genetics:** Diagnosing sex chromosome aneuploidies (e.g., Turner, Klinefelter).
- **Forensic science:** DNA-based sex determination using **amelogenin gene** (AMELX on Xp22, AMELY on Yp11).
- **Agriculture:** Sex control in livestock (sexed semen using flow cytometry of X/Y-bearing sperm).
- **Conservation:** Managing skewed sex ratios in endangered species (e.g., sea turtles, crocodiles).
- **Evolutionary biology:** Understanding how sex determination systems evolved.
- **Cancer research:** SRY and SOX9 dysregulation in some gonadal cancers.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Barr Body Count from Karyotype

**Statement:** The number of Barr bodies in a somatic cell equals the total number of X chromosomes minus 1.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

Let $n_X$ = number of X chromosomes in the karyotype. In a normal female, $n_X = 2$. The rule of dosage compensation requires that **only one X chromosome remain active per diploid cell**. Therefore, the number of *inactivated* X chromosomes (i.e., Barr bodies) is:

$$
B = n_X - 1
$$

**Verification with standard karyotypes:**

**(a) Normal male (46, XY):** $n_X = 1 \Rightarrow B = 1 - 1 = 0$. ✅ Correct.

**(b) Normal female (46, XX):** $n_X = 2 \Rightarrow B = 2 - 1 = 1$. ✅ Correct.

**(c) Turner syndrome (45, X):** $n_X = 1 \Rightarrow B = 1 - 1 = 0$. ✅ Correct.

**(d) Klinefelter syndrome (47, XXY):** $n_X = 2 \Rightarrow B = 2 - 1 = 1$. ✅ Correct.

**(e) Triple X syndrome (47, XXX):** $n_X = 3 \Rightarrow B = 3 - 1 = 2$. ✅ Correct.

**(f) Tetrasomy X (48, XXXX):** $n_X = 4 \Rightarrow B = 4 - 1 = 3$. ✅ Correct.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is one of the most heavily tested in the KTU 2024 cytogenetics module. Always specify that the rule applies to **interphase somatic nuclei**, and the Barr body represents the **late-replicating, heterochromatinized X**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Probability of an X-linked Recessive Trait in a Cross

**Problem:** A phenotypically normal mother is a carrier of an X-linked recessive disease (e.g., hemophilia A, Duchenne muscular dystrophy). The father is phenotypically normal. What is the probability that their son will be affected?

**Setup:**

- Mother's genotype: $X^H X^h$ (carrier, $H$ = normal allele, $h$ = disease allele).
- Father's genotype: $X^H Y$ (normal male).

**Punnett square derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mother's gametes: } & X^H \text{ or } X^h \\
\text{Father's gametes: } & X^H \text{ or } Y
\end{aligned}
$$

The cross produces four equally likely offspring (each with probability $\frac{1}{4}$):

| Offspring | Genotype | Phenotype | Probability |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $X^H X^H$ | Normal female | $\frac{1}{4}$ |
| 2 | $X^H X^h$ | Carrier female (phenotypically normal) | $\frac{1}{4}$ |
| 3 | $X^H Y$ | Normal male | $\frac{1}{4}$ |
| 4 | $X^h Y$ | **Affected male** | $\frac{1}{4}$ |

**Therefore:**

$$
P(\text{affected son}) = P(X^h Y) = \frac{1}{4}
$$

**Conditional probability** (given that the offspring is male):

$$
P(\text{affected} \mid \text{male}) = \frac{P(X^h Y)}{P(\text{male})} = \frac{1/4}{1/2} = \frac{1}{2}
$$

This is a classic result: **half of all sons of a carrier mother will be affected** for an X-linked recessive trait.

## 3.3 Derivation: Holandric (Y-linked) Inheritance Pattern

**Statement:** Y-linked (holandric) genes pass from father to **every son** and to **no daughter**.

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

A father has karyotype $XY$. His gametes are either $X$ or $Y$, each produced with probability $\frac{1}{2}$.

- A son inherits the **Y** from his father (with probability 1) and the **X** from his mother.
- A daughter inherits the **X** from her father (with probability 1) and the **X** from her mother.

Thus, the Y chromosome (and any genes on its non-recombining region, called the **MSY — Male-Specific Region**) is transmitted strictly along the patrilineal line.

$$
P(\text{son receives Y}) = 1, \quad P(\text{daughter receives Y}) = 0
$$

**Examples of holandric traits:** hypertrichosis pinnae auris (hairy ear rims, reported in some families), certain infertility factors (AZF deletions).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Sex Determination Across Major Taxa

| Taxon | ♀ Symbol | ♂ Symbol | Master Switch | Default Pathway | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Humans / most mammals** | XX | XY | **SRY** (Yp11.3) | Female | Y is dominant; XXY male, XO female |
| **Drosophila** | XX | XY | **X:A ratio** (Sxl) | Male | Y is fertility-only, not sex-determining |
| **Birds** | ZW | ZZ | **W (putative)** + **DMRT1 dosage** on Z | Male | Female heterogamety |
| **Butterflies/Moths** | ZW | ZZ | W-chromosome | Male | |
| **Grasshoppers** | XX | X0 | X-chromosome count | Female | No Y in males |
| **Honeybees** | 2n (diploid) | n (haploid) | **csd heterozygosity** | Male | Haplodiploidy |
| **Turtles (e.g., diamondback)** | — | — | **Incubation temperature** | — | Pattern Ia |
| **Crocodiles** | — | — | **Incubation temperature** | — | Pattern Ib |
| **Bonellia worm** | — | — | **Environmental (settling location)** | — | Larvae settling on female → male |

## 3.5 Derivation: Cumulative Probability Across Multiple Pregnancies

**Problem:** A couple has 3 children, all girls. What is the probability that the 4th child is a boy?

**Derivation:**

Sex of any child is independent of previous births. Each child has $P(\text{boy}) = \frac{1}{2}$.

$$
P(\text{4th is boy} \mid \text{first 3 are girls}) = P(\text{boy}) = \frac{1}{2}
$$

This is the **gambler's fallacy trap**—KTU examiners sometimes use this to test whether students understand independence of events.

> [!WARNING]
> Do **not** confuse with the conditional probability of having at least one boy in 4 births: $P(\geq 1 \text{ boy}) = 1 - \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^4 = 1 - \frac{1}{16} = \frac{15}{16}$.

## 3.6 Analytical Check: Why XXY is Male in Humans but Sterile

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

1. The XXY individual has a Y chromosome → **SRY present**.
2. SRY triggers testis development → male phenotype.
3. **However:** Two X chromosomes are present → one X is inactivated (Barr body = 1).
4. The presence of two X chromosomes disrupts normal spermatogenesis → **infertility**.
5. Klinefelter syndrome: tall stature, gynecomastia, small testes, azoospermia.

Compare with **Drosophila XXY**: this is a **fertile female** because in *Drosophila*, the Y is not sex-determining and the X:A ratio = 1.0 (female) is preserved.
