<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between paracentric and pericentric inversions.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Paracentric Inversion | Pericentric Inversion |
|---------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Centromere involvement | **Excluded** from inverted segment | **Included** in inverted segment |
| Location of breaks | Both in the same chromosome arm | One break in each arm |
| Meiotic loop | Spans only one arm | Spans the centromere |
| Recombinant outcome | **Dicentric + acentric** chromatids | **Duplication-deletion** chromatids |
| ISCN example | `inv(2)(p11p21)` | `inv(3)(p25q21)` |

*(3 marks)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the Philadelphia chromosome? Mention its clinical significance.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Philadelphia chromosome**, `der(22)t(9;22)(q34;q11)`, is a derivative chromosome resulting from a **reciprocal translocation** between chromosomes **9** and **22**, producing the **BCR-ABL1 fusion gene**.

**Clinical Significance:**
- Hallmark cytogenetic abnormality of **Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML)**.
- Also seen in 25% of adult **Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL)**.
- The BCR-ABL1 fusion encodes a **constitutively active tyrosine kinase**.
- Treatable with **Imatinib (Gleevec)**, a targeted tyrosine kinase inhibitor — first example of precision medicine in oncology.

*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]`

**(a)** Explain the **Breakage-Reunion Model** of chromosome structural rearrangements. Discuss the role of double-strand breaks (DSBs) and non-homologous end joining (NHEJ) with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Classify **translocations** in detail. With a neat diagram, explain the meiotic behavior of a **reciprocal translocation heterozygote** and the genetic consequences of alternate vs adjacent-1 segregation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1 — Define the Model:**
The Breakage-Reunion Model (Stadler, 1930s; refined by Revell, 1959) proposes that structural abnormalities originate from **chromosome breakage** followed by **rejoining in new combinations**.

**Step 2 — DSB Induction:**
- Caused by **ionizing radiation**, **alkylating agents** (e.g., EMS), **viral integration**, and **replication stress**.
- A single DSB → terminal deletion (after exonuclease activity).
- Two DSBs on the same chromosome → interstitial deletion, inversion, or ring.
- DSBs on two different chromosomes → translocation.

**Step 3 — Repair Pathways:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NHEJ (error-prone)} &\rightarrow \text{joins non-homologous ends} \rightarrow \text{structural variants} \\
\text{HRR (homology-directed)} &\rightarrow \text{NAHR via low-copy repeats} \rightarrow \text{recurrent rearrangements}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Outcomes:**

$$
\text{DSB Repair Outcome} = f(\text{number of breaks}, \text{chromosomes involved}, \text{repair template})
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **"DSB"** with **"SSB"** (single-strand break). Only **double-strand breaks** can lead to chromosome-level aberrations. SSBs are repaired by base-excision pathways and do NOT cause structural chromosome abnormalities.

*(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1 — Classification of Translocations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Translocations} &= \begin{cases}
\text{Reciprocal (bidirectional swap)} \\
\text{Robertsonian (acrocentric fusion)} \\
\text{Non-reciprocal (one-way transfer)} \\
\text{Insertional (three-break event)}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Reciprocal Translocation Carrier:**
A carrier has the karyotype `46,XX,t(9;22)(q34;q11)`. At meiosis I, the four involved chromosomes form a **quadrivalent** (cross-shaped pairing).

**Step 3 — Segregation Patterns:**

| Pattern | Description | Gamete Viability |
|---------|-------------|------------------|
| **Alternate** | Alternate centromeres to opposite poles | Balanced (normal or carrier) — viable |
| **Adjacent-1** | Homologous centromeres to same pole | Unbalanced — usually lethal |
| **Adjacent-2** | Non-homologous centromeres to same pole | Unbalanced — rare, often lethal |
| **3:1** | Three chromosomes to one pole | Highly unbalanced — lethal |

**Step 4 — Reproductive Risk:**

$$
P(\text{unbalanced offspring}) = \frac{2}{3} \approx 66.7\% \text{ theoretical} \quad (\text{actual} \approx 10\text{-}15\% \text{ due to selection})
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently describe *adjacent-2* segregation as common — it is actually **rare** due to geometric constraints in the quadrivalent. Also, remember the **adjacent-1 produces duplication of one segment AND deletion of another** in the same gamete (compound imbalance).

*(7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**(a)** Describe the different types of **chromosome deletions**. Explain the mechanisms of origin and the clinical significance of **Cri-du-chat syndrome** and **Williams syndrome**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **ring chromosomes** and **isochromosomes** with respect to their formation, structural consequences, and clinical examples. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1 — Types of Deletions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Deletions} &= \begin{cases}
\text{Terminal: 1 break, distal fragment lost} \\
\text{Interstitial: 2 breaks, mid-piece excised} \\
\text{Cryptic: sub-band, detected only by CMA}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Cri-du-chat Syndrome (5p minus):**
- **Karyotype:** `46,XX,del(5)(p15.1)` or `46,XX,del(5)(p15.3)`
- **Mechanism:** Terminal deletion of short arm of chromosome 5.
- **Clinical Features:**
  - High-pitched cat-like cry in infancy → "Cri-du-chat" = "cry of the cat" in French.
  - Severe intellectual disability, microcephaly, hypotonia.
  - Round face, hypertelorism.
- **Critical Region:** 5p15.2-5p15.3 (contains *CTNND2* gene).

**Step 3 — Williams Syndrome (7q11.23 deletion):**
- **Karyotype:** `46,XX,del(7)(q11.23)`
- **Mechanism:** Microdeletion (~1.5 Mb) mediated by **NAHR** between flanking low-copy repeats.
- **Clinical Features:**
  - "Elfin facies", supravalvular aortic stenosis.
  - Hypersocial personality, exceptional verbal skills.
  - Hypercalcemia in infancy.
- **Deleted Genes:** *ELN* (elastin) → cardiovascular defects; *LIMK1* → cognitive profile.

*(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1 — Ring Chromosome Formation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Process:} \quad &\text{Two terminal DSBs} \rightarrow \text{Sticky ends fuse} \rightarrow \text{Circular chromosome} \\
&\text{Subtelomeric DNA lost} \\
\text{Consequence:} \quad &\text{Deletion of subtelomeric genes} + \text{Instability in mitosis}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Clinical Example: r(20) Syndrome:**
- Mosaic ring chromosome 20.
- Drug-resistant epilepsy, mild-to-moderate intellectual disability.
- Detected by **karyotype + FISH** confirmation.

**Step 3 — Isochromosome Formation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Normal centromere split:} &\rightarrow \text{Vertical division} \rightarrow \text{Two p+q chromatids} \\
\text{Misdivision:} &\rightarrow \text{Horizontal division} \rightarrow \text{Two p+p OR q+q chromatids}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Clinical Example: i(Xq):**
- Common in **Turner syndrome mosaics** (~15%).
- Karyotype: `45,X/46,X,i(Xq)` — loss of Xp, gain of extra Xq.
- Results in **short stature** (SHOX gene deletion) and **gonadal dysgenesis**.

**Step 5 — Ring vs Isochromosome Comparison:**

| Feature | Ring | Isochromosome |
|---------|------|---------------|
| Number of centromeres | 1 | 1 (but symmetric) |
| Genetic content | Loss of subtelomeres | Loss of one arm, duplication of other |
| Stability | Unstable in mitosis | Stable |
| ISCN symbol | `r` | `i` |

*(7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Structural Chromosome Abnormalities**

### ✅ Definitions to Memorize
- **Deletion:** Loss of a chromosome segment.
- **Duplication:** Repetition of a chromosome segment.
- **Inversion:** 180° reversal of a segment (no net loss).
- **Reciprocal Translocation:** Bidirectional exchange between non-homologous chromosomes.
- **Robertsonian Translocation:** Fusion of two acrocentric chromosomes at the centromere.
- **Ring Chromosome:** Circular chromosome from fusion of two broken ends.
- **Isochromosome:** Mirror-image chromosome with duplicated arms.

### ✅ ISCN Notation Rules
- Lower band number comes **first**.
- `del`, `dup`, `inv`, `t`, `rob`, `r`, `i` — always **lowercase**.
- Sex chromosomes written **before** abnormalities: `46,XX,del(5)(p13)`.

### ✅ Balanced vs Unbalanced — Quick Test
- **Balanced:** Same total DNA content (inversion, reciprocal translocation, Robertsonian).
- **Unbalanced:** Net DNA loss or gain (deletion, duplication, ring, isochromosome).

### ✅ High-Yield Clinical Correlations
- **Philadelphia chromosome** → CML
- **Cri-du-chat** → 5p deletion
- **Williams syndrome** → 7q11.23 deletion
- **Turner mosaic** → i(Xq)
- **Down syndrome (familial)** → Robertsonian rob(14;21)
- **EML4-ALK fusion** → Non-small cell lung cancer (inv(2)(p21p23))

### ✅ Common Examiner Traps
1. Confusing *SSB* vs *DSB* — only **DSBs** cause chromosome aberrations.
2. Stating **adjacent-2 segregation is common** — it is **rare**.
3. Forgetting that **Robertsonian translocation carriers have 45 chromosomes**, not 46.
4. Mis-writing ISCN — remember the band numbers are in **ascending order** within the parentheses.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chromosome inactivation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBB861 - CYTOGENETICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Chromosome inactivation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Chromosome inactivation** is the genetically programmed, heritable transcriptional silencing of an entire chromosome (or a large chromosomal domain) that occurs as a dosage-compensation mechanism or as a parent-of-origin regulatory process. In the context of cytogenetics and the KTU 2024 PEBB861 syllabus, the term principally refers to:

1. **X-Chromosome Inactivation (XCI / Lyonization):** The random, irreversible, and clonally heritable silencing of one of the two X chromosomes in the somatic cells of female placental mammals (XX), thereby equating X-linked gene dosage with that of males (XY).
2. **Genomic Imprinting:** The parent-of-origin specific silencing of certain autosomal chromosomes or chromosomal regions.

The inactive chromosome is cytologically observable as a **Barr body** (sex chromatin), a heterochromatic mass typically located at the nuclear periphery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 1**
> Chromosome inactivation is a cornerstone of mammalian dosage compensation. The canonical model is **Mary F. Lyon's hypothesis (1961)**, which remains the framework for evaluating X-linked disorders, mosaicism, and epigenetic regulation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Volume Knob on a Stereo":**
> Imagine a male cell as a stereo with **one speaker (the single X chromosome)** playing at volume 5. A female cell, having **two speakers (two X chromosomes)**, would naturally play at volume 10 — twice as loud — causing "overexpression." Nature's solution is **NOT to unplug one speaker entirely**, but to attach a **mute button (the XIST gene)** to exactly one of the two X chromosomes in each cell. The cell *chooses* one X to silence at random early in embryonic life, then *locks* that choice, and every daughter cell inherits the same mute state. The mute is **epigenetic** — the chromosome is still physically present, but its genes are switched off.
>
> The mute is enforced by chemical modifications: **DNA methylation**, **histone deacetylation**, and incorporation of the macro-histone variant **macroH2A**.

### 1.3 Key Physical / Quantitative Parameters

- **Number of Barr bodies per cell** = Number of X chromosomes − 1
  (e.g., normal female XX → **1 Barr body**; normal male XY → **0 Barr bodies**; Turner XO → **0 Barr bodies**; Klinefelter XXY → **1 Barr body**; Triple-X XXX → **2 Barr bodies**)
- **Timing of X-inactivation in humans:** Begins at the **late blastocyst stage (~Day 5–6 post-fertilization)**, specifically around the time of implantation, in the **epiblast lineage**.
- **XIST RNA length:** ~**17 kb** (kb = kilobases), a long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) that coats the chromosome *in cis*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Dosage Compensation Rule of Thumb (KTU Memorize):**
> $$B = N_X - 1$$
> where $B$ = number of Barr bodies, and $N_X$ = total number of X chromosomes per diploid somatic cell.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Lyon's Hypothesis — The Three Pillars

Mary Lyon proposed **three experimentally verifiable tenets** that govern X-inactivation. Each is a high-yield KTU point.

1. **Randomness:** Either the maternally inherited X (X$^m$) or the paternally inherited X (X$^p$) may be inactivated in any given cell of the embryonic lineage.
2. **Clonal Heritability (Stability):** Once a particular X is inactivated in a progenitor cell, that *same* X remains inactive in all descendant cells. This produces **functional mosaicism** in adult females.
3. **Initiation in the Early Embryo:** Inactivation begins in a defined developmental window and is then stably maintained through subsequent somatic mitoses.

### 2.2 Mechanism of X-Inactivation — Stepwise Logic

1. **Counting:** The cell tallies the number of X chromosomes and the **X:Autosome ratio**. Only one X remains active per diploid (2n) genome.
2. **Choice:** A single *X-inactivation centre* (**Xic**, ~**1 Mb** region on Xq13) on each X "competes." The future-active X *escapes* silencing; the future-inactive X initiates the cascade.
3. **Initiation:** The **XIST gene** (X-inactive specific transcript) on the future-inactive X is upregulated and its RNA spreads *in cis*, coating the entire chromosome.
4. **Spermatogenesis / Oogenesis Reset:** In the germ line, the inactive state is erased so that every gamete transmits a single, *active* X.
5. **Propagation / Maintenance:** Recruitment of **Polycomb Repressive Complex 2 (PRC2)**, deposition of **H3K27me3**, **H3K9me2/3**, **H2AK119ub**, incorporation of **macroH2A**, **DNA methylation** at CpG islands, and **histone deacetylation** lock in the heterochromatic state.
6. **Escape Genes:** Approximately **15%** of human X-linked genes (e.g., *SMCX/ JARID1C*, *STS*, *RPS4X*) escape XCI and remain biallelically expressed — clinically critical.

### 2.3 Types of X-Inactivation

| Type | Occurs In | Pattern | KTU Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Random XCI** | Placental mammals (eutherians) — humans, mice | 50:50 X$^m$ vs X$^p$ | Standard Lyon model |
| **Imprinted XCI** | Extraembryonic tissues (trophoblast, primitive endoderm) in mouse | Always X$^p$ (paternal) silenced | Not prominent in humans |
| **Skewed XCI** | Adult females, often age-related or XIST-mutation driven | >80% one X active | Influences severity of X-linked disease |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Expression / Value | Units | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| Barr body count | $B = N_X - 1$ | dimensionless | Valid for $N_X \geq 1$ |
| Active X per 2n cell | $1$ | chromosome | Universally true for normal mammals |
| XIST RNA size | $\approx 17{,}000$ | bases (nt) | Long non-coding RNA |
| Xic locus size | $\approx 1 \times 10^6$ | base pairs (Mb) | Xq13.2 region |
| Escape gene fraction (human X) | $\approx 0.15$ | fraction (dimensionless) | ~15% of X-linked genes |
| Initiation time (human) | $\approx$ Day 5–6 | days post-fertilization | Late blastocyst |
| Heterochromatin marks | H3K27me3, H3K9me2, H2AK119ub, 5-mC, macroH2A | — | Synergistic silencing |
| Heterochromatin spread speed | $\approx 0.002$ to $0.02$ | µm/hour (varies by cell type) | Coatings proceeds from Xic |
| Allele expression in carrier female | $\approx 50\%$ mutant : $\approx 50\%$ normal | ratio | Modified by skewing |
| Required minimum proteins in complex | 3 (e.g., PRC2 = EZH2 + EED + SUZ12 core) | count | KTU high-yield |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering / Biomedical Utility:**
> The principle of targeted chromosome inactivation underpins modern **X-linked disease therapeutics** (e.g., reactivation of the inactive X in *Rett syndrome*/*MECP2* mutants), **reprogramming in iPSC generation** (XCI status is a quality marker), and **XCI-skewing diagnostics** in carrier females of hemophilia A/B, Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD), and Fragile X syndrome.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation 1 — Barr Body Number from Karyotype

The general rule for the number of Barr bodies per somatic interphase nucleus is derived from counting logic:

$$
\text{Let } N_X = \text{Total X chromosomes per diploid cell}
$$
$$
\text{Let } N_A = \text{Active X chromosomes per diploid cell (always } = 1\text{)}
$$
$$
\text{Then } B = N_X - N_A = N_X - 1
$$

$$
\therefore B = N_X - 1
$$

This is a **direct logical subtraction** rather than a complex derivation. It is, however, the most heavily tested KTU relationship in Module 1.

**Numerical Examples (KTU standard style):**

**Example 1 — Turner Syndrome (XO):**
$$
N_X = 1 \implies B = 1 - 1 = 0
$$

A 45,X female has **zero** Barr bodies.

**Example 2 — Klinefelter Syndrome (XXY):**
$$
N_X = 2 \implies B = 2 - 1 = 1
$$

A 47,XXY male has **one** Barr body — clinically useful for buccal smear screening.

**Example 3 — Triple X Syndrome (XXX):**
$$
N_X = 3 \implies B = 3 - 1 = 2
$$

A 47,XXX female has **two** Barr bodies.

**Example 4 — Tetrasomy X (XXXX):**
$$
N_X = 4 \implies B = 4 - 1 = 3
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Derivation 2 — Dosage Compensation Quantification

In *Drosophila* and *C. elegans*, dosage compensation is achieved by *upregulating* the single X (2-fold in *Drosophila*, 0.5-fold dampening in *C. elegans*). In mammals, we quantify gene-product equivalence:

Let $E_X^F$ = total X-linked gene expression in female (XX), and $E_X^M$ = expression in male (XY).

For an X-linked gene of product per active X $= p$:

$$
E_X^F = 2 \cdot (1 - f_{esc}) \cdot p + 2 \cdot f_{esc} \cdot p = 2p \quad \text{(without XCI)}
$$

where $f_{esc}$ = fraction of escaping genes.

With **complete XCI** (escape fraction $f_{esc} = 0$):

$$
E_X^F^{XCI} = 1 \cdot (1 - 0) \cdot p + 1 \cdot 0 \cdot p = p
$$

For male:

$$
E_X^M = 1 \cdot p = p
$$

Therefore:

$$
\therefore E_X^F^{XCI} = E_X^M = p
$$

**Equivalence achieved.** This is the *quantitative essence* of dosage compensation.

### 3.3 Analytical Derivation 3 — Effect of Skewing on Carrier Phenotype

For a heterozygous female carrier of an X-linked recessive allele (genotype $X^N X^m$), let $s$ be the *skewing fraction* (proportion of cells in which the normal allele $X^N$ is on the active X). Then:

$$
\text{Fraction of cells expressing normal allele} = s
$$
$$
\text{Fraction of cells expressing mutant allele} = (1 - s)
$$

When $s = 0.5$ (random XCI, equal inactivation), the carrier is phenotypically normal on average because ~50% of cells express enough functional protein via mosaicism (lyonization).

If skewing is severe, e.g., $s = 0.95$:

$$
\text{Mutant-expressing cells} = 1 - s = 0.05 = 5\%
$$

In this scenario the carrier is phenotypically near-normal (mildly affected).

Conversely, $s = 0.05$ implies **95%** of cells express the mutant allele → the carrier may manifest a **partial disease phenotype**.

$$
\boxed{\text{Phenotype severity} \propto (1 - s)}
$$

where $s$ is the fraction of cells with the wild-type X active.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical: A Carrier of Hemophilia A (F8 mutation)

A woman is heterozygous for an $F8$ frameshift mutation. Buccal-cell X-inactivation studies report $s = 0.30$. Estimate her symptomatic risk.

$$
\text{Cells expressing mutant } F8 = 1 - s = 1 - 0.30 = 0.70 \;(70\%)
$$

A typical coagulation factor threshold for hemophilia A is ~**40%** Factor VIII activity.

Effective activity from mosaicism:

$$
A_{eff} = s \times A_{normal} = 0.30 \times 100\% = 30\%
$$

Since $30\% < 40\%$ threshold, she is predicted to be a **manifesting carrier / mildly affected**, consistent with skewed X-inactivation in the XCI assay.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Mechanism of X-Inactivation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Fertilized Zygote XX"] --> B["Cleavage Divisions 2 to 8 cell stage"]
    B --> C["Blastocyst Formation Day 5"]
    C --> D["Counting X to Autosome Ratio"]
    D --> E{"Single X Active Decision"}
    E -- "Future Inactive X" --> F["XIST Gene Upregulation 17 kb lncRNA"]
    E -- "Future Active X" --> G["XIST Silenced RNF12 Activated"]
    F --> H["XIST RNA Coats Chromosome in cis"]
    H --> I["Recruitment of PRC2 Complex"]
    I --> J["Histone Marks H3K27me3 H3K9me2 H2AK119ub"]
    J --> K["DNA Methylation of CpG Islands"]
    K --> L["MacroH2A Incorporation"]
    L --> M["Heterochromatic Barr Body Visible at Nuclear Periphery"]
    M --> N["Stable Inheritance in All Daughter Cells"]
    N --> O["Functional Mosaicism in Adult Female"]
    G --> P["Active X Continues Transcription"]
    P --> O
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Comparison of Dosage Compensation Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Mammals
        M1["Female XX Two X"] --> M2["Random XCI"]
        M2 --> M3["One Inactive Barr Body"]
        M3 --> M4["Single Active X Equal to Male"]
        M5["Male XY One X"] --> M4
    end

    subgraph Drosophila
        D1["Female XX Two X"] --> D2["Each X Expressed at 50 percent"]
        D1 --> D3["Male XY One X"] --> D4["Single X Upregulated 2 fold via MSL Complex"]
        D2 --> D4
        D4 --> D5["Equal X linked Output"]
    end

    subgraph Celegans
        C1["Hermaphrodite XX Two X"] --> C2["Each X Damped by 50 percent"]
        C2 --> C3["Output Equal to Male XO"]
        C4["Male XO Single X"] --> C3
    end
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Layered Silencing of the Inactive X

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1["Layer 1 XIST lncRNA Coating"] --> L2["Layer 2 PRC2 and H3K27me3"]
    L2 --> L3["Layer 3 PRC1 and H2AK119ub"]
    L3 --> L4["Layer 4 Histone Deacetylation HDACs"]
    L4 --> L5["Layer 5 H3K9 Methylation HP1 Recruitment"]
    L5 --> L6["Layer 6 CpG Methylation 5 methyl C"]
    L6 --> L7["Layer 7 macroH2A Replacement"]
    L7 --> L8["Stable Heterochromatin Barr Body"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
*State Lyon's hypothesis of X-chromosome inactivation. List its three fundamental tenets.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Lyon's hypothesis (1961) explains dosage compensation in mammalian females by proposing that one of the two X chromosomes in each somatic cell of a female is inactivated early in embryogenesis.
The **three tenets** are:
1. **Randomness** – Either the maternal or paternal X is inactivated in any given cell.
2. **Clonal heritability** – The same X remains inactive in all progeny of that cell.
3. **Embryonic initiation** – The process begins at the early blastocyst stage and is stably maintained. *(3 × 1 mark)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*A buccal smear from a phenotypically male patient reveals one Barr body per cell. Predict the most likely karyotype and explain the molecular basis of the Barr body.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Karyotype: **47,XXY — Klinefelter syndrome.** Barr body number is given by $B = N_X - 1 = 2 - 1 = 1$. The Barr body is a **heterochromatinised inactive X chromosome**, silenced by XIST lncRNA coating, H3K27 trimethylation, CpG methylation, and macroH2A incorporation. *(1 mark karyotype + 1 mark formula + 1 mark molecular basis)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

**Internal Choice — Answer ANY ONE**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024, Retest Module 1]*

**(a)** Describe the molecular mechanism of X-chromosome inactivation with emphasis on the role of **XIST** and the epigenetic modifications that maintain the silent state. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how **skewed X-inactivation** influences the clinical phenotype in female carriers of X-linked recessive disorders. Support your answer with two named clinical examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Molecular mechanism (7 marks):**
1. **Counting & choice** – The cell assesses the X:autosome ratio through *RNF12* (RLIM) and *XACT* lncRNA, ensuring a single active X per diploid genome. *(1 mark)*
2. **XIST upregulation** – On the future-inactive X, the *XIST* gene produces a **17 kb lncRNA** that spreads *in cis* along the chromosome, coating it from the *Xic* (Xq13.2). *(1 mark)*
3. **PRC2 recruitment** – XIST recruits Polycomb Repressive Complex 2, whose catalytic subunit **EZH2** deposits **H3K27me3**. *(1 mark)*
4. **Histone deacetylation** – HDAC3 and the *SMRT/NCoR* complex remove activating H3K9Ac marks. *(1 mark)*
5. **H3K9 methylation & HP1** – H3K9me2/3 recruits **HP1** proteins, reinforcing heterochromatin. *(1 mark)*
6. **DNA methylation** – *DNMT3B* methylates CpG islands at X-linked promoters. *(1 mark)*
7. **macroH2A & late maintenance** – Histone variant **macroH2A1** replaces H2A in the Barr body, completing the silenced state. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Skewed XCI and X-linked disorders (7 marks):**

When X-inactivation is non-random (skewed), the proportion of cells expressing the mutant allele deviates from 50%, altering clinical severity. *(1 mark)*

$$
\text{Mutant-expressing cells} = 1 - s
$$

where $s$ = fraction of cells with the wild-type X active.

**Clinical Example 1 — Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy (DMD):** *(3 marks)*
Females heterozygous for *DMD* gene mutations are typically asymptomatic carriers (s ~ 0.5). However, with skewed XCI ($s < 0.3$), manifesting carriers develop muscle weakness, elevated creatine kinase, and dystrophic changes on biopsy.

**Clinical Example 2 — Hemophilia A (F8 mutation):** *(3 marks)*
Coagulation requires ~40% Factor VIII activity. With random XCI, $A_{eff} = 0.5 \times 100\% = 50\%$ → asymptomatic. With severe skewing ($s = 0.30$), $A_{eff} = 30\%$ → **mild-to-moderate hemophilia**, the classic "manifesting carrier."

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Main Sessional]*

**(a)** Compare and contrast the dosage compensation strategies in **mammals**, **Drosophila**, and **Caenorhabditis elegans**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A phenotypic female presents with primary amenorrhea and short stature. Karyotyping reveals **45,X**. Calculate the expected number of Barr bodies and explain the molecular consequences of monosomy X with respect to X-linked gene expression and escape from inactivation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparative dosage compensation (7 marks):**

| Organism | Genetic Strategy | Mechanism | Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mammals (XX♀ vs XY♂)** | One X silenced in ♀ | XIST-mediated heterochromatinization | $E_X^F = E_X^M = p$ |
| **Drosophila (XX♀ vs XY♂)** | Single X upregulated in ♂ | **MSL complex** acetylates H4K16; 2-fold upregulation | $E_X^F = E_X^M = 2p$ |
| **C. elegans (XX hermaphrodite vs XO ♂)** | Both X damped in ♀ | **DCC** (dosage compensation complex) halves transcription per X | $E_X^{herm} = E_X^{male} = p$ |

*(1 mark per organism comparison × 3, plus 1 mark for the unifying principle, plus 3 marks for clarity/detail of mechanisms)*

**(b) Turner Syndrome – Barr body count and expression (7 marks):**

Barr body calculation:

$$
B = N_X - 1 = 1 - 1 = 0 \quad \textbf{(Zero Barr bodies)} \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

**Molecular consequences:**
1. **Haploinsufficiency** – Genes that normally escape XCI (~15% of the human X) are now present in only **one functional copy**, leading to a phenotypic dose deficit. *(2 marks)*
2. **Key escape genes implicated** – *SHOX* (short stature homeobox) on the pseudoautosomal region; its haploinsufficiency explains the **short stature** seen in 45,X. *(1 mark)*
3. **Other clinical correlates** – *USP9X*, *UTX/KDM6A*, *DDX3X*, and *TIMM8A* (Mohr–Tranebjærg syndrome) escape XCI; their dose reduction contributes to Turner features. *(1 mark)*
4. **No Barr body** – Confirms XCI is **not** simply a numerical compensation; the *single* X remains active and is **overworked** relative to the autosomes. *(1 mark)*
5. **Ovarian failure** – *XIST* dysregulation and premature oocyte attrition in 45,X explains primary amenorrhea. *(1 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the escape fraction:** Many students assume XCI silences 100% of the inactive X. The correct figure is ~**85%** silenced; ~**15%** escape. Lose **2 marks** for this omission in 14-mark answers.
> 2. **Confusing imprinting with random XCI:** Imprinted XCI occurs in *extraembryonic* tissues only; do not attribute it to whole-body inactivation. Lose **1 mark**.
> 3. **Off-by-one in Barr body formula:** Some students write $B = N_X + 1$. Always verify with karyotype 46,XX → 1 Barr body.
> 4. **Skewing vs mosaicism:** Skewing is *quantitative deviation* from random XCI; mosaicism is the *normal* functional consequence of random XCI. Do not interchange.
> 5. **MacroH2A ≠ H2A.X:** macroH2A is a unique replacement histone variant specific to the inactive X; do not confuse with H2A.X phosphorylation.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Chromosome inactivation** = heritable transcriptional silencing of an entire chromosome; the prototypical example is **X-chromosome inactivation (XCI)** in female mammals.
- **Lyon's hypothesis (1961)** rests on three pillars: randomness, clonal heritability, embryonic initiation.
- **Barr body count** $B = N_X - 1$ — universal rule for somatic interphase nuclei.
- **XIST** is a **17 kb lncRNA**, *cis*-acting, coats the future-inactive X from the **Xic** locus at Xq13.2.
- **Epigenetic silencing layers:** XIST coating → PRC2/H3K27me3 → PRC1/H2AK119ub → H3K9me2/3 + HP1 → CpG methylation → macroH2A.
- **~15% of human X-linked genes escape XCI**; the **SHOX** gene in the pseudoautosomal region is a clinically critical example.
- **Skewed XCI** modifies carrier phenotype in X-linked recessive disorders (DMD, hemophilia A/B, Fragile X).
- **Timing:** XCI initiates at the **late blastocyst / early post-implantation** stage (~Day 5–6 in humans) in the epiblast.
- **Imprinted XCI:** In mouse, the *paternal X* is preferentially silenced in extraembryonic lineages.
- **Comparison of strategies:**
  - **Mammals:** one X silenced (XIST).
  - **Drosophila:** single X upregulated 2-fold (MSL complex).
  - **C. elegans:** both X damped by 50% (DCC complex).
- **Klinefelter (47,XXY)** has 1 Barr body; **Triple-X (47,XXX)** has 2; **Turner (45,X)** has 0.
- **Germ-line reversal:** The inactive X is **reactivated** in the female germ line before meiosis, ensuring every oocyte transmits a single *active* X.
- **Clinical pearls:** Manifesting carriers, XCI skewing in aged females, X-reactivation in iPSCs, Rett syndrome therapeutics via Xi reactivation.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line Board Exam Punchline (memorize verbatim):**
> *"In female mammalian somatic cells, one X chromosome is randomly and stably inactivated early in embryogenesis through XIST-mediated heterochromatinization, forming a Barr body, thereby achieving dosage compensation between XX females and XY males — as proposed by Mary Lyon in 1961."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variations in chromosome number and structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBB861 - CYTOGENETICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Variations in chromosome number and structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Chromosomal Variation – The KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chromosomal Variation (Cytogenetic Aberration):** Any change in the normal number (numerical aberration) or the normal architecture (structural aberration) of chromosomes in a cell, which may arise spontaneously or be induced by physical/chemical/biological agents. Such variations alter the gene balance of the genome and are the primary cytogenetic basis of syndromes, speciation, and crop improvement.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, chromosomal variations are broadly classified into two super-categories:

| Super-category | Sub-types (KTU syllabus terminology) |
|---|---|
| **Numerical Variations** | Euploidy (Haploidy, Polyploidy – Autopolyploidy, Allopolyploidy) and Aneuploidy (Monosomy, Nullisomy, Trisomy, Tetrasomy, Double Trisomy) |
| **Structural Variations** | Deletion, Duplication, Inversion (Paracentric, Pericentric), Translocation (Reciprocal, Robertsonian), Insertion, Ring chromosome, Isochromosome |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Book Library" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine your cell's genome as a **library of 46 instruction manuals** (chromosomes), each containing **chapters** (genes). Now imagine the librarian gets careless:
> - **Numerical changes** = Either the librarian **loses an entire manual** (monosomy), **copies one extra** (trisomy), or **doubles the entire collection** (polyploidy).
> - **Structural changes** = A single manual has **pages torn out** (deletion), **pages photocopied and pasted twice** (duplication), a **chapter's pages reversed and re-bound** (inversion), or a **chapter from one manual ends up in another** (translocation).
>
> Every alteration changes the "reading frame" of life — sometimes harmless, sometimes catastrophic (e.g., Down syndrome), sometimes beneficial (e.g., wheat evolution).

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics in Cytogenetics

- **Human diploid number (2n):** **46** chromosomes
- **Human haploid number (n):** **23** chromosomes
- **Down syndrome critical chromosome:** **Chromosome 21 (trisomy)**
- **Klinefelter's syndrome karyotype:** **47, XXY**
- **Turner's syndrome karyotype:** **45, X**
- **Robertsonian fusion chromosomes:** **13, 14, 15, 21, 22 (acrocentric group)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Numerical aberrations are most often the result of **non-disjunction** during meiosis I or meiosis II, while structural aberrations usually follow **chromosome breakage** followed by **abnormal reunion** of broken ends during the G₁ or S phase of the cell cycle.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Variations in Chromosome NUMBER

### 2.1.1 Euploidy — Multiples of the Complete Set
Euploidy refers to cells whose chromosome number is an exact multiple of the basic set (x) or the gametic number (n).

**Step-by-step logic (Why & How):**
1. **Haploidy (n or x):** Cells contain a single complete set of chromosomes. Arises from parthenogenesis (e.g., drone bees). In plants, haploids are smaller, sterile, and used in producing homozygous lines.
2. **Diploidy (2n):** The normal somatic state in most animals and many plants.
3. **Polyploidy (>2n):** Three or more complete sets. Very rare in animals, but extremely common and agriculturally significant in plants (e.g., wheat, cotton, banana, coffee).

**Sub-classification of Polyploidy:**

- **Autopolyploidy:** Multiple chromosome sets originate from the **same species** (e.g., AAA in banana). Caused by failure of cytokinesis after DNA replication. Offspring are usually sterile due to unequal pairing at meiosis (trivalents, quadrivalents).
- **Allopolyploidy:** Multiple sets come from **hybridisation of two or more distinct species** followed by chromosome doubling. The classic KTU example is **Triticale** (Triticum × Secale) and bread wheat (Triticum aestivum, 6n = 42).

### 2.1.2 Aneuploidy — Deviation from Exact Multiples
Aneuploidy occurs when the chromosome number is **not an exact multiple of the basic set**. The most common cause is **non-disjunction** — the failure of homologous chromosomes (meiosis I) or sister chromatids (meiosis II) to separate.

| Type | Formula | KTU Clinical/Scientific Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Monosomy** | $2n - 1$ | Turner's syndrome (45, X) — single X in humans; usually lethal in autosomes |
| **Nullisomy** | $2n - 2$ | Lethal in most diploid organisms; observed in polyploid wheat aneuploids |
| **Trisomy** | $2n + 1$ | Down syndrome (47, XX or XY, +21); Patau (47, +13); Edwards (47, +18) |
| **Tetrasomy** | $2n + 2$ | 48, XXXX; rare, viable only for sex chromosomes |
| **Double Trisomy** | $2n + 1 + 1$ | 48, XXY, +21 (Down + Klinefelter co-occurrence) |

## 2.2 Variations in Chromosome STRUCTURE

Structural changes require **two breaks** in one or two chromosomes, followed by **reunion in an abnormal pattern**.

| Aberration | Mechanism | Cytogenetic Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **Deletion (deficiency)** | Loss of a chromosome segment; 2 breaks → segment lost | Loop formation in heterozygotes; pseudo-dominance; Cri-du-chat (5p−) |
| **Duplication** | Segment appears twice on the same chromosome | Bar eye in *Drosophila* (X-linked tandem duplication) |
| **Inversion** | Segment reversed 180°; 2 breaks → segment rotated | Inversion loop; paracentric → acentric/dicentric bridges + fragments |
| **Translocation** | Segment moves to a non-homologous chromosome | Reciprocal or Robertsonian; cross-shaped quadrivalent at pachytene |
| **Insertion** | Segment moves into a new locus on same or different chromosome | Requires 3 breakpoints |
| **Ring chromosome** | Two telomeric breaks → ends fuse to form a circle | Mitotic instability; ring syndrome phenotypes |
| **Isochromosome** | Centromere mis-divides along the transverse axis | Two arms are mirror images; common in X-chromosome of Turner mosaics |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Logic Sheet

| Parameter / Concept | Equation or Rule | Boundary Condition / Note |
|---|---|---|
| Euploid set | $E = k \cdot x$ where $k = 1, 2, 3, 4...$ | $x$ = monoploid (basic) set |
| Ploidy level (DNA content) | $C = n \cdot q$ | $q$ = DNA per monoploid genome |
| Aneuploid gamete viability | Lethal if $\Delta n \geq 2$ in autosomes | Exceptions: sex chromosomes, polyploids |
| Non-disjunction probability | Increases with maternal age | Trivalent vs bivalent segregation |
| Dosage compensation (humans) | One X inactivated (Barr body) per extra X | $\text{Bodies} = n_{X} - 1$ |
| Polyploid meiotic figure | Multivalent formation | $\text{Quadrivalent} \Leftrightarrow \text{Allotetraploid}$ |
| Breakage-Reunion hypothesis | Requires $\geq 2$ breaks for most aberrations | Single break → terminal deletion only |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter in engineering?** Cytogenetics underpins **molecular diagnostics** (karyotyping microarrays, FISH probes), **radiation biology** (chromosome aberration dosimetry — dicentric assay), **pharmacogenomics** (chromosomal drug targets), and **plant biotechnology** (polyploidy breeding for higher biomass yield).

## 2.4 Origin and Induction Mechanisms

**How do these variations arise?**

1. **Non-disjunction** during anaphase (meiotic or mitotic) → numerical aberrations.
2. **Lagging chromosome** at anaphase → aneuploid daughter cells (mosaicism).
3. **Chromosome breakage** induced by ionising radiation, alkylating agents, viral integration (e.g., *Agrobacterium* in plants) → structural aberrations.
4. **Unequal crossing-over** in meiosis → duplications and deletions (basis of Bar-eye in *Drosophila*).
5. **Centromere mis-division** → isochromosomes.
6. **Telomere fusion** → ring chromosomes and Robertsonian translocations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Quantitative Models & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations — From Genotype Dosage to Phenotype

### 3.1.1 Derivative 1 — Expected Aneuploid Frequencies from a Single Non-Disjunctional Event

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Chain (Why & How):** If non-disjunction occurs in **meiosis I** in one of 20 oocytes of a female, and fertilisation occurs with a normal sperm (n), what fraction of zygotes will be trisomic?

**Step 1:** A single primary oocyte normally produces 1 functional ovum (n) + 1 polar body (degenerate). After non-disjunction in Meiosis I, **both homologues enter the secondary oocyte**; after Meiosis II, the ovum carries **n + 1** chromosomes.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ovum genotype from non-disjunctive meiosis I} &= (n+1) \\
\text{Normal sperm genotype} &= n \\
\text{Zygote from fertilisation} &= (n+1) + n = 2n + 1 = \text{Trisomic}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** The complementary polar body carries **n − 1** chromosomes; upon fertilisation:

$$
(n - 1) + n = 2n - 1 = \text{Monosomic}
$$

**Step 3:** Therefore, **1/2 the affected zygotes are trisomic, 1/2 monosomic** from a single meiosis-I non-disjunction event.

**Text-row explanation:** A single primary oocyte produces a *bivalent* of four chromatids in Meiosis II; when homologues fail to separate, the segregation is reversed — what should have gone into the polar body stays in the ovum, and vice-versa.

---

### 3.1.2 Derivative 2 — Quantitative Behaviour of Polyploid Series

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Chain (Why & How):** Why are allotetraploids fertile while autotetraploids show reduced fertility?

**Step 1 — Autotetraploid (AAAA) pairing:**
Homologous chromosomes (4 of each type) may pair as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Quadrivalent (4 chromosomes)} &\rightarrow \text{random segregation} \\
P(\text{balanced gamete, } 2n) &= \frac{2}{3} \\
P(\text{unbalanced gamete)}} &= \frac{1}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Allotetraploid (AABB) pairing:**
Each genome pairs strictly with its homolog; only bivalents form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bivalent (AA)} + \text{Bivalent (BB)} &\rightarrow \text{100 \% balanced gametes} \\
\text{Fertility} &= 100 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Text-row explanation:** The fertility collapse in autopolyploids is mathematically explained by **random chromosome segregation** — with 4 homologues, there are $\binom{4}{2} = 6$ possible pairing patterns, but only 2 produce balanced $2n$ gametes. This gives the KTU-asked probability of $\frac{1}{3}$ sterility.

---

### 3.1.3 Derivative 3 — Dosage Compensation (Lyon Hypothesis)

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Chain (Why & How):** How does the cell avoid lethal X-chromosome dosage imbalance in XX (female) vs XY (male)?

**Step 1:** Female = XX, male = XY → X-linked gene expression would be 2:1 in females.

**Step 2:** According to **Mary Lyon's hypothesis** (1961), one X chromosome in each somatic cell of the female is **heterochromatinised** to form a **Barr body**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of Barr bodies} &= \text{Number of X chromosomes} - 1 \\
\text{Hence in 47, XXX} &\rightarrow 2 \text{ Barr bodies} \\
\text{Hence in 48, XXXY} &\rightarrow 3 \text{ Barr bodies} \\
\text{Hence in 45, X (Turner)} &\rightarrow 0 \text{ Barr bodies}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Text-row explanation:** The inactivated X is randomly chosen in each cell during early embryogenesis (around the 8-cell stage in humans). The choice becomes **clonal** — all daughter cells inactivate the same X, producing the **patchy (mosaic) phenotype** seen in calico cats.

---

### 3.1.4 Derivative 4 — Quantitative Aberration Dosimetry (Radiation Cytogenetics)

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Chain (Why & How):** How is the dicentric chromosome assay used as a radiation dosimeter?

**Step 1:** Ionising radiation induces **double-strand breaks (DSBs)**. Two broken chromosomes may rejoin to form a **dicentric** (two centromeres) plus an **acentric fragment**.

**Step 2:** Number of dicentrics per cell is linearly related to dose (low-dose range):

$$
Y = \alpha D + \beta D^{2}
$$

where:
- $Y$ = dicentric yield per cell
- $D$ = radiation dose (in Gray, **Gy**)
- $\alpha$ = linear coefficient (single-track events)
- $\beta$ = quadratic coefficient (two-track events, dominant at high dose)

**Step 3:** For **low-LET radiation** (X-rays, gamma), $\beta$ dominates; for **high-LET radiation** (alpha particles, neutrons), $\alpha$ dominates, making the curve linear.

**Text-row explanation:** This is the foundational principle used by the **IAEA cytogenetic biodosimetry laboratories** to estimate absorbed dose in radiation accident victims by simply scoring dicentrics in their peripheral blood lymphocytes.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Aberration Type to Origin & Consequence

| Aberration | Origin Event | Required Breaks | Diagnostic Cytogenetic Sign | KTU-Mentioned Syndrome |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Terminal Deletion | One break + telomere loss | 1 | Single terminal gap, acentric fragment | Cri-du-chat (5p−) |
| Interstitial Deletion | Two internal breaks | 2 | Deletion loop in heterozygote | Prader-Willi / Angelman (15q11) |
| Tandem Duplication | Unequal crossing-over | 0 (replication slippage) | Bar eye phenotype (*Drosophila*) | Charcot-Marie-Tooth 1A (PMP22) |
| Paracentric Inversion | Two breaks on one arm | 2 | Inversion loop, anaphase bridge | Usually silent |
| Pericentric Inversion | Two breaks spanning centromere | 2 | Inversion loop, changed arm ratio | Recurrent abortions |
| Reciprocal Translocation | Two breaks on two chromosomes | 2 (one per chromosome) | Cross-shaped quadrivalent | Burkitt lymphoma t(8;14) |
| Robertsonian Translocation | Fusion of two acrocentrics at centromere | 1 per chromosome | Single large metacentric | Familial Down syndrome |
| Ring Chromosome | Two telomeric breaks + fusion | 2 | Closed circle, ring syndrome | Ring 20 syndrome |
| Isochromosome | Centromere transverse mis-division | 1 (centromere mis-split) | Mirror-image arms | Isochromosome Xq (Turner mosaic) |

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Question:** A phenotypically normal couple produces one child with Down syndrome (47, +21). Karyotype analysis of the father shows a Robertsonian translocation involving chromosomes 14 and 21. What is the probability that their next child will be affected? Use the standard segregation analysis.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Father's karyotype: **45, XY, der(14;21)(q10;q10)** — has the translocation plus one normal 21.

**Step 2:** Six possible gametes from a Robertsonian heterozygote (adjacent, alternate, or 3:0 segregation). The viable gametes and outcomes with a normal mother are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Alternate segregation} &\rightarrow \text{normal OR balanced carrier (phenotypically normal)} \\
\text{Adjacent segregation} &\rightarrow \text{Trisomy 21 OR Monosomy 21 (lethal)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Empirical KTU/clinical data from repeated ascertainment:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Trisomy 21 child}) &= \frac{1}{3} \\
P(\text{Balanced carrier}) &= \frac{1}{3} \\
P(\text{Normal}) &= \frac{1}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (excluding monosomy 21 as lethal and balanced carriers as phenotypically normal):**

$$
P(\text{affected liveborn}) = \frac{P(\text{Trisomy 21})}{P(\text{Trisomy 21}) + P(\text{Normal}) + P(\text{Carrier})}
$$

$$
P(\text{affected liveborn}) = \frac{1/3}{1/3 + 1/3 + 1/3} = \frac{1}{3}
$$

**Answer:** **33.3% (≈ 1/3)** risk of an affected liveborn child — the empirical recurrence risk used in genetic counselling for Robertsonian translocation Down syndrome.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Master Taxonomy of Chromosomal Variations

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Chromosomal Variation]

```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Mechanism Flow for Non-Disjunction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Prophase I Homologous Pairing]
    S2[Metaphase I Bivalent Alignment]
    S3[Anaphase I Homologue Separation]

    S5[Normal Ovum plus Polar Body]
    S6[Non-disjunction at Meiosis I]
    S7[Non-disjunction at Meiosis II]
    S8[Trisomic Zygote 2n plus 1]
    S9[Monosomic Zygote 2n minus 1]
    S10[Lethal Outcome]
    S11[Viable Trisomy]
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5
    S3 -.-> S6
    S4 -.-> S7
    S6 --> S8
    S6 --> S9
    S7 --> S8
    S7 --> S9
    S8 --> S11
    S9 --> S10
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Structural Aberration Block Architecture

```mermaid

    CHR[Normal Chromosome with Centromere C and Arms P and Q]
    BRK[Two Chromosome Breaks]
    DEL[Deletion Result]

    RING[Ring Result]

    CHR --> BRK
    BRK --> DEL
    BRK --> DUP
    BRK --> INV
    BRK --> TRA
    BRK --> RING
    BRK --> ISO
    DEL --> C1
    DUP --> C2
    INV --> C3
    TRA --> C4
    RING --> C5
    ISO --> C6
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Aberration-Functional Impact Mapping

```mermaid

    AberrationInput[Chromosomal Variation Input]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define aneuploidy. Distinguish between monosomy and nullisomy with one clinical example each.**

**Model Answer:**
Aneuploidy is a numerical chromosomal variation in which the chromosome number deviates from the exact multiple of the basic (haploid) set, arising most commonly from non-disjunction during meiosis or mitosis. **Monosomy** is the loss of a single chromosome from the diploid set ($2n-1$), with the human clinical example being **Turner's syndrome (45, X)** where only one X chromosome is present. **Nullisomy** is the complete loss of a homologous chromosome pair ($2n-2$), which is lethal in diploid organisms but observed in aneuploid series of polyploid plants like wheat. Thus, monosomy affects one member of a pair, whereas nullisomy affects both. **(3 marks)**

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is a Robertsonian translocation? Mention its clinical significance.**

**Model Answer:**
A Robertsonian translocation is a structural chromosomal aberration in which two **acrocentric chromosomes** (in humans, 13, 14, 15, 21, 22) fuse at their centromeres, resulting in a single large **metacentric chromosome** and loss of the small p-arm material. Clinically, it is significant because carriers are phenotypically normal but produce gametes with unbalanced chromosome content. When a Robertsonian translocation involving chromosome 21 (typically der(14;21) or der(21;21)) segregates, it produces **familial Down syndrome**, with a recurrence risk of **10–15% in female carriers** and **2–5% in male carriers**, much higher than the sporadic risk. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** paracentric with pericentric inversions — examiners heavily penalise this. *Para* = same arm; *Peri* = across the centromere.
> 2. **Do NOT write** "polyploidy is a chromosomal disease." Polyploidy is a *variation* and is mostly beneficial in plants; only unbalanced aneuploidies cause severe disease in humans.
> 3. **Always specify** the segregation mechanism (alternate vs adjacent) when discussing translocation outcomes — generic answers lose 3–4 marks.
> 4. **For polyploidy**, always cite a real KTU-approved example (Triticale, Wheat, Banana, Coffee) — generic descriptions lose credit.
> 5. **Numerical derivations** (like 1/3 translocation recurrence risk) MUST show the segregation pattern — partial steps get zero.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) Classify chromosomal variations in detail. With neat diagrams, explain the different types of structural aberrations of chromosomes.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Classification:**

Chromosomal variations are classified into:

**1. Numerical Variations:**
- **Euploidy:** Exact multiples of the basic set — haploidy (n), diploidy (2n), polyploidy (3n, 4n, etc.).
- **Aneuploidy:** Non-exact multiples — monosomy (2n−1), nullisomy (2n−2), trisomy (2n+1), tetrasomy (2n+2), double trisomy.

**2. Structural Variations:**
- Deletion, Duplication, Inversion (paracentric, pericentric), Translocation (reciprocal, Robertsonian), Insertion, Ring chromosome, Isochromosome.

**Structural Aberrations (with diagrams):**

(i) **Deletion:** Loss of a chromosome segment. Two breaks occur and the middle piece is lost.
- *Terminal deletion*: One break near the telomere with loss of the terminal segment (e.g., Cri-du-chat — deletion of short arm of chromosome 5).
- *Interstitial deletion*: Two internal breaks with loss of the intervening segment.

(ii) **Duplication:** A segment is repeated; arises from unequal crossing-over.
- *Tandem duplication*: Repeated segment lies adjacent to the original.
- *Reverse tandem*: Repeated segment is in reverse orientation.
- Example: **Bar-eye in *Drosophila*** due to tandem duplication of 16A region on X chromosome.

(iii) **Inversion:** A segment is broken at two points and reinserted in reverse orientation.
- *Paracentric inversion*: Both breaks lie on the same arm; centromere not included.
- *Pericentric inversion*: Breaks lie on opposite sides of the centromere; arm ratio changes — used as a chromosomal marker in evolution.

(iv) **Translocation:** Transfer of a segment to a non-homologous chromosome.
- *Reciprocal translocation*: Two chromosomes exchange segments (e.g., Burkitt lymphoma t(8;14)).
- *Robertsonian translocation*: Fusion of two acrocentric chromosomes at the centromere (e.g., 14;21 — familial Down syndrome).

(v) **Ring Chromosome:** Breaks at both telomeres of a chromosome, followed by fusion to form a closed circle.

(vi) **Isochromosome:** Centromere divides transversely instead of longitudinally, producing two chromosomes with mirror-image arms (e.g., iXq in Turner syndrome mosaics).

**(b) What is non-disjunction? Explain how it leads to aneuploidy in humans with suitable examples.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (b) — Non-disjunction and Aneuploidy:**

**Definition:**
Non-disjunction is the failure of homologous chromosomes (in Meiosis I) or sister chromatids (in Meiosis II) to separate during cell division, producing gametes with an abnormal chromosome number.

**Mechanism:**
During normal meiosis, homologous chromosomes pair at prophase I, align as bivalents at metaphase I, and separate at anaphase I. Sister chromatids then separate at anaphase II. When this segregation fails, both members of a pair (or both chromatids) migrate to the same pole.

**Types and Outcomes:**

**1. Meiosis I non-disjunction:**
Both homologues enter the same secondary oocyte. After meiosis II, the ovum contains $(n+1)$ and the polar body contains $(n-1)$ chromosomes. Fertilisation produces:
- $(n+1) + n = 2n + 1$ → **Trisomy** (e.g., Trisomy 21 — Down syndrome).
- $(n-1) + n = 2n - 1$ → **Monosomy** (e.g., 45,X — Turner syndrome; usually lethal in autosomes).

**2. Meiosis II non-disjunction:**
Sister chromatids fail to separate; one ovum is $(n+1)$, another $(n-1)$, and two are normal $(n)$.

**3. Mitotic non-disjunction (post-zygotic):**
Produces **mosaicism** — e.g., 46,XY/47,XXY mosaicism in Klinefelter variants.

**Human Aneuploidy Examples:**

| Syndrome | Karyotype | Phenotype |
|---|---|---|
| Down | 47, XX, +21 | Short stature, flat facies, intellectual disability |
| Patau | 47, +13 | Cleft lip/palate, polydactyly, cardiac defects (lethal early) |
| Edwards | 47, +18 | Clenched fists, rocker-bottom feet (lethal early) |
| Turner | 45, X | Streak ovaries, short stature, webbed neck |
| Klinefelter | 47, XXY | Tall stature, small testes, gynaecomastia |
| Triple X | 47, XXX | Usually fertile, mild learning difficulties |

**Conclusion:** Non-disjunction is the principal cytogenetic event underlying aneuploidy, with advanced maternal age being the major risk factor due to weakened cohesin proteins holding homologues together.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) Explain in detail the different types of polyploidy. Discuss the significance of polyploidy in plant breeding.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Polyploidy:**

Polyploidy is the state of having three or more complete sets of chromosomes ($3n$, $4n$, $5n$, $6n$, etc.) in a cell. It is categorised as:

**1. Autopolyploidy:**
All chromosome sets come from the **same species**. Mechanism: failure of cytokinesis after DNA replication, or production of unreduced gametes.
- Examples: Triploid banana (AAA, 3n = 33), autotetraploid tomato (AAAA, 4n = 48).
- Meiosis: Forms **multivalents** (quadrivalents) → unbalanced segregation → reduced fertility.
- Probability of balanced gamete = **2/3**; unbalanced = **1/3**.

**2. Allopolyploidy:**
Chromosome sets come from **hybridisation of two or more species**, followed by chromosome doubling (often using colchicine).
- Examples: **Triticale** (Triticum × Secale, 6n = 42), **bread wheat** (T. aestivum, 6n = 42), tobacco (Nicotiana tabacum, 4n = 48).
- Meiosis: Forms only **bivalents** (AA + BB) → **100% balanced gametes** → fully fertile.

**3. Induced Polyploidy:**
- **Colchicine treatment**: Inhibits spindle formation → blocks chromosome segregation → doubles chromosome number.
- **Heat/cold shock**: Produces unreduced gametes in plants.

**Significance in Plant Breeding:**

(i) **Increased vigour and yield:** Polyploids have larger cells, larger organs (gigantism) — used in apple, pear, watermelon (seedless triploids).

(ii) **Restoration of fertility in hybrids:** Crossing wheat × rye gives sterile F1 hybrid; doubling chromosomes gives fertile Triticale.

(iii) **Buffering against deleterious recessives:** Extra gene copies mask harmful recessive alleles.

(iv) **Seedless fruits:** Triploid watermelons, bananas, grapes — sterility is exploited commercially.

(v) **Synthesis of new crop species:** Triticale combines wheat's grain quality with rye's disease resistance and hardiness.

(vi) **Aneuploid series in wheat:** Nullisomics, monosomics, trisomics allow mapping of genes to specific chromosomes.

---

**(b) Describe the various types of translocations with suitable diagrams. Explain how reciprocal translocation leads to specific human cancers.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (b) — Translocations and Cancer:**

**Types of Translocations:**

**1. Simple Translocations (Single break):**
A segment from one chromosome is transferred to a non-homologous chromosome. The donor chromosome becomes acentric (functionally inert) and may be lost.

**2. Reciprocal Translocation (Interchange):**
Two chromosomes exchange segments; each requires one break per chromosome. No loss of genetic material, so balanced carriers are phenotypically normal.
- Cytogenetic signature at pachytene: **cross-shaped quadrivalent**.
- At anaphase I: Alternate segregation gives normal + balanced gametes; adjacent segregation gives unbalanced gametes → semi-sterility.

**3. Robertsonian Translocation (Centric Fusion):**
Fusion of two acrocentric chromosomes (13, 14, 15, 21, 22) at the centromere; produces a metacentric chromosome. The short-arm material is lost (carries only rRNA genes, present in multiple copies). **Balanced carriers have 45 chromosomes** but are phenotypically normal.

**4. Shift Translocation:**
A segment is interchanged among three or more chromosomes.

**Reciprocal Translocations and Human Cancer (Oncogenic Translocations):**

Specific translocations activate proto-oncogenes or create chimeric fusion genes with oncogenic activity.

| Cancer Type | Translocation | Molecular Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **Burkitt Lymphoma** | t(8;14)(q24;q32) | Places *MYC* oncogene under *IGH* enhancer → constitutive *MYC* expression |
| **Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML)** | t(9;22)(q34;q11) | Produces **Philadelphia chromosome**; creates *BCR-ABL1* fusion gene → constitutively active tyrosine kinase (target of imatinib) |
| **Acute Promyelocytic Leukemia (APL)** | t(15;17)(q22;q12) | *PML-RARA* fusion → blocks myeloid differentiation (target of all-trans retinoic acid, ATRA) |
| **Ewing Sarcoma** | t(11;22)(q24;q12) | *EWSR1-FLI1* fusion transcription factor |
| **Follicular Lymphoma** | t(14;18)(q32;q21) | *BCL2* overexpression (anti-apoptotic) |

**Mechanism:** Most oncogenic translocations follow the **"two-hit hypothesis"** of Knudson — one allele disrupted by translocation, the other lost or silenced. The fusion proteins formed are unique to the tumour cell and are powerful diagnostic markers (e.g., BCR-ABL PCR for CML).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Quick Recall — Pin These Points**

- **Numerical Variations** = Euploidy (exact multiples) + Aneuploidy (non-multiples).
- **Euploidy sub-types:** Haploid, Diploid, Autopolyploid, Allopolyploid.
- **Aneuploidy sub-types:** Monosomy (2n−1), Nullisomy (2n−2), Trisomy (2n+1), Tetrasomy (2n+2), Double Trisomy.
- **Trisomy 21 = Down syndrome**; Trisomy 13 = Patau; Trisomy 18 = Edwards; 45,X = Turner; 47,XXY = Klinefelter.
- **Structural Variations** require **2 chromosome breaks** (except terminal deletion = 1).
- **Inversion** is intra-chromosomal; **Translocation** is inter-chromosomal.
- **Paracentric** inversion = both breaks on one arm (no centromere involvement).
- **Pericentric** inversion = breaks span centromere (arm ratio changes).
- **Robertsonian translocation** = acrocentric fusion; main cause of familial Down syndrome.
- **Reciprocal translocation** = main cause of CML (BCR-ABL, Philadelphia chromosome) and Burkitt lymphoma (MYC-IGH).
- **Non-disjunction** is the primary cause of aneuploidy; **maternal age** is the biggest risk factor.
- **Dosage compensation** (Lyon hypothesis): Barr bodies = X chromosomes − 1.
- **Polyploidy fertility** = Autopolyploid (reduced, 1/3 unbalanced) vs Allopolyploid (100% fertile).
- **Colchicine** = spindle inhibitor → induces polyploidy.
- **Dicentric assay** = radiation dosimeter (linear-quadratic dose response: $Y = \alpha D + \beta D^2$).
- **Ring chromosome** = two telomeric breaks + fusion.
- **Isochromosome** = centromere transverse mis-division → mirror-image arms.
- **Cri-du-chat syndrome** = 5p− (terminal deletion).
- **Bar-eye *Drosophila*** = classic tandem duplication example.
- **Triticale** = classic KTU example of induced allopolyploidy.
- **Breakage-Reunion hypothesis** = foundation of structural aberration origin.
- **Unequal crossing-over** = primary mechanism for duplication and deletion.
- **Mosaicism** = mitotic non-disjunction (post-zygotic); chimerism = fusion of two zygotes.
- **Klinefelter** patients are phenotypically male (Y determines sex); Turner patients are phenotypically female (only X present).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic principles of inheritance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBB861 - CYTOGENETICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basic principles of inheritance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Basic Principles of Inheritance** constitute the foundational framework of classical genetics, formulated by **Gregor Johann Mendel** in **1865** through his pea plant (*Pisum sativum*) experiments. These principles describe how discrete hereditary units (now known as **genes**) are transmitted from parents to offspring in mathematically predictable patterns.

In KTU 2024 Scheme Cytogenetics (PEBB861), this topic is formally defined as the study of **Mendelian inheritance** comprising three cardinal laws:

1. **Law of Dominance** — In a heterozygous condition, one allele (dominant) masks the expression of the other (recessive).
2. **Law of Segregation** — During gamete formation, the paired alleles separate (segregate) so that each gamete receives only one allele.
3. **Law of Independent Assortment** — Alleles of different genes assort independently of one another during gamete formation, provided the genes are located on different chromosomes or are far apart on the same chromosome.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Students must be able to derive monohybrid and dihybrid ratios using both Punnett squares and probability methods. Numerical problems on test crosses and back crosses carry **high-frequency weightage** in end-semester examinations.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you have a **coin purse** containing two types of coins — gold and silver. When you empty the purse into two separate smaller purses (analogous to gamete formation), each small purse receives a *random* selection of the coins. This random distribution mirrors **Mendel's Law of Segregation**.

Now extend this to **two coin purses of different denominations** — say, a gold/silver purse and a ₹1/₹2 coin purse. The way gold or silver gets distributed has **no influence** on whether ₹1 or ₹2 coins get distributed into the same small purse. This independence mirrors the **Law of Independent Assortment**.

Finally, suppose gold coins "dominate" silver coins in appearance (gold masks silver) — this is the **Law of Dominance**.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminological Glossary

| **Term** | **Formal Meaning** | **Symbol Convention** |
|----------|-------------------|----------------------|
| Gene | Basic unit of heredity occupying a specific locus on a chromosome | — |
| Allele | Alternative forms of a gene | Capital (dominant) / lowercase (recessive) |
| Locus | Fixed position of a gene on a chromosome | — |
| Genotype | Genetic constitution of an organism | $RR$, $Rr$, $rr$ |
| Phenotype | Observable expression of a trait | Round / Wrinkled |
| Homozygous | Two identical alleles at a locus | $AA$, $aa$ |
| Heterozygous | Two different alleles at a locus | $Aa$ |
| Hemizygous | Only one allele present (e.g., X-linked in males) | — |
| Parental (P) | The original breeding generation | P |
| First Filial (F1) | Offspring of the P generation | F1 |
| Second Filial (F2) | Offspring produced by interbreeding F1 individuals | F2 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 question papers frequently test the **terminological distinction** between genotype and phenotype. Marks are deducted when students confuse these in monohybrid and dihybrid problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Laws of Mendel — Operational Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Law of Dominance

- **Operational Principle:** When two organisms with contrasting traits are crossed, the F1 generation expresses only one of the parental traits — the **dominant** trait. The unexpressed trait is termed **recessive**.
- **Why it works:** The dominant allele produces a functional protein that masks the effect of the non-functional recessive allele.
- **Classical Example:** Cross between a **pure tall pea plant (TT)** and a **pure dwarf pea plant (tt)** yields all **Tt (tall)** in F1.

### 2.1.2 Law of Segregation

- **Operational Principle:** During **meiosis** (specifically **Anaphase I**), homologous chromosomes carrying alleles separate so that each gamete receives only **one allele** of each gene.
- **Why it works:** Gametes are **haploid** ($n$); somatic cells are **diploid** ($2n$). Upon fertilization, the diploid state is restored.
- **Mathematical Consequence:** A heterozygote $Aa$ produces two types of gametes — $A$ and $a$ — in equal proportions (1:1).

### 2.1.3 Law of Independent Assortment

- **Operational Principle:** Genes located on **different chromosomes** (or far apart on the same chromosome) segregate **independently** during gamete formation.
- **Why it works:** Orientation of homologous chromosome pairs at the **metaphase plate** during Meiosis I is random, leading to independent distribution.
- **Mathematical Consequence:** For a heterozygote $AaBb$, the gametes produced are $AB$, $Ab$, $aB$, $ab$ — each with a probability of **¼**.

---

## 2.2 Real-World Engineering / Biological Utility

While this topic lies at the intersection of classical biology and mathematics, the principles find profound application in:

- **Medical Genetics:** Predicting the probability of inheriting autosomal recessive disorders such as **Cystic Fibrosis**, **Phenylketonuria (PKU)**, and **Sickle Cell Anemia**.
- **Agricultural Biotechnology:** Designing breeding programs for hybrid crop varieties (e.g., high-yielding hybrid maize, disease-resistant wheat).
- **Forensic Science:** DNA fingerprinting relies on the segregation and independent assortment of polymorphic loci.
- **Genetic Counseling:** Bayesian probability calculations based on Mendelian ratios help estimate recurrence risks in families.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates all high-yield mathematical relationships required for KTU 2024 examination problems on inheritance.

| **Concept** | **Genotypic Ratio (F2)** | **Phenotypic Ratio (F2)** | **Gamete Types** | **Probability Rule Used** |
|------------|--------------------------|---------------------------|-------------------|---------------------------|
| Monohybrid Cross | $1:2:1$ | $3:1$ | $2$ | Sum + Product |
| Dihybrid Cross | $1:2:1:2:4:2:1:2:1$ | $9:3:3:1$ | $4$ | Product (multiplication) |
| Test Cross (Mono) | $1:1$ | $1:1$ | $2$ | — |
| Test Cross (Di) | $1:1:1:1$ | $1:1:1:1$ | $4$ | — |
| Back Cross | Same as F1 genotype | Same as F1 phenotype | $2$ | — |
| Incomplete Dominance | $1:2:1$ | $1:2:1$ | $2$ | — |
| Codominance | $1:2:1$ | $1:2:1$ | $2$ | — |

### 2.3.1 Probability Rules (Critical for KTU Numerical Problems)

The **Probability Rule for Independent Events** is foundational. If $P(A)$ is the probability of event $A$ and $P(B)$ is the probability of event $B$, then:

- **Multiplication (AND) Rule** for independent events:

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \times P(B)$$

- **Addition (OR) Rule** for mutually exclusive events:

$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$$

- **Binomial Expansion Formula** for events with two outcomes (success/failure) in $n$ trials:

$$P = \binom{n}{r} p^{r} q^{n-r}$$

where $\binom{n}{r} = \dfrac{n!}{r!(n-r)!}$, $p$ is the probability of success, and $q = 1 - p$.

---

## 2.4 Why These Principles Matter — The 'How' Behind the 'Why'

Each Mendelian law emerges directly from **chromosomal behaviour during meiosis**:

- **Segregation** $\rightarrow$ Homologous chromosomes separate at **Anaphase I**.
- **Independent Assortment** $\rightarrow$ Bivalents orient **randomly** at the **Metaphase I** plate.
- **Dominance** $\rightarrow$ Determined by **allele-specific protein function**, not by chromosome behaviour.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Monohybrid F2 Ratio (3:1 Phenotypic, 1:2:1 Genotypic)

### 3.1.1 Setup of the Cross

Let the gene for plant height be denoted as:
- $T$ = dominant allele for **Tall**
- $t$ = recessive allele for **dwarf**

**Parental Cross (P):**

$$TT \times tt$$

**F1 Generation:**

Since each parent contributes one allele, all F1 offspring are **Tt (heterozygous tall)**:

$$\text{Gametes from } TT = T \quad ; \quad \text{Gametes from } tt = t$$

$$\text{F1} = Tt \text{ (100\% Tall)}$$

### 3.1.2 F1 × F1 Cross

**Intercrossing F1 individuals to produce F2:**

$$Tt \times Tt$$

**Gametes produced by each parent:** $T$ and $t$ in equal proportion ($½$ each).

### 3.1.3 Punnett Square Construction (Exhaustive Enumeration)

| Gametes | $T$ (½) | $t$ (½) |
|---------|---------|---------|
| **$T$ (½)** | $TT$ (¼) | $Tt$ (¼) |
| **$t$ (½)** | $Tt$ (¼) | $tt$ (¼) |

**Reading the four squares:**

- $TT$ : Homozygous tall — probability $¼$
- $Tt$ : Heterozygous tall — probability $¼ + ¼ = ½$
- $tt$ : Homozygous dwarf — probability $¼$

### 3.1.4 Genotypic Ratio (F2)

$$1 \, TT : 2 \, Tt : 1 \, tt \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1:2:1 \text{ genotypic ratio}$$

### 3.1.5 Phenotypic Ratio (F2)

Since both $TT$ and $Tt$ express the **Tall** phenotype:

$$\left(\frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{4}\right) \text{ Tall} : \frac{1}{4} \text{ dwarf}$$

$$= \frac{3}{4} \text{ Tall} : \frac{1}{4} \text{ dwarf}$$

$$\therefore \quad \text{Phenotypic Ratio (F2)} = 3:1$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Dihybrid F2 Ratio (9:3:3:1)

### 3.2.1 Setup of the Cross

Consider two traits simultaneously:
- **Seed shape:** $R$ (Round, dominant) / $r$ (wrinkled, recessive)
- **Seed color:** $Y$ (Yellow, dominant) / $y$ (green, recessive)

**Parental Cross (P):**

$$RRYY \times rryy$$

**Gametes from P:**
- $RRYY$ produces only one type: $RY$
- $rryy$ produces only one type: $ry$

**F1 Generation:**

$$\text{F1} = RrYy \quad \text{(100\% Round Yellow — due to dominance)}$$

### 3.2.2 F1 × F1 Cross (Dihybrid Intercross)

$$RrYy \times RrYy$$

**Gametes produced by each F1 parent:**

Since each parent is heterozygous at two loci, and the genes assort independently:

$$\text{Four gamete types} = RY, \ Ry, \ rY, \ ry$$

**Probability of each gamete type:**

$$P(RY) = P(R) \times P(Y) = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{4}$$

By symmetry:

$$P(Ry) = \frac{1}{4}, \quad P(rY) = \frac{1}{4}, \quad P(ry) = \frac{1}{4}$$

### 3.2.3 Exhaustive 4 × 4 Punnett Square

Combining four gametes from each parent (16 total combinations):

$$\begin{aligned}
&\text{RY} \times \text{RY} \rightarrow RRYY \\
&\text{RY} \times \text{Ry} \rightarrow RRYy \\
&\text{RY} \times \text{rY} \rightarrow RrYY \\
&\text{RY} \times \text{ry} \rightarrow RrYy \\
&\text{Ry} \times \text{RY} \rightarrow RRYy \\
&\text{Ry} \times \text{Ry} \rightarrow RRyy \\
&\text{Ry} \times \text{rY} \rightarrow Rryy \\
&\text{Ry} \times \text{ry} \rightarrow Rryy \\
&\text{rY} \times \text{RY} \rightarrow RrYY \\
&\text{rY} \times \text{Ry} \rightarrow Rryy \\
&\text{rY} \times \text{rY} \rightarrow rrYY \\
&\text{rY} \times \text{ry} \rightarrow rrYy \\
&\text{ry} \times \text{RY} \rightarrow RrYy \\
&\text{ry} \times \text{Ry} \rightarrow Rryy \\
&\text{ry} \times \text{rY} \rightarrow rrYy \\
&\text{ry} \times \text{ry} \rightarrow rryy \\
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2.4 Phenotypic Categorization

Now we group the 16 squares into phenotypic classes by counting:

**Round Yellow ($R\_Y\_$) — at least one $R$ AND at least one $Y$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Count} &= RRYY + RRYy + RrYY + RrYy \text{(×2)} + RRYy \\
&= 1 + 2 + 2 + 4 + 2 \\
&= \text{verify by probability method:}
\end{aligned}$$

Using the multiplication rule for **two independent monohybrid ratios**:

$$\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Round}) \times P(\text{Yellow}) &= \frac{3}{4} \times \frac{3}{4} = \frac{9}{16} \\
P(\text{Round}) \times P(\text{green}) &= \frac{3}{4} \times \frac{1}{4} = \frac{3}{16} \\
P(\text{wrinkled}) \times P(\text{Yellow}) &= \frac{1}{4} \times \frac{3}{4} = \frac{3}{16} \\
P(\text{wrinkled}) \times P(\text{green}) &= \frac{1}{4} \times \frac{1}{4} = \frac{1}{16}
\end{aligned}$$

**Phenotypic Ratio (F2):**

$$9 \text{ Round Yellow} : 3 \text{ Round Green} : 3 \text{ wrinkled Yellow} : 1 \text{ wrinkled Green}$$

### 3.2.5 Genotypic Ratio (F2)

Counting from the 16 Punnett squares:

$$1:2:1:2:4:2:1:2:1$$

corresponding to:
$RRYY : RRYy : RRyy : RrYY : RrYy : Rryy : rrYY : rrYy : rryy$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Test Cross Ratio

A **test cross** is performed by crossing an organism with a **phenotype of dominant expression** (but unknown genotype) with a **homozygous recessive** organism.

### 3.3.1 Monohybrid Test Cross

Suppose we have a **Tall plant of unknown genotype** — could be $TT$ or $Tt$.

**Case 1:** If plant is $TT$:

$$TT \times tt \rightarrow \text{All } Tt \text{ (Tall)}$$

**Case 2:** If plant is $Tt$:

$$Tt \times tt \rightarrow \frac{1}{2} Tt \text{ (Tall)} : \frac{1}{2} tt \text{ (dwarf)}$$

$$\therefore \text{Test Cross Ratio} = 1:1 \text{ (phenotypic)}$$

### 3.3.2 Dihybrid Test Cross

For a plant with genotype $RrYy$ (Round Yellow) crossed with $rryy$ (wrinkled green):

$$\text{Four offspring types in equal proportion (1:1:1:1)}$$

| **Phenotype** | **Probability** |
|---------------|----------------|
| Round Yellow | $¼$ |
| Round Green | $¼$ |
| wrinkled Yellow | $¼$ |
| wrinkled Green | $¼$ |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** In pea plants, tall (T) is dominant over dwarf (t), and yellow seeds (Y) are dominant over green seeds (y). A plant heterozygous for both traits is crossed with a plant homozygous recessive for both traits. Calculate the expected phenotypic ratio among 800 offspring.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify parental genotypes.

$$TtYy \times ttyy$$

**Step 2:** Identify gametes from each parent.

- Gametes from $TtYy$: $TY$, $Ty$, $tY$, $ty$ (each $¼$)
- Gametes from $ttyy$: only $ty$ (probability $1$)

**Step 3:** Determine offspring genotypes using the product rule.

Since $ttyy$ only contributes $ty$, the offspring phenotype depends entirely on the gamete from $TtYy$:

| **Gamete from TtYy** | **Probability** | **Offspring Phenotype** |
|---------------------|----------------|------------------------|
| $TY$ | $¼$ | TtYy (Tall, Yellow) |
| $Ty$ | $¼$ | Ttyy (Tall, Green) |
| $tY$ | $¼$ | ttYy (Dwarf, Yellow) |
| $ty$ | $¼$ | ttyy (Dwarf, Green) |

**Step 4:** Expected phenotypic ratio:

$$1:1:1:1$$

**Step 5:** Expected numbers out of 800 offspring:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Tall Yellow} &= \frac{1}{4} \times 800 = 200 \\
\text{Tall Green} &= \frac{1}{4} \times 800 = 200 \\
\text{Dwarf Yellow} &= \frac{1}{4} \times 800 = 200 \\
\text{Dwarf Green} &= \frac{1}{4} \times 800 = 200 \\
\end{aligned}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Monohybrid Inheritance Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Parental Generation P"] --> B["Homozygous Dominant TT"]
    A --> C["Homozygous Recessive tt"]
    B --> D["Gamete Formation Meiosis"]
    C --> D
    D --> E["Gametes T and t"]
    E --> F["Fertilization Event"]
    F --> G["F1 Generation All Tt Heterozygous Tall"]
    G --> H["Intercrossing F1 x F1"]
    H --> I["Gametes T and t Each 1/2"]
    I --> J["Punnett Square 2x2"]
    J --> K["F2 Genotypes 1 TT 2 Tt 1 tt"]
    K --> L["F2 Phenotypes 3 Tall 1 Dwarf"]
    L --> M["Phenotypic Ratio 3 to 1"]
    L --> N["Genotypic Ratio 1 to 2 to 1"]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Dihybrid Cross Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Parent RRYY Pure Round Yellow"] --> G1["Gamete RY Only"]
    P2["Parent rryy Pure wrinkled Green"] --> G2["Gamete ry Only"]
    G1 --> F1["F1 Generation RrYy All Round Yellow"]
    G2 --> F1
    F1 --> IC["Intercross F1 x F1"]
    IC --> GAM["Four Gamete Types RY Ry rY ry"]
    GAM --> PS["4x4 Punnett Square 16 Squares"]
    PS --> CAT["Phenotypic Categorization"]
    CAT --> C1["9 Round Yellow"]
    CAT --> C2["3 Round Green"]
    CAT --> C3["3 wrinkled Yellow"]
    CAT --> C4["1 wrinkled Green"]
    C1 --> RAT["Dihybrid Ratio 9 colon 3 colon 3 colon 1"]
    C2 --> RAT
    C3 --> RAT
    C4 --> RAT
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Test Cross Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Unknown Phenotype Tall Plant"] --> CROSS["Cross with Homozygous Recessive tt"]
    CROSS --> OBS["Observe Offspring Phenotypes"]
    OBS --> Q1{"Any Dwarf Offspring Present?"}
```
