```mermaid
    Q1 -- YES --> HET["Genotype of Parent is Tt Heterozygous"]
    Q1 -- NO --> HOM["Genotype of Parent is TT Homozygous"]
    HET --> RAT1["Test Cross Ratio 1 Tall 1 Dwarf 1 to 1"]
    HOM --> RAT2["Test Cross Ratio All Tall"]
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Mendelian Inheritance System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Parental Input"]
        P1G["Parent 1 Gametes"]
        P2G["Parent 2 Gametes"]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS["Meiotic Process"]
        SEG["Law of Segregation Homologous Pair Separation"]
        IND["Law of Independent Assortment Random Orientation"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["Offspring Output"]
        F1GEN["F1 Genotype"]
        F1PHE["F1 Phenotype"]
        F2GEN["F2 Genotypic Ratio"]
        F2PHE["F2 Phenotypic Ratio"]
    end
    P1G --> SEG
    P2G --> SEG
    SEG --> IND
    IND --> F1GEN
    F1GEN --> F1PHE
    F1PHE --> F2GEN
    F2GEN --> F2PHE
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Define Mendel's Law of Segregation. How does it relate to the behaviour of chromosomes during meiosis?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Mendel's **Law of Segregation** states that during gamete formation, the two alleles for a given trait **separate** (segregate) so that each gamete receives only **one allele**. When fertilization occurs, the offspring receives one allele from each parent, restoring the diploid state.

**Chromosomal Correlation (½ mark):**
The physical basis of segregation lies in **Anaphase I of meiosis**, where homologous chromosomes (each carrying one allele of a gene pair) are pulled apart to opposite poles of the cell. This ensures that each resulting gamete is **haploid** and carries only one allele per locus.

**Key Feature (½ mark):**
In a heterozygote $Aa$, the two alleles segregate in a **1:1 ratio** into the gametes, which is the foundation for the **1:2:1 genotypic** and **3:1 phenotypic** ratio observed in F2.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Distinguish between genotype and phenotype with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Basis** | **Genotype** | **Phenotype** |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|
| Definition | The genetic constitution of an organism at one or more loci | The observable expression of the genotype |
| Visibility | Not directly observable; determined by molecular/progeny analysis | Directly observable (morphology, colour, behaviour) |
| Stability | Generally constant through life | Can be modified by environment |
| Example | $Tt$, $TT$, $tt$ | Tall / Dwarf |

**Example (1 Mark):**
In pea plants, both $TT$ and $Tt$ genotypes express the **Tall phenotype**, but they differ in their genetic constitution. The $tt$ genotype expresses the **dwarf phenotype**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice A]`

**(a) State and explain Mendel's Law of Independent Assortment. (7 Marks)**

**(b) In a dihybrid cross between pure round yellow (RRYY) and wrinkled green (rryy) pea plants, derive the F2 phenotypic ratio of 9:3:3:1 using a Punnett square. (7 Marks)**

---

**Model Answer:**

### Part (a) — Mendel's Law of Independent Assortment (7 Marks)

**Statement (2 Marks):**
The Law of Independent Assortment states that when two or more pairs of alleles are involved in a cross, the alleles of different gene pairs assort **independently** of one another during gamete formation, provided the genes are located on **different chromosomes** (or are far apart on the same chromosome).

**Explanation (3 Marks):**
During Meiosis I, homologous chromosome pairs (bivalents) align randomly at the **metaphase plate**. The orientation of one bivalent is **independent** of the orientation of any other bivalent. Consequently, the maternal and paternal chromosomes are distributed into gametes in all possible combinations with equal probability.

**Example (2 Marks):**
For a pea plant heterozygous at two loci ($RrYy$), the four possible gametes — $RY$, $Ry$, $rY$, $ry$ — are produced in **equal frequency (¼ each)**. This is mathematically equivalent to the product of two independent monohybrid events:

$$P(RY) = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{4}$$

---

### Part (b) — Dihybrid Cross Derivation (7 Marks)

**Parental Generation:**

$$RRYY \times rryy$$

**F1 Generation:**

All F1 individuals are $RrYy$ (Round Yellow — due to dominance).

**F1 × F1 Intercross:**

$$RrYy \times RrYy$$

**Gametes from each F1:** $RY$, $Ry$, $rY$, $ry$ (each $¼$)

**4 × 4 Punnett Square (16 squares):**

| Gametes | RY | Ry | rY | ry |
|---------|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| **RY** | RRYY | RRYy | RrYY | RrYy |
| **Ry** | RRYy | RRyy | RrYy | Rryy |
| **rY** | RrYY | RrYy | rrYY | rrYy |
| **ry** | RrYy | Rryy | rrYy | rryy |

**Phenotypic Categorization (counting from the 16 squares):**

- **Round Yellow ($R\_Y\_$):** 9 squares → **9/16**
- **Round Green ($R\_yy$):** 3 squares → **3/16**
- **wrinkled Yellow ($rrY\_$):** 3 squares → **3/16**
- **wrinkled Green ($rryy$):** 1 square → **1/16**

**Final Phenotypic Ratio:**

$$\boxed{9:3:3:1}$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice B]`

**(a) Explain the difference between a monohybrid test cross and a monohybrid back cross. (7 Marks)**

**(b) In a cross between a heterozygous tall pea plant (Tt) and a dwarf pea plant (tt), predict the genotypic and phenotypic ratios of the offspring. If 240 seeds are obtained, how many are expected to be tall and how many dwarf? (7 Marks)**

---

**Model Answer:**

### Part (a) — Test Cross vs Back Cross (7 Marks)

**Test Cross (3 Marks):**
A test cross is the cross between an organism showing the **dominant phenotype** (but with unknown genotype) and an organism that is **homozygous recessive** for the same trait. Its purpose is to **determine the genotype** of the dominant-expressing parent.
- Example: $Tt \times tt$ — used to distinguish between $TT$ and $Tt$.

**Back Cross (2 Marks):**
A back cross is the cross between an F1 hybrid and **either of its parents** (or a genetically identical individual). It is used to **recover the parental genotype** in breeding programs.
- Example: $Tt \times TT$ (back cross to dominant parent) OR $Tt \times tt$ (back cross to recessive parent).

**Key Distinction (2 Marks):**

| Feature | Test Cross | Back Cross |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| Partner used | Always homozygous recessive | Either parent (or genetic equivalent) |
| Purpose | Genotype determination | Recovering parental traits |
| Ratio (monohybrid) | 1:1 (when partner is heterozygous) | Varies (1:1 with recessive, all dominant with homozygous dominant) |

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Prediction (7 Marks)

**Cross:**

$$Tt \times tt$$

**Gametes:**
- From $Tt$: $T$ (½), $t$ (½)
- From $tt$: $t$ (only)

**Punnett Square (2 × 2):**

| | $T$ (½) | $t$ (½) |
|---|---|---|
| **$t$ (1)** | $Tt$ (½) | $tt$ (½) |

**Genotypic Ratio:**

$$1 \, Tt : 1 \, tt \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1:1$$

**Phenotypic Ratio:**

$$1 \text{ Tall} : 1 \text{ dwarf}$$

**Expected Numbers out of 240 Offspring (3 Marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Tall} &= \frac{1}{2} \times 240 = 120 \\
\text{Dwarf} &= \frac{1}{2} \times 240 = 120 \\
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Tall} = 120 \quad ; \quad \text{Dwarf} = 120}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing Test Cross with Back Cross (–2 Marks):** A test cross *always* uses a homozygous recessive partner. If a student writes "$Tt \times TT$" and calls it a test cross, marks will be deducted.
> 2. **Forgetting the F1 Genotype Step (–1 Mark):** When deriving dihybrid ratios, students often skip the F1 genotype ($RrYy$) and jump directly to the F2 square.
> 3. **Misinterpreting Independent Assortment as Universality (–2 Marks):** Independent assortment applies only when genes are on **different chromosomes** (or unlinked). Genes on the same chromosome that are close together exhibit **linkage** and deviate from 9:3:3:1.
> 4. **Phenotype–Genotype Confusion in Ratios (–2 Marks):** Writing "1:2:1 phenotypic ratio" for a monohybrid cross instead of "3:1" loses marks.
> 5. **Skipping Units in Numerical Problems (–1 Mark):** Always write the final answer with units (e.g., "120 tall plants") and box the numerical result.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Three Mendelian Laws:** Dominance, Segregation, Independent Assortment.
- **Monohybrid F2:** Genotypic $1:2:1$; Phenotypic $3:1$.
- **Dihybrid F2:** Genotypic $1:2:1:2:4:2:1:2:1$; Phenotypic $9:3:3:1$.
- **Test Cross Partner:** Always homozygous recessive ($tt$ or $rryy$).
- **Test Cross Ratios:** Monohybrid $1:1$; Dihybrid $1:1:1:1$.
- **Back Cross Partner:** Either parental genotype.
- **Gametes of $AaBb$:** Four types ($AB$, $Ab$, $aB$, $ab$), each with probability $¼$.
- **Probability Rules:** Multiplication (AND) and Addition (OR) for independent/mutually exclusive events.
- **Chromosomal Basis:** Segregation ↔ Anaphase I; Independent Assortment ↔ Metaphase I orientation.
- **Genotype vs Phenotype:** Genotype is genetic; Phenotype is observable.
- **Common F1 Generation:** Always heterozygous at all loci under consideration.
- **Mathematical Tool:** Punnett square for visual enumeration; Probability method for quick numerical solving.
- **Linked genes** violate independent assortment → no $9:3:3:1$ ratio.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chromosomal basis of Mendelism
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBB861 - CYTOGENETICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Chromosomal basis of Mendelism # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Chromosomal Theory of Inheritance** (also termed the **Sutton-Boveri Hypothesis**) is the foundational cytogenetic principle proposed independently by **Walter Sutton (1902)** and **Theodor Boveri (1903)**, which states that the **Mendelian hereditary factors (genes)** are physically located on **chromosomes**, and that the behavior of chromosomes during meiosis provides the physical and mathematical basis for the inheritance patterns first described by Gregor Mendel. In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned Cytogenetics framework, this theory bridges classical genetics (Mendelism) with molecular cytology, establishing that: 1. **Genes** are the units of heredity situated at fixed loci on chromosomes. 2. **Homologous chromosomes** carry alleles of the same gene at identical loci. 3. The **separation of homologous chromosomes** during Anaphase I of meiosis is the cytological equivalent of Mendel's **Law of Segregation**. 4. The **independent orientation** of non-homologous chromosome pairs at the Metaphase I plate is the cytological equivalent of Mendel's **Law of Independent Assortment**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is the cornerstone of cytogenetics because it unifies the abstract mathematical ratios of Mendelism with the observable physical behavior of chromosomes during gametogenesis. Questions from this module frequently appear as 7-mark or 14-mark problems in End Semester Evaluations (ESE). ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Think of a **wardrobe with two large shelves** (the two homologous chromosomes of a pair). Each shelf has a fixed slot for a particular type of clothing — say, slot for *shirts*. The two shelves together represent the diploid genotype. The *shirt* slot is the **gene locus**, and the actual shirt kept in that slot is the **allele**. When the wardrobe owner packs for a trip (meiosis), he selects **one shirt from each slot on one shelf only** — packing only one full shelf. This is exactly what happens in Anaphase I: the cell divides, and each gamete receives only **one chromosome from each homologous pair**, carrying with it one allele per gene. If he has two different shirts (say, blue and red — heterozygote), then half the trips get the blue shirt and half get the red shirt — a perfect 1:1 segregation. If he has multiple wardrobes (non-homologous chromosome pairs) and he packs one shelf from each wardrobe independently, the colors end up in random combinations — independent assortment. ## 1.3 Key Terminology Standardized for KTU | Term | Standard Cytogenetic Meaning | |---|---| | **Locus (plural: Loci)** | The fixed, addressable physical position of a gene on a chromosome | | **Allele** | One of two or more alternative forms of a gene occupying the same locus on homologous chromosomes | | **Homologous Chromosomes** | A pair of chromosomes (one maternal, one paternal) that are similar in size, shape, centromere position, and carry the same gene loci | | **Haploid (n)** | A cell containing **one complete set** of chromosomes (e.g., gametes) | | **Diploid (2n)** | A cell containing **two complete sets** of chromosomes (e.g., somatic cells) | | **Synapsis** | The intimate pairing of homologous chromosomes during Prophase I of meiosis | | **Bivalent / Tetrad** | The paired homologous chromosomes (4 chromatids) formed during Prophase I | | **Chiasma (plural: Chiasmata)** | The visible point of physical exchange (crossing over) between non-sister chromatids of homologous chromosomes | > [!NOTE] > **NEP 2020 Outcome Mapping:** This topic directly maps to **CO2** of the PEBB861 syllabus — *"Illustrate the chromosomal basis of inheritance and explain how chromosomal behavior validates Mendelian principles."* # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Pillars of the Sutton-Boveri Parallelism The cytogenetic basis of Mendelism rests on **four critical parallel observations** that link Mendel's abstract laws to the physical behavior of chromosomes: ### Pillar 1: Chromosomes Occur in Pairs (Diploidy ↔ Allelic Pairs) - Somatic cells contain **two homologous chromosomes** for every autosomal chromosome — one inherited from the mother, one from the father. - This exactly parallels Mendel's observation that organisms carry **two alleles** for every gene (AA, Aa, or aa). - A heterozygote (Aa) physically has one chromosome carrying A and its homolog carrying a. ### Pillar 2: Segregation During Meiosis ↔ Law of Segregation - During **Anaphase I** of meiosis, homologous chromosomes **separate** and migrate to opposite poles. - As a result, each gamete receives **only one chromosome** from each homologous pair and therefore only **one allele** for each gene. - This physical separation is the cytological mechanism that produces Mendel's 1:1 allele ratio in gametes. ### Pillar 3: Independent Orientation ↔ Law of Independent Assortment - At **Metaphase I**, homologous pairs align independently at the equatorial plate. - The orientation of each bivalent (maternal/paternal) toward the poles is **random** and independent of the orientation of every other bivalent. - For **n** pairs of homologous chromosomes, there are **2ⁿ possible gamete combinations**. - A diploid organism with n = 2 (e.g., pea, where Mendel studied two genes) produces 2² = 4 genetically distinct gametes (AB, Ab, aB, ab) in equal proportion (1:1:1:1). ### Pillar 4: Gamete Fusion Restores Diploidy ↔ Fertilization - During fertilization, two haploid gametes fuse to form a **diploid zygote**, restoring the paired condition. - This explains why inheritance patterns remain constant across generations. ## 2.2 Meiosis as the Physical Engine of Mendel's Laws Meiosis consists of two sequential divisions — **Meiosis I (Reductional)** and **Meiosis II (Equational)** — each with four phases: Prophase, Metaphase, Anaphase, Telophase. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** > - **Meiosis I** → separates **homologous chromosomes** (Law of Segregation operates here). > - **Meiosis II** → separates **sister chromatids** (analogous to mitosis). ### 2.2.1 Critical Meiotic Events Mapped to Mendel's Laws | Meiotic Event | Mendelian Law Explained | Phase | |---|---|---| | Synapsis of homologs | Allows physical exchange of alleles | Prophase I (Zygotene) | | Crossing over | Produces recombinant alleles | Prophase I (Pachytene) | | Random orientation of bivalents at Metaphase plate | Independent Assortment | Metaphase I | | Separation of homologs | Segregation of alleles | Anaphase I | | Separation of sister chromatids | Equational distribution | Anaphase II | ## 2.3 The Discovery of Linkage: A Critical Refinement Mendel's Law of Independent Assortment holds **only when genes are located on different chromosomes or are very far apart on the same chromosome**. In **1905**, **William Bateson** and **Reginald Punnett** discovered **linkage** in sweet peas (Lathyrus odoratus), observing that some gene combinations were inherited together more frequently than expected. This was confirmed cytologically and genetically by **Thomas Hunt Morgan** (1910) using **Drosophila melanogaster**, who proposed: - Genes are arranged in a **linear sequence** on chromosomes. - Genes located close together on the same chromosome tend to be inherited together (**linked genes**). - The physical exchange of segments between non-sister chromatids of homologous chromosomes (**crossing over**) breaks linkages and produces **recombinants**. ## 2.4 The Cytological Proof: Creighton and McClintock (1931) **Harriet Creighton** and **Barbara McClintock** provided the **first cytological proof** of crossing over using maize (Zea mays). They used a chromosome that had: - A **translocation** (cytological marker — knob at one end) - A **colored endosperm gene** (genetic marker — C for colored, c for colorless) When crossing over occurred between the gene and the translocation breakpoint, the cytological marker and the genetic marker were exchanged simultaneously — proving that **crossing over involves a physical exchange of chromosome segments**. > [!NOTE] > **Why This Matters:** Creighton-McClintock's experiment is the single most important cytogenetic validation of the chromosome theory. It is frequently asked in KTU ESE as a 7-mark or 14-mark question. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following equations are essential for solving linkage, recombination, and mapping problems in the KTU 2024 scheme. | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Boundary Condition | |---|---|---|---| | Recombination Frequency (RF) | $\text{RF} = \dfrac{\text{Number of recombinant progeny}}{\text{Total number of progeny}} \times 100$ | Expressed in percentage | 0% (completely linked) ≤ RF ≤ 50% (unlinked) | | Map Distance | $\text{Map Distance (cM)} = \text{RF (\%)}$ | 1% RF = 1 centiMorgan (cM) | Valid for distances < 20 cM (linear) | | Number of Gamete Types | $2^{n}$ | n = number of heterozygous gene pairs | Assumes independent assortment | | Expected Phenotypic Ratio (Dihybrid, Independent) | 9 : 3 : 3 : 1 | n = 2 heterozygotes | Modified if linkage present | | Test Cross Ratio (Linked Genes) | 1 : 1 (parental) + recombinant classes | Equal parental, less recombinant | Linkage phase matters | | Coefficient of Coincidence | $\text{CoC} = \dfrac{\text{Observed DCO}}{\text{Expected DCO}}$ | DCO = Double Crossover | 0 ≤ CoC ≤ 1 | | Interference | $I = 1 - \text{CoC}$ | I = 1 means no DCO observed | 0 ≤ I ≤ 1 | ## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering and Biotechnology The chromosomal basis of Mendelism is not merely academic. It has direct engineering applications in: - **Medical Genetics**: Predicting inheritance probabilities of Mendelian disorders (e.g., cystic fibrosis, sickle cell anemia). - **Forensic DNA Fingerprinting**: Exploiting STR (Short Tandem Repeat) polymorphisms whose inheritance follows Mendelian principles. - **Agricultural Biotechnology**: Designing breeding programs for marker-assisted selection (MAS) in crops. - **Genome Mapping**: The Human Genome Project relied entirely on recombination frequency calculations derived from this theory. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivations (Exhaustive, Step-by-Step) ### 3.1.1 Derivation: Number of Gamete Types from Independent Assortment **Problem:** Show that a diploid organism heterozygous for *n* gene pairs produces exactly $2^{n}$ genetically distinct gametes. **Derivation:** Consider a diploid organism with genotype $AaBbCc$ (n = 3 heterozygous gene pairs). During Meiosis I, three bivalents (one for each homologous pair) align at the Metaphase plate. Each bivalent has two possible orientations relative to the spindle poles: $$\text{Bivalent for } A/a : \text{Orientation 1 (A to pole 1)} \quad \text{or} \quad \text{Orientation 2 (A to pole 2)}$$ $$\text{Bivalent for } B/b : \text{Orientation 1 (B to pole 1)} \quad \text{or} \quad \text{Orientation 2 (B to pole 2)}$$ $$\text{Bivalent for } C/c : \text{Orientation 1 (C to pole 1)} \quad \text{or} \quad \text{Orientation 2 (C to pole 2)}$$ The number of **independent orientation combinations** is the product of the choices per bivalent: $$N_{\text{combinations}} = 2 \times 2 \times 2 = 2^{n}$$ Each combination yields a unique gamete. Therefore: $$\boxed{N_{\text{distinct gametes}} = 2^{n}}$$ For n = 3 (AaBbCc), the eight gametes are: ABC, ABc, AbC, Abc, aBC, aBc, abC, abc, each produced with equal probability $\dfrac{1}{2^{n}} = \dfrac{1}{8}$. ### 3.1.2 Derivation: Recombination Frequency from a Test Cross **Problem:** In maize, a test cross involving genes *A* (aleurone color) and *Bt* (leaf shape) yielded the following progeny: | Phenotype | Count | Class | |---|---|---| | Colored, Normal leaf | 645 | Parental | | Colorless, Broad leaf | 612 | Parental | | Colored, Broad leaf | 87 | Recombinant | | Colorless, Normal leaf | 78 | Recombinant | Calculate the recombination frequency and the map distance between genes A and Bt. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Identify the parental and recombinant classes. The parental classes are the most numerous (645 and 612). The recombinant classes are the least numerous (87 and 78) — they arose from crossing over in the heterozygous parent. **Step 2:** Sum all progeny to get the total. $$N_{\text{total}} = 645 + 612 + 87 + 78$$ $$N_{\text{total}} = 1422$$ **Step 3:** Sum the recombinant progeny. $$N_{\text{recombinant}} = 87 + 78 = 165$$ **Step 4:** Apply the Recombination Frequency formula. $$\text{RF} = \dfrac{N_{\text{recombinant}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100$$ $$\text{RF} = \dfrac{165}{1422} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{\text{RF} = 11.60\%}$$ **Step 5:** Convert to map distance. $$\text{Map Distance} = \text{RF} = 11.60 \text{ cM}$$ The genes A and Bt are **11.6 centiMorgans** apart on the same chromosome. ### 3.1.3 Derivation: Coefficient of Coincidence and Interference **Problem:** A three-point test cross involving genes A, B, and C produced the following progeny (parental classes = most numerous): | Class | Phenotype | Count | |---|---|---| | 1 | ABC | 568 | | 2 | abc | 572 | | 3 | AbC | 113 | | 4 | aBc | 109 | | 5 | ABc | 65 | | 6 | abC | 63 | | 7 | Abc | 9 | | 8 | aBC | 11 | Determine (i) the gene order, (ii) map distances, (iii) coefficient of coincidence, (iv) interference. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Identify the gene order. The two most numerous classes (1 and 2) are the **parental types**. The two least numerous (7 and 8) are the **double crossover (DCO)** classes. Comparing DCO with parental reveals the middle gene. - Parental: ABC / abc - DCO: Abc / aBC The alleles flipped between parental and DCO are at gene B (B became b, and b became B). Therefore, **B is the middle gene**. $$\text{Gene order: } A - B - C$$ **Step 2:** Identify single crossover (SCO) classes. - SCO in region I (between A and B): Classes 3 and 4 (AbC, aBc) → 113 + 109 = 222 - SCO in region II (between B and C): Classes 5 and 6 (ABc, abC) → 65 + 63 = 128 - DCO: Classes 7 and 8 (Abc, aBC) → 9 + 11 = 20 **Step 3:** Calculate total progeny. $$N_{\text{total}} = 568 + 572 + 113 + 109 + 65 + 63 + 9 + 11$$ $$N_{\text{total}} = 1510$$ **Step 4:** Calculate map distance A–B. $$\text{RF}_{A-B} = \dfrac{\text{SCO}_{\text{I}} + \text{DCO}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100$$ $$\text{RF}_{A-B} = \dfrac{222 + 20}{1510} \times 100 = \dfrac{242}{1510} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{\text{RF}_{A-B} = 16.03 \text{ cM}}$$ **Step 5:** Calculate map distance B–C. $$\text{RF}_{B-C} = \dfrac{\text{SCO}_{\text{II}} + \text{DCO}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100$$ $$\text{RF}_{B-C} = \dfrac{128 + 20}{1510} \times 100 = \dfrac{148}{1510} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{\text{RF}_{B-C} = 9.80 \text{ cM}}$$ **Step 6:** Calculate the coefficient of coincidence. $$\text{Expected DCO} = \text{RF}_{A-B} \times \text{RF}_{B-C} \times N_{\text{total}}$$ $$\text{Expected DCO} = 0.1603 \times 0.0980 \times 1510$$ $$\text{Expected DCO} = 2.37$$ $$\text{CoC} = \dfrac{\text{Observed DCO}}{\text{Expected DCO}} = \dfrac{20}{2.37}$$ $$\boxed{\text{CoC} = 8.44}$$ Wait — a CoC > 1 is biologically unusual. This indicates a calculation error in the parental hypothesis or that the data are illustrative only. In KTU exams, the **expected calculation** is: $$\text{Expected DCO proportion} = \text{RF}_{A-B} \times \text{RF}_{B-C} = 0.1603 \times 0.0980 = 0.01571$$ $$\text{Expected DCO count} = 0.01571 \times 1510 = 23.72$$ $$\text{CoC} = \dfrac{20}{23.72} = 0.843$$ **Step 7:** Calculate interference. $$I = 1 - \text{CoC} = 1 - 0.843$$ $$\boxed{I = 0.157}$$ This means approximately **15.7%** of the expected double crossovers did not occur — an indication of mild **chromosomal interference**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Marking Insight:** Examiners expect students to identify the **DCO class** as the lowest-frequency class and use the **flip allele** technique to determine gene order. Marks are commonly lost when students confuse SCO and DCO counts. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Chromosomal Basis of Mendel's Laws The following block diagram illustrates the logical architecture connecting the four Mendelian principles to their cytological counterparts during meiosis. ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Mendel Abstraction
Hereditary Factors] --> B1[Chromosomal Theory
Sutton 1902 Boveri 1903] B1 --> C1[Genes are located on
chromosomes at fixed loci] C1 --> D1[Homologous chromosomes
carry alleles of same gene] C1 --> D2[Meiosis is the
physical mechanism] D2 --> E1[Prophase I
Synapsis and Crossing Over] D2 --> E2[Metaphase I
Random Orientation] D2 --> E3[Anaphase I
Separation of Homologs] D2 --> E4[Anaphase II
Separation of Chromatids] E1 --> F1[Recombinants and
Linkage Breakage] E2 --> F2[Law of Independent
Assortment 2 to power n gametes] E3 --> F3[Law of Segregation
1 to 1 allele ratio] E4 --> F4[Haploid Gametes
n chromosomes] F1 --> G1[Genetic Mapping
Recombination Frequency] F2 --> G2[Classical Mendelian
Ratios 9 3 3 1] F3 --> G3[Mendelian Phenotypic
Ratios 3 to 1 in monohybrid] F4 --> G4[Fertilization Restores
Diploid 2n State] G1 --> H1[Creighton McClintock 1931
Cytological Proof in Maize] H1 --> I1[Final Validation
Physical Exchange of Segments] G2 --> I1 G3 --> I1 G4 --> I1 I1 --> J1[Modern Molecular
Cytogenetics FISH Karyotyping
Genome Mapping] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Parallelism Between Meiosis and Mendelian Inheritance ```mermaid flowchart LR M1[Meiotic Stage] --> M2[Chromosomal Event] M2 --> M3[Mendelian Outcome] M1 --> P1[Prophase I] P1 --> P2[Synapsis and Tetrad Formation] P2 --> P3[Allelic Pairing Physically] M1 --> P4[Metaphase I] P4 --> P5[Random Orientation of Bivalents] P5 --> P6[Independent Assortment of Genes] M1 --> P7[Anaphase I] P7 --> P8[Disjunction of Homologs] P8 --> P9[Segregation of Alleles 1 to 1] M1 --> P10[Anaphase II] P10 --> P11[Disjunction of Sister Chromatids] P11 --> P12[Equational Distribution] P12 --> P13[Fertilization] P13 --> P14[Restoration of Diploidy] P14 --> P15[Mendelian Ratios in F2] ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Logic Tree for Solving KTU Linkage Problems ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1[Problem Statement Given] --> Q2{How many genes
involved} Q2 --> Q3[Two Genes] Q2 --> Q4[Three Genes] Q3 --> R1[Two Point Test Cross] R1 --> R2[Identify Parental Most Numerous] R2 --> R3[Identify Recombinant Least Numerous] R3 --> R4[Apply RF Formula
Recombinants by Total times 100] R4 --> R5[Map Distance in cM] Q4 --> R6[Three Point Test Cross] R6 --> R7[Identify Parental Most Numerous] R7 --> R8[Identify DCO Least Numerous] R8 --> R9[Determine Middle Gene
by Flip Allele] R9 --> R10[Identify SCO Regions I and II] R10 --> R11[Compute RF for Region I A to B] R10 --> R12[Compute RF for Region II B to C] R11 --> R13[Compute CoC and Interference] R12 --> R13 R13 --> R14[Construct Genetic Map] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** Each block represents a logical node. Students can trace any question through the diagram to identify the correct computational strategy. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: Define the Chromosomal Theory of Inheritance. Who proposed it and what were the key observations that led to its formulation?** **Model Answer:** The Chromosomal Theory of Inheritance states that **Mendelian genes are physically located on chromosomes** and that the behavior of chromosomes during meiosis provides the physical basis for Mendel's laws of heredity. It was independently proposed by **Walter Sutton** (1902) and **Theodor Boveri** (1903). The key observations that led to its formulation were: 1. **Diploidy**: Chromosomes, like Mendelian factors, occur in pairs in somatic cells — one maternal, one paternal (homologous chromosomes). 2. **Reduction in meiosis**: The chromosome number is halved during gamete formation, paralleling the segregation of alleles. 3. **Random orientation**: Non-homologous chromosome pairs orient independently at Metaphase I, paralleling independent assortment. 4. **Restoration in fertilization**: Fertilization restores the diploid number, paralleling the reappearance of paired alleles in the zygote. This theory unified Mendelian genetics with cytology and was later confirmed experimentally by Morgan (1910) using *Drosophila melanogaster* and definitively proved by Creighton and McClintock (1931) using maize. **[3 Marks Allocation: Definition 1m + Names 0.5m + Four observations 1m + Validation 0.5m]** --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: What is crossing over? Explain its cytological basis and significance in genetic mapping.** **Model Answer:** **Crossing over** is the physical exchange of segments between **non-sister chromatids** of homologous chromosomes during the **Pachytene stage of Prophase I** of meiosis. **Cytological Basis:** - During Prophase I, homologous chromosomes pair (synapsis) to form bivalents. - Non-sister chromatids come into physical contact at points called **chiasmata** (singular: chiasma). - At each chiasma, a break and rejoin event occurs, swapping distal segments between the chromatids. **Significance in Genetic Mapping:** 1. Crossing over produces **recombinant gametes** with new combinations of alleles. 2. The **frequency of crossing over** between two genes is proportional to their **physical distance** apart on the chromosome. 3. Recombination frequencies can be used to construct **genetic maps** (linkage maps), where 1% recombination = 1 map unit (centiMorgan). > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse crossing over (which occurs between **non-sister chromatids** of homologous chromosomes) with the **sister chromatid exchange (SCE)** of mitotic cells. SCE does not produce genetic recombination because sister chromatids are genetically identical. --- ## 5.2 PART B: Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — (14 Marks) **Q: (a)** Explain in detail the **Sutton-Boveri Chromosomal Theory of Inheritance**. Discuss the four key parallelisms between chromosome behavior during meiosis and Mendel's laws. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** What is **linkage**? Describe Morgan's experiments on **Drosophila melanogaster** that established linkage and led to the chromosomal theory of linkage. How did this theory modify Mendel's Law of Independent Assortment? **(7 Marks)** ### Model Answer — Part A (a) [7 Marks] **Introduction:** The Chromosomal Theory of Inheritance was proposed by **Walter Sutton** (1902) and **Theodor Boveri** (1903). It bridges classical Mendelian genetics with the observable physical behavior of chromosomes during cell division. **The Four Parallelisms:** **1. Chromosomes occur in pairs (Diploidy ↔ Allelic Pairs):** Somatic cells of diploid organisms contain two homologous chromosomes for each autosomal set — one from each parent. This parallels Mendel's concept that organisms carry two alleles per gene. **2. Chromosome reduction in gametogenesis (↔ Law of Segregation):** During **Anaphase I** of meiosis, homologous chromosomes separate into different daughter cells. Each gamete receives only one chromosome from each pair, and therefore only one allele per gene. This physical separation is the **cytological basis of Mendel's Law of Segregation**. **3. Independent orientation at Metaphase I (↔ Law of Independent Assortment):** At Metaphase I, each bivalent orients independently at the spindle equator. The maternal/paternal orientation is random, yielding $2^{n}$ possible gamete types for n heterozygous gene pairs. **4. Restoration of diploidy at fertilization:** Fertilization of two haploid gametes restores the diploid state and the paired allele condition, explaining the continuity of inheritance. **Conclusion:** Sutton and Boveri established that Mendel's "factors" have a physical existence as chromosome-borne entities. This theory was later validated by Morgan, Creighton, and McClintock. ### Model Answer — Part A (b) [7 Marks] **Definition of Linkage:** Linkage is the tendency of two or more genes located on the **same chromosome** to be inherited together because they are physically close and thus less likely to be separated by crossing over. **Morgan's Experiments (1910 onwards):** Thomas Hunt Morgan worked with the fruit fly *Drosophila melanogaster*, which has only **4 pairs of chromosomes** and a short generation time (10-14 days). Key findings: - White-eyed mutant (*w*) appeared spontaneously and was **sex-linked** to the X chromosome — the first gene assigned to a specific chromosome. - Genes for body color (*b*) and wing shape (*vg*) also showed **non-independent assortment** (deviation from 9:3:3:1 in F₂). - Morgan proposed that genes are arranged in **linear order** on chromosomes. - Genes close together on the same chromosome are **linked**; genes far apart assort independently due to frequent crossing over. **Modification of Mendel's Law of Independent Assortment:** Mendel's Law of Independent Assortment holds strictly only for genes on **different chromosomes** or genes **far apart** on the same chromosome. For genes on the same chromosome, **linkage restricts independent assortment**, producing modified phenotypic ratios in test crosses (e.g., 7:1:1:7 instead of 1:1:1:1). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:** > - Students frequently lose marks by stating that "Linkage disproves Mendel." This is incorrect. **Linkage refines Mendel's Law** by adding the condition of chromosomal proximity; it does not negate the law. > - In Part (a), ensure all **four parallelisms** are listed explicitly with phase names — examiners allocate 1.5 marks per parallelism. > - In Part (b), **name the mutant genes** (white eye, yellow body, etc.) — generic answers receive partial credit only. --- ### Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — (14 Marks, Alternative) **Q: (a)** Describe the **cytological proof of crossing over** provided by **Creighton and McClintock** in 1931. Why is this experiment considered the definitive validation of the Chromosomal Theory of Inheritance? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A three-point test cross in *Drosophila* involving genes A, B, and C produced the following progeny: | Class | Phenotype | Number | |---|---|---| | 1 | ABC | 430 | | 2 | abc | 425 | | 3 | Abc | 32 | | 4 | aBC | 34 | | 5 | ABc | 38 | | 6 | abC | 41 | | 7 | AbC | 82 | | 8 | aBc | 78 | Determine (i) the gene order, (ii) the map distances, (iii) the coefficient of coincidence, and (iv) interference. **(7 Marks)** ### Model Answer — Part B (a) [7 Marks] **Background:** Before 1931, the Chromosomal Theory was inferred from indirect evidence. The definitive cytological proof came from **Harriet Creighton** and **Barbara McClintock** working at Cornell University with **maize (Zea mays)**. **The Experimental Design:** They used a maize plant heterozygous for two chromosome 9 markers: 1. **Cytological marker**: One chromosome 9 had a **terminal knob** (visible under microscope), and its homolog had **no knob**. 2. **Genetic marker**: The same chromosome pair carried alleles for **aleurone color** (*C* = colored, *c* = colorless). The plant's genotype was therefore: $$\text{Knobby chromosome: } C \quad | \quad \text{Normal chromosome: } c$$ **The Experiment:** - The plant was test-crossed to a homozygous recessive (*cc*, knobless) plant. - Progeny were scored for **color phenotype** (genetic marker) and **knob presence** (cytological marker). - If crossing over occurred **between the knob and the C locus**, the chromatids exchanged segments — producing progeny where **color and knob were separated** in new combinations. **The Result:** - In some progeny, the **colored allele was associated with the knobless chromosome**, and in others, the **colorless allele was associated with the knobby chromosome**. - This proved that **crossing over involves a physical exchange of chromosome segments**, not a hypothetical gene-to-gene interaction. - Every crossover event between the markers produced both genetic recombination and cytological exchange — a perfect 1:1 correlation. **Why This Was Definitive:** - It was the **first direct cytological observation** linking a visible chromosome change with a Mendelian recombination event. - It eliminated all alternative hypotheses (e.g., "gene conversion" or "bacterial transformation"-like phenomena). - It established that the **chromosome is the physical vehicle of heredity** — a foundational principle of modern cytogenetics. ### Model Answer — Part B (b) [7 Marks] **Given Data:** | Class | Phenotype | Number | Type | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | ABC | 430 | Parental | | 2 | abc | 425 | Parental | | 3 | Abc | 32 | DCO | | 4 | aBC | 34 | DCO | | 5 | ABc | 38 | SCO region II | | 6 | abC | 41 | SCO region II | | 7 | AbC | 82 | SCO region I | | 8 | aBc | 78 | SCO region I | **Step 1: Identify Gene Order (1 Mark)** Parental classes (most numerous): ABC / abc DCO classes (least numerous): Abc / aBC Comparing parental to DCO: - A → A (unchanged) - B → b (flipped) ← **Middle gene** - C → C (unchanged) $$\text{Gene order: } A - B - C$$ **Step 2: Calculate Total Progeny (0.5 Mark)** $$N_{\text{total}} = 430 + 425 + 32 + 34 + 38 + 41 + 82 + 78 = 1160$$ **Step 3: Map Distance A–B (Region I) (2 Marks)** SCO region I + DCO = 82 + 78 + 32 + 34 = 226 $$\text{RF}_{A-B} = \dfrac{226}{1160} \times 100 = 19.48 \text{ cM}$$ **Step 4: Map Distance B–C (Region II) (2 Marks)** SCO region II + DCO = 38 + 41 + 32 + 34 = 145 $$\text{RF}_{B-C} = \dfrac{145}{1160} \times 100 = 12.50 \text{ cM}$$ **Step 5: Coefficient of Coincidence (1 Mark)** $$\text{Expected DCO} = \text{RF}_{A-B} \times \text{RF}_{B-C} \times N_{\text{total}}$$ $$\text{Expected DCO} = 0.1948 \times 0.1250 \times 1160 = 28.25$$ $$\text{Observed DCO} = 32 + 34 = 66$$ $$\text{CoC} = \dfrac{66}{28.25} = 2.34$$ **Step 6: Interference (0.5 Mark)** $$I = 1 - \text{CoC} = 1 - 2.34 = -1.34$$ A **negative interference** (CoC > 1) is biologically interpreted as **negative chromatid interference**, meaning crossovers in adjacent regions **enhance** rather than suppress each other. While rare, it has been observed in *Drosophila* and certain fungal species. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU Examiner's Warning):** > 1. **Wrong gene order identification** (2 marks lost) — Students often pick the most numerous class as the parental without checking. Always compare DCO to parental to find the flipped allele. > 2. **Forgetting to add DCO to SCO counts** for map distance (1 mark lost) — DCO events represent crossovers in BOTH regions; they must be counted in both. > 3. **Incorrect CoC sign** — Some students report CoC > 1 as an error. In reality, it indicates negative interference; report it as is. > 4. **Mixing up interference formula** — Use $I = 1 - \text{CoC}$, not $I = \text{CoC} - 1$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Summary — Print This Section for Last-Minute Revision** ### Core Concepts to Memorize - **Chromosomal Theory of Inheritance** — Proposed by Sutton (1902) and Boveri (1903); states genes are on chromosomes. - **Four Parallelisms**: 1. Diploidy ↔ allelic pairs 2. Anaphase I ↔ Law of Segregation 3. Metaphase I random orientation ↔ Independent Assortment 4. Fertilization ↔ Restoration of diploidy - **Meiosis I is reductional** (homologs separate); **Meiosis II is equational** (sister chromatids separate). - **Crossing over** occurs in **Pachytene** of Prophase I; visible as **chiasmata** in Diplotene. - **Creighton and McClintock (1931)** — Definitive cytological proof using maize chromosome 9 (knob marker + C/c gene). ### Key Formulas (Must Know by Heart) - Gamete types: $2^{n}$ - Recombination Frequency: $\dfrac{\text{Recombinants}}{\text{Total}} \times 100$ - Map Distance (cM) = RF (%) - Coefficient of Coincidence: $\dfrac{\text{Observed DCO}}{\text{Expected DCO}}$ - Interference: $I = 1 - \text{CoC}$ ### Standard Cross Ratios - Monohybrid: 3:1 (phenotypic), 1:2:1 (genotypic) - Dihybrid (independent): 9:3:3:1, 1:2:1:2:4:1:2:1:1 - Test cross (independent): 1:1:1:1 - Test cross (linked): Two parental classes (most numerous) + two recombinant classes (least numerous) ### Historical Milestones - **1866**: Mendel's laws published - **1902–1903**: Sutton-Boveri hypothesis - **1910**: Morgan's white-eyed Drosophila discovery (sex linkage) - **1931**: Creighton-McClintock cytological proof - **1953**: Watson-Crick DNA structure (extension of the chromosomal theory to molecular level) ### Frequently Confused Terms - **Homologous chromosomes** vs. **Sister chromatids** - **Synapsis** vs. **Crossing over** - **Linkage** vs. **Independent Assortment** - **Recombination frequency** vs. **Mutation rate** - **Centimorgan (cM)** vs. **Megabase (Mb)**

topics.title: Chromosomal rearrangements associated with Mendelian disorders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBB861 - CYTOGENETICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Chromosomal rearrangements associated with Mendelian disorders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chromosomal Rearrangements (Structural Aberrations)** are heritable or *de novo* modifications in the normal chromosome architecture that result from **breakage and reunion** of DNA segments. They include **deletions, duplications, inversions, translocations, ring chromosomes, and isochromosomes**, and are cytogenetically identified through banding patterns and molecular cytogenetic techniques (FISH, CGH, SNP arrays).

A **Mendelian disorder**, in classical cytogenetic parlance, is a phenotype produced by alteration of a single gene locus (monogenic) or a contiguous gene segment whose transmission follows Mendelian principles. However, when chromosomal rearrangements disrupt such loci — or remove a contiguous stretch containing many genes — the resulting disorder may present as a **contiguous gene syndrome (CGS)**, bridging the classical Mendelian and chromosomal domains.

### Common Mendelian/CGS disorders with chromosomal rearrangements

| Syndrome | Rearrangement | Locus |
|----------|---------------|-------|
| Cri-du-Chat | Terminal deletion | **5p15.3** |
| Wolf–Hirschhorn | Terminal deletion | **4p16.3** |
| Prader–Willi | Deletion of paternal 15q | **15q11-q13** |
| Angelman | Deletion of maternal 15q | **15q11-q13** |
| Williams–Beuren | Microdeletion | **7q11.23** |
| DiGeorge / Velocardiofacial | Microdeletion | **22q11.2** |
| Smith–Magenis | Microdeletion | **17p11.2** |
| Miller–Dieker | Microdeletion | **17p13.3** |
| Philadelphia CML | Reciprocal translocation | **t(9;22)(q34;q11)** |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Library of Encyclopedias**

Imagine a library of **46 encyclopedias** (chromosomes) lined up in a specific order on two giant shelves (the 22 autosomal pairs + XX or XY). Each encyclopedia contains thousands of "chapters" (genes) that tell the body how to build proteins. Now imagine the librarian is a little clumsy:

- **Deletion** → A whole chapter is *torn out* of one book. If a critical chapter is missing, the story (phenotype) is incomplete.
- **Duplication** → A chapter is *photocopied and pasted twice* — over-expression of that gene leads to a "loud" phenotype.
- **Inversion** → A chapter is *cut out, flipped, and glued back in* — the order of words is reversed, breaking the sense of the sentence.
- **Translocation** → A page from one book is *swapped with a page from another* — the books no longer contain the original text in the right place.
- **Ring Chromosome** → The two ends of a book are *glued together*, and the front/back covers lost.
- **Isochromosome** → The book is photocopied such that *two left-hand pages face each other*, mirroring the long arm.

Each of these accidents is a **chromosomal rearrangement** — and when the affected chapter corresponds to a Mendelian gene or a contiguous gene cluster, the resulting phenotype is a **Mendelian or contiguous-gene disorder**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key cytogenetic parameters (BOLD values are KTU-relevant constants):**
> - **Human diploid chromosome number: 2n = 46**
> - **Autosomes: 22 pairs (44 chromosomes)**
> - **Sex chromosomes: 1 pair (XX or XY)**
> - **Haploid genome size: ~3.2 × 10⁹ bp**
> - **Resolution of standard G-banding: ~5–10 Mb**
> - **Resolution of high-resolution banding: ~3–5 Mb**
> - **Resolution of FISH: ~100 kb – 1 Mb**
> - **Resolution of array-CGH: ~50–100 kb**
> - **Resolution of SNP array: ~10–50 kb**
> - **Resolution of optical mapping: ~1–5 kb**
> - **Standard nomenclature: ISCN 2020**

---

## 1.4 Classification of Chromosomal Rearrangements — Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Balanced vs Unbalanced Rearrangements**
> - **Balanced**: No net gain/loss of genetic material (e.g., reciprocal translocation, inversion). Usually phenotypically normal but may cause infertility or recurrent miscarriages.
> - **Unbalanced**: Net loss or gain of material → abnormal phenotype (e.g., deletions, duplications, derivative chromosomes).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Chromosomal Rearrangements — Operational Logic

### A. DELETIONS (Δ)

> **Mechanism**: A single chromosome breaks in **two places**, and the **interstitial segment is lost** (interstitial deletion), or a **single terminal break** with loss of the distal fragment (terminal deletion).

**Logic Steps:**
1. DNA double-strand break (DSB) occurs due to ionizing radiation, viral integration, or replication errors.
2. The broken segment lacks a centromere → lost in next cell division.
3. The remaining chromosome is *monocentric* but shortened.
4. If the deleted segment contains a Mendelian gene → **haploinsufficiency** (50% gene dose insufficient) → disease.

**Examples (Memorize for KTU):**
- **Cri-du-Chat** → 5p15.3 deletion → cry resembling cat meow, microcephaly.
- **Wolf–Hirschhorn** → 4p16.3 deletion → "Greek warrior helmet" facies, growth retardation.
- **1p36 deletion syndrome** → largest terminal deletion syndrome.

### B. DUPLICATIONS (dup)

> **Mechanism**: A segment is **replicated and inserted** into the same or different chromosome.

**Logic Steps:**
1. Unequal crossing-over during meiosis I (most common mechanism).
2. One chromatid gains extra copy; homolog loses it.
3. Three gene doses instead of two → **gene dosage effect**.

**Examples:**
- **Charcot–Marie–Tooth 1A (CMT1A)** → duplication of **PMP22** at **17p12** (a Mendelian neuropathy with chromosomal duplication mechanism).
- **Beckwith–Wiedemann** → 11p15.5 duplication (paternal allele).

### C. INVERSIONS (inv)

> **Mechanism**: A segment is **excised, rotated 180°, and reinserted**.

**Two sub-types:**
- **Paracentric inversion**: Both breaks on **same arm**; centromere NOT included.
- **Pericentric inversion**: Breaks on **opposite arms**; centromere IS included.

**Logic Steps:**
1. Inversion does not change gene content → usually phenotypically normal.
2. **Problem arises at meiosis**: the inverted chromosome must form an **inversion loop** to pair with its normal homolog.
3. **Paracentric**: results in **dicentric + acentric** fragments → inviable gametes.
4. **Pericentric**: results in **partial trisomy + partial monosomy** gametes.

**KTU Pearl:** *Pericentric inversion of chromosome 9 — inv(9)(p11q13) — is the most common human inversion, often a benign variant.*

### D. TRANSLOCATIONS (t)

> **Mechanism**: Exchange of segments between **non-homologous chromosomes**.

**Sub-types:**
- **Reciprocal (balanced)**: t(A;B) — segments exchanged, no net loss/gain.
- **Robertsonian (centric fusion)**: Two **acrocentric** chromosomes (13, 14, 15, 21, 22) fuse at centromere.
- **Insertional**: Segment of one chromosome inserted into another.

**Robertsonian Logic:**
1. Break occurs at centromere of both acrocentrics.
2. Long arms fuse; short arms (containing rRNA genes only) are lost.
3. Effective chromosome count = **45** (one less pair).
4. Carriers are phenotypically normal but produce **unbalanced gametes**.

**Examples:**
- **Philadelphia chromosome** → t(9;22)(q34;q11) → **BCR-ABL fusion** → CML (a Mendelian-like cancer predisposition at somatic level).
- **Down syndrome (translocation form)** → der(14;21) or der(21;21) Robertsonian translocation, ~4% of Down cases.

### E. RING CHROMOSOMES (r)

> **Mechanism**: **Both telomeres** of a chromosome are lost; sticky ends fuse to form a ring.

- Often **unstable** in mitosis → breakage-fusion-bridge cycles.
- Example: **r(20) syndrome** — refractory epilepsy.

### F. ISOCHROMOSOMES (i)

> **Mechanism**: Centromere divides **transversely** (instead of longitudinally) during mitosis.
- One daughter cell receives **two copies of p-arm**; the other receives **two copies of q-arm**.
- **i(Xq)** is a classic cause of **Turner syndrome** variant.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Table for Numerical / Banding Problems**

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Haploid number | $n = 23$ | Human |
| Diploid number | $2n = 46$ | Human |
| Gamete chromosome set | $n$ | Haploid |
| Zygote restoration | $n + n = 2n$ | Fertilization |
| Aneuploidy | $2n \pm 1$ | e.g., 45,X; 47,XXY |
| Polyploidy | $3n,\ 4n$ | Triploid / Tetraploid |
| Centromere position | $r = \dfrac{p}{p+q}$ | $r < 0.33$ acrocentric |
| Robertsonian effective count | $2n - 1 = 45$ | Each fusion |
| Deletion syndrome | $\text{Phenotype} = f(\text{deleted genes})$ | Contiguous |
| Duplication gene dose | $1.5\times$ normal | Trisomic segment |
| Pericentric inversion carrier | $2n$ (balanced) | Normal phenotype |
| FISH resolution | $100 \text{ kb} - 1 \text{ Mb}$ | Probe based |
| aCGH resolution | $\sim 50-100 \text{ kb}$ | Oligo-based |
| SNP array resolution | $\sim 10-50 \text{ kb}$ | Allele-aware |
| Karyotype band count | $300-850$ bands | Stage dependent |
| Average gene density | $\sim 1 \text{ gene} / 100 \text{ kb}$ | Genome-wide avg |
| MITOTIC INDEX | $\text{MI} = \dfrac{\text{Dividing cells}}{\text{Total cells}}$ | Diagnostic cytogenetics |
| SCE frequency | $\text{SCE}/\text{cell} = 5-10$ | Sister Chromatid Exchange |

**Critical note on table syntax:** All conditional or absolute bars have been replaced with `\vert` (e.g., $r < 0.33$) to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering / Biotechnology

> [!NOTE]
> Chromosomal rearrangement analysis is foundational in:
> - **Cancer diagnostics** — karyotyping & FISH for BCR-ABL, HER2, MYC translocations.
> - **Preimplantation Genetic Testing (PGT-SR)** for structural rearrangements in IVF.
> - **Recurrent pregnancy loss** workup — 2–5% of couples carry a balanced rearrangement.
> - **CRISPR/Cas9 genome editing QC** — off-target rearrangements detected by FISH/aCGH.
> - **Forensic cytogenetics** — chromosomal polymorphisms in identity testing.
> - **Agricultural biotechnology** — induced translocations in crop breeding programs (e.g., *Oryza sativa*, *Triticum aestivum*).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Numerical Derivation: Robertsonian Translocation Carrier Offspring Ratios

> **Problem:** A phenotypically normal human carries a Robertsonian translocation between chromosomes **14 and 21** (der(14;21)). Their partner has a **normal 46,XX or 46,XY** karyotype. Determine the **theoretical gamete ratio** and the **karyotype distribution** among liveborn offspring.

### Step 1: Identify the chromosomes in the translocation
A Robertsonian carrier of 14;21 fusion has effectively:
- 1 normal chromosome **14** ($N_{14}$)
- 1 normal chromosome **21** ($N_{21}$)
- 1 derivative chromosome **der(14;21)** carrying q-arms of both ($D$)
- Total chromosome count = **45**

### Step 2: List possible gametes

The carrier produces **6 types of gametes** through meiotic segregation (3:1 segregation pattern for Robertsonian trivalents).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gamete 1:}&\ N_{14} + N_{21} \quad &\text{(Normal)} \\
\text{Gamete 2:}&\ D \quad &\text{(Balanced carrier)} \\
\text{Gamete 3:}&\ N_{14} + D \quad &\text{(Trisomy 14, lethal)} \\
\text{Gamete 4:}&\ N_{21} + D \quad &\text{(Trisomy 21 → Down syndrome)} \\
\text{Gamete 5:}&\ N_{14} \quad &\text{(Monosomy 14, lethal)} \\
\text{Gamete 6:}&\ N_{21} \quad &\text{(Monosomy 21, lethal)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute fertilization outcomes (with normal partner gamete $n$)

Fertilizing each gamete with a normal $n$ (containing $N_{14}$ and $N_{21}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Offspring 1:}&\ 46,\ N_{14}N_{14}N_{21}N_{21} \quad &\text{Normal} \\
\text{Offspring 2:}&\ 45,\ N_{14}N_{21}D \quad &\text{Balanced carrier} \\
\text{Offspring 3:}&\ 46,\ N_{14}N_{14}N_{21}D \quad &\text{Trisomy 14 — non-viable} \\
\text{Offspring 4:}&\ 46,\ N_{14}N_{21}N_{21}D \quad &\text{Down syndrome (translocation type)} \\
\text{Offspring 5:}&\ 45,\ N_{14}D \quad &\text{Monosomy 14 — non-viable} \\
\text{Offspring 6:}&\ 45,\ N_{21}D \quad &\text{Monosomy 21 — non-viable} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Liveborn distribution (assuming all unbalanced lethal ones die *in utero*)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Normal} &= \frac{1}{3} \\
\text{Balanced carrier} &= \frac{1}{3} \\
\text{Down syndrome (translocation)} &= \frac{1}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Therefore, the recurrence risk of translocation-Down syndrome from a der(14;21) carrier parent is theoretically $\frac{1}{3} \approx 33.3\%$**.
> In practice, empirical risk is **10–15%** for maternal carriers and **2–5%** for paternal carriers (due to gametic selection).

---

## 3.3 Contiguous Gene Syndrome — Mathematical Reasoning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does deletion of multiple genes cause a Mendelian-like phenotype?**

A **contiguous gene syndrome (CGS)** arises when a deletion removes **multiple neighboring genes** that individually cause **separate** Mendelian phenotypes. The resulting combined phenotype is a **sum of the constituent sub-phenotypes**.

Let:
- $G_1, G_2, G_3, \ldots, G_n$ be the deleted genes.
- $P_1, P_2, P_3, \ldots, P_n$ be their respective single-gene phenotypes.

Then the **CGS phenotype** is:

$$
P_{\text{CGS}} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} P_i
$$

where $\cup$ denotes the **phenotypic union** (combined clinical features).

### Example: Williams Syndrome (7q11.23 deletion)
- *ELN* (elastin) deletion → **Supravalvular Aortic Stenosis (SVAS)** — a Mendelian AD disorder.
- *LIMK1* deletion → **visuospatial cognitive deficit**.
- *GTF2I* deletion → **hyper-sociability**.
- *STX1A* deletion → **mild intellectual disability**.

$$
P_{\text{Williams}} = P_{\text{SVAS}} \cup P_{\text{cognition}} \cup P_{\text{behavior}} \cup P_{\text{CNS}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip**: A pure single-gene Mendelian disorder involves **one** locus; a CGS involves **multiple** co-deleted loci but is **often inherited in a Mendelian (typically AD) fashion** because the deletion itself is a single event transmitted as a unit.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Karyotype Interpretation (Mock KTU Question)

**Given karyotype string:** `46,XX,del(5)(p15.3)` — interpret and answer sub-questions.

**Step 1**: Total chromosome count = 46 (euploid).
**Step 2**: Sex chromosomes = XX (female).
**Step 3**: Rearrangement = del(5)(p15.3) — terminal deletion on the **short arm of chromosome 5** at **band 5p15.3**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Conclude:}&\ \text{Cri-du-Chat syndrome} \\
\text{Sex:}&\ \text{Female} \\
\text{Prognosis:}&\ \text{Severe intellectual disability, microcephaly, high-pitched cry}
\end{aligned}
$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, PEBB861, Module 2, Q1.a]**

**Q1. (3 marks)**
Define a **microdeletion syndrome**. Name two examples and the chromosomal loci involved.

**Model Answer:**

A **microdeletion syndrome** is a contiguous gene disorder caused by a submicroscopic interstitial deletion (typically **<5 Mb**) of a chromosome segment that is undetectable by standard G-banding but identifiable by FISH or array-CGH. The deletion removes multiple closely linked genes, producing a recognizable constellation of clinical features that follows Mendelian (usually autosomal dominant) inheritance.

> **Examples:**
> 1. **Williams–Beuren syndrome** — deletion at **7q11.23** (genes *ELN*, *LIMK1*, *GTF2I*).
> 2. **DiGeorge / Velocardiofacial syndrome (CATCH-22)** — deletion at **22q11.2** (genes *TBX1*, *COMT*).
>
> *Equivalently acceptable: Prader–Willi (15q11-q13), Smith–Magenis (17p11.2).*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEBB861, Module 2, Q1.b]**

**Q2. (3 marks)**
Differentiate between **paracentric** and **pericentric** inversions with one cytogenetic example of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Paracentric | Pericentric |
|---|---|---|
| Break points | **Same arm** of chromosome | **Opposite arms** (centromere within inversion) |
| Centromere in inverted segment | **No** | **Yes** |
| Meiotic products | Dicentric + acentric fragments (inviable) | Recombinant chromosomes (partial trisomy/monosomy) |
| Effect on chromosome shape | **No** change in arm ratio | **Change** in arm ratio (c-band position alters) |
| Example | inv(3)(q21q26) | inv(9)(p11q13) — most common human variant |

*Diagrams not required for 3-mark answers but a sketch gains bonus credit.*
