## 5.3 Model Solutions to Part B — Question A

### (a) Solution — Classification + Robertsonian Translocation (7 marks)

**Classification of Chromosomal Rearrangements (3 marks):**

| Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Deletion (del)** | Loss of a chromosome segment | del(5p) — Cri-du-Chat |
| **Duplication (dup)** | Gain of a segment; 3 gene doses | dup(17p12) — CMT1A |
| **Inversion (inv)** | Segment reversed 180° in situ | inv(9)(p11q13) |
| **Reciprocal translocation (t)** | Exchange of segments between non-homologs | t(9;22) — Philadelphia |
| **Robertsonian translocation (rob)** | Fusion of two acrocentrics at centromere | rob(14;21) |
| **Ring chromosome (r)** | Both ends joined, telomeres lost | r(20) syndrome |
| **Isochromosome (i)** | Centromere misdivision → mirror arms | i(Xq) — Turner variant |

**Robertsonian Translocation — Mechanism (2 marks):**
1. Occurs in **acrocentric chromosomes** (13, 14, 15, 21, 22), whose short arms contain only rRNA genes.
2. Breakage at or near centromere in **two** acrocentric chromosomes.
3. **Long arms fuse** at the centromere; **short arms are lost** (functionally inconsequential, multiple rRNA copies elsewhere).
4. Net chromosome number = **45**; carrier is phenotypically normal.

**Meiotic Consequence (1 mark):**
- Forms a **trivalent** at meiosis I.
- Produces 6 gamete types; 3 lead to **trisomy or monosomy** in zygote (most non-viable).
- Liveborn outcomes: Normal : Balanced carrier : Trisomy-affected = **1 : 1 : 1**.

**Clinical Significance (1 mark):**
- **Translocation Down syndrome** (~4% of all Down cases) — familial recurrence.
- **Recurrent miscarriages** in carrier couples.
- Important in **genetic counseling** and **prenatal diagnosis**.

---

### (b) Solution — Recurrence Risk Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Identify paternal karyotype (1 mark)**
- Father = 45,XY,der(14;21)(q10;q10) — Robertsonian carrier.
- He has: 1 normal **14**, 1 normal **21**, and 1 derivative **der(14;21)**.

**Step 2: Possible gametes (2 marks)**
- G1: Normal 14 + Normal 21 → balanced
- G2: der(14;21) only → balanced carrier
- G3: Normal 14 + der(14;21) → Trisomy 14 (lethal)
- G4: Normal 21 + der(14;21) → Trisomy 21 (Down)
- G5: Normal 14 alone → Monosomy 14 (lethal)
- G6: Normal 21 alone → Monosomy 21 (lethal)

**Step 3: Fertilization with normal 23,X or 23,Y maternal gamete (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Offspring} = &\ 46,\text{XX or XY} \quad &\text{Normal} \\
& 45,\text{XX or XY, der(14;21)} \quad &\text{Balanced carrier} \\
& 46,\text{XX or XY, der(14;21), +21} \quad &\text{Translocation Down syndrome}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate the theoretical recurrence risk (1 mark)**

$$
P(\text{Down}) = \frac{1}{3} \approx 33.3\%
$$

**Step 5: Empirical correction (1 mark)**
- In practice, empirical recurrence risk is **10–15%** for **maternal** carriers and **2–5%** for **paternal** carriers (since the father in this question is the carrier, the risk is ~2–5%).
- This is due to **meiotic drive** and selective loss of trisomic embryos.

**Final answer:** The theoretical recurrence risk is **33.3%**; the **empirical (real-world) risk** is approximately **2–5%** for a paternal der(14;21) carrier.

---

## 5.4 Model Solutions to Part B — Question B (Alternative)

### (a) Solution — Contiguous Gene Syndromes (7 marks)

**Concept (2 marks):** A contiguous gene syndrome (CGS) is a clinical disorder produced by **deletion or duplication of a chromosomal segment containing multiple functionally unrelated genes**, leading to a composite phenotype. The inheritance is **Mendelian (typically autosomal dominant)**, but the disorder bridges Mendelian and chromosomal genetics.

**Williams Syndrome (7q11.23 deletion) (2.5 marks):**
- Critical deleted region: **~1.5 Mb** containing ~26 genes.
- *ELN* (elastin) → **supravalvular aortic stenosis (SVAS)**, hoarse voice.
- *LIMK1* → **visuospatial cognitive deficits**.
- *GTF2I* → **hypersociability, "cocktail personality"**.
- *STX1A* → mild intellectual disability.
- **Diagnosis**: FISH with *ELN* probe; confirmed by **aCGH** for size estimation.

**DiGeorge Syndrome (22q11.2 deletion) (2.5 marks):**
- Critical region: **3 Mb** (LCR-A to LCR-D) or **1.5 Mb** (LCR-A to LCR-B).
- *TBX1* → **conotruncal cardiac defects** (tetralogy of Fallot, truncus arteriosus).
- *COMT* → behavioral phenotypes (schizophrenia risk).
- *DGCR8* → microRNA processing deficit.
- **CATCH-22** mnemonic: **C**ardiac, **A**bnormal facies, **T**hymic aplasia, **C**left palate, **H**ypocalcemia, **22q11.2**.

### (b) Solution — Philadelphia Chromosome (7 marks)

**Origin (2 marks):**
- The **Philadelphia (Ph) chromosome** is a **derivative chromosome 22** resulting from a **reciprocal translocation t(9;22)(q34;q11)**.
- Discovered in **1960** by Peter Nowell and David Hungerford in CML patients.

**BCR-ABL Fusion Transcript (2.5 marks):**
- Break at **9q34** in *ABL1* gene; break at **22q11** in *BCR* gene.
- Fusion of *BCR* exons 1-13 with *ABL1* exons 2-11.
- Produces a **210 kDa chimeric protein (p210)** in CML (and p190 in Ph⁺ ALL).
- The fusion protein has **constitutive tyrosine kinase activity** → drives uncontrolled myeloid proliferation.

**Diagnostic Techniques (2 marks):**
- **Karyotyping** — visualizes t(9;22).
- **FISH** — dual-fusion probe for *BCR* and *ABL1*.
- **RT-PCR / qPCR** — detects BCR-ABL transcript (used for monitoring MRD).
- **Targeted therapy**: **Imatinib (Gleevec)** — tyrosine kinase inhibitor.

**Mendelian-like designation (0.5 mark):**
Although the rearrangement is **somatic** (acquired), the resulting clonal proliferation follows a **dominant** pattern at the cellular level, hence its "Mendelian-like" somatic behavior.

---

## 5.5 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "Cri-du-Chat" with "Cat-eye syndrome"** — Cat-eye is **idic(22)(q11)**. Cri-du-chat is **del(5p)**.
> 2. **Misidentifying translocation Down syndrome recurrence risk** — 33.3% theoretical ≠ 10–15% empirical. Examiners expect both numbers.
> 3. **Forgetting ISCN 2020 nomenclature** — always use `del(5)(p15.3)`, not "5p-". Wrong notation = −1 mark.
> 4. **Conflating balanced Robertsonian with reciprocal translocation** — Robertsonian is acrocentric-only; reciprocal is general.
> 5. **Missing the "Mendelian-contiguous" bridge concept** — Williams & DiGeorge are often mis-categorized as "pure chromosomal" when they are technically **AD contiguous-gene syndromes**.
> 6. **Wrong centromere position for inv(9)** — correct notation is **inv(9)(p11q13)**, *not* inv(9)(p11.1q13.1).
> 7. **Failing to mention CATCH-22 mnemonic** for DiGeorge — KTU examiners love mnemonics.
> 8. **Omitting array-CGH resolution** — quoting outdated 5 Mb banding resolution loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

### ✔ Key Definitions
- **Chromosomal rearrangement** = breakage + reunion altering normal architecture.
- **Microdeletion** = submicroscopic deletion <5 Mb.
- **CGS (Contiguous Gene Syndrome)** = deletion of multiple adjacent genes.
- **Robertsonian translocation** = fusion of two acrocentric chromosomes.
- **Reciprocal translocation** = mutual exchange of segments.
- **Paracentric inversion** = same arm; **Pericentric** = opposite arm.
- **Ring chromosome** = fused telomeres.
- **Isochromosome** = mirror duplication of one arm.

### ✔ Numerical Constants to Memorize
- Human **2n = 46**; n = 23.
- Resolution: G-banding **5–10 Mb**, FISH **100 kb – 1 Mb**, aCGH **50–100 kb**, SNP array **10–50 kb**.
- Robertsonian carrier: **2n − 1 = 45** chromosomes.
- der(14;21) recurrence risk (theoretical) = **1/3**.
- Ph chromosome translocation = **t(9;22)(q34;q11)**.

### ✔ "Must-Memorize" Disorders
1. **5p15.3 del** → Cri-du-Chat
2. **4p16.3 del** → Wolf–Hirschhorn
3. **7q11.23 del** → Williams
4. **15q11-q13 pat del** → Prader–Willi
5. **15q11-q13 mat del** → Angelman
6. **17p11.2 del** → Smith–Magenis
7. **17p13.3 del** → Miller–Dieker
8. **22q11.2 del** → DiGeorge (CATCH-22)
9. **t(9;22)** → Philadelphia / CML
10. **rob(14;21)** → Translocation Down syndrome
11. **17p12 dup** → CMT1A
12. **i(Xq)** → Isochromosome Turner

### ✔ Critical Mnemonics
- **CATCH-22** = DiGeorge features + 22q11.2.
- **Wolf–Hirschhorn** = "Greek warrior helmet" face.
- **Cri-du-Chat** = cry of cat.
- **Patau** = trisomy 13; **Edwards** = trisomy 18; **Down** = trisomy 21.

### ✔ ISCN Notation Rules
- Always specify chromosome number, region, band, sub-band.
- Examples: `46,XX,del(5)(p15.3)` or `46,XY,der(14;21)(q10;q10)` or `45,XX,rob(14;21)(q10;q10)`.
- Use **"+"** or **"-"** before chromosome for whole-chromosome aneuploidy: `47,XX,+21`.

### ✔ Diagnostic Hierarchy
1. **G-banding (5–10 Mb)** → first-line.
2. **High-resolution banding (3–5 Mb)** → second-line.
3. **FISH (100 kb–1 Mb)** → targeted confirmation.
4. **aCGH / SNP array (10–100 kb)** → for microdeletions/duplications.
5. **Optical mapping / WGS** → research / complex cases.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Eukaryotic chromosomes, telomeres, and extrachromosomal replicons
Linkage, crossing over, and chromosome mapping in eukaryotes
Autosomal inheritance and chromosome rearrangements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBB861 - CYTOGENETICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Autosomal inheritance and chromosome rearrangements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Autosomal Inheritance — Formal Definition

**Autosomal inheritance** refers to the pattern of transmission of genetic traits and disorders that are governed by genes located on the **autosomes** (chromosomes 1–22 in humans), as opposed to sex-linked inheritance governed by the X or Y chromosomes. Because every individual carries two copies (alleles) of every autosomal gene — one from each parent — these traits follow predictable Mendelian ratios, modified occasionally by phenomena such as **penetrance**, **expressivity**, **pleiotropy**, and **epistasis**.

Within autosomal inheritance, the two principal patterns studied in cytogenetics are:

- **Autosomal Dominant (AD) Inheritance** — A phenotype is expressed when only **one** of the two alleles carries the mutant/dominant variant (heterozygous state is sufficient).
- **Autosomal Recessive (AR) Inheritance** — A phenotype is expressed only when **both** alleles are mutated (homozygous or compound heterozygous state).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The 2024 scheme expects students to (a) draw and interpret 3-generation pedigrees, (b) compute Mendelian risk probabilities using Punnett squares and probability rules, and (c) recognize the characteristic pedigree signatures of dominant vs. recessive autosomal traits.

## 1.2 Chromosome Rearrangements — Formal Definition

**Chromosome rearrangements** are structural alterations of chromosomes resulting from **breakage and incorrect rejoining** of DNA segments. They arise spontaneously or are induced by ionizing radiation, chemical mutagens, transposable elements, or replication errors. Four classical categories exist:

| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Deletion (Del)** | Loss of a chromosome segment |
| **Duplication (Dup)** | Repetition of a chromosome segment |
| **Inversion (Inv)** | A segment is reversed end-to-end |
| **Translocation (T)** | Movement of a segment to a non-homologous chromosome |

Inversions are further subdivided into **paracentric** (centromere NOT included) and **pericentric** (centromere IS included). Translocations are subdivided into **reciprocal** (exchange between non-homologues) and **Robertsonian** (fusion at centromeres of two acrocentric chromosomes).

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Library Book Shelf" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Imagine each chromosome as a **library bookshelf** containing numbered "books" (genes) arranged in a strict order. Each cell carries **two copies** of every bookshelf (one from each parent).
>
> - **Autosomal Dominant** is like a *single torn page* in one copy of a book — even with the other copy intact, the tear is visible (phenotype appears).
> - **Autosomal Recessive** is like a *torn page in BOTH copies* of the book — only then does the reading get affected.
> - **Deletion** = pages ripped out of the shelf.
> - **Duplication** = the same pages accidentally bound twice.
> - **Inversion** = a chapter glued in upside-down.
> - **Translocation** = chapters swapped between two completely different bookshelves.
>
> This mental model makes it clear why *dose*, *position*, and *context* of genes matter in cytogenetics.

## 1.4 Pedigree Symbols — Universal Standard

Standard pedigree symbols used across KTU 2024 cytogenetics questions:

- **Square** = Male; **Circle** = Female
- **Filled** = Affected; **Half-filled (Carrier)** = Heterozygous carrier (typical of recessive traits)
- **Horizontal line** between square & circle = Mating
- **Vertical line** = Offspring
- **Double horizontal line** = Consanguineous mating
- **Slash through symbol** = Deceased
- **Roman numerals (I, II, III)** = Generation number
- **Arabic numerals (1, 2, 3)** = Individual number within a generation
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Autosomal Dominant (AD) Inheritance — Key Logic Steps

1. **The trait appears in every generation** (vertical transmission pattern) because each affected individual typically has at least one affected parent.
2. **Males and females are affected in roughly equal proportions** (autosomal → no sex bias).
3. **Male-to-male transmission IS observed** — this is the cardinal sign distinguishing AD from X-linked dominant.
4. **An affected heterozygous parent (Aa) crossed with a normal partner (aa) yields a 1:1 ratio** of affected to unaffected offspring.
5. **Two unaffected parents cannot produce an affected child** in a *pure* AD trait (rare exceptions exist via *de novo* mutations).
6. **Homozygous dominant (AA) individuals** are often more severely affected (e.g., homozygous achondroplasia is usually lethal).

> [!NOTE]
> **Reduced Penetrance Caveat:** A dominant allele may be present but not phenotypically expressed. KTU examiners frequently test this with the term **"incomplete penetrance."**

## 2.2 Autosomal Recessive (AR) Inheritance — Key Logic Steps

1. **The trait can skip generations** (horizontal transmission pattern).
2. **Males and females are affected equally.**
3. **Two unaffected (but heterozygous carrier) parents can produce affected offspring** — the classical Mendelian 3:1 ratio in F₂ or 1:2:1 genotypic.
4. **Consanguinity (mating between relatives) dramatically increases recurrence risk** because relatives are more likely to share the same rare recessive allele.
5. **Affected individuals, when mated with unaffected non-carriers, produce ALL carrier offspring (Aa × aa → 1 Aa : 0 aa).**
6. **Affected × affected (aa × aa) produces ALL affected offspring** — a hallmark signature.

## 2.3 Probability / Risk Calculation Framework (KTU High-Yield)

The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to compute **recurrence risk** for both AD and AR pedigrees. Use the following master logic:

### 2.3.1 For Autosomal Dominant Traits

- Affected heterozygous parent (Aa) × Normal (aa):
$$P(\text{affected child}) = \tfrac{1}{2} = 50\%$$
- Unaffected × Unaffected: $P(\text{affected}) = 0$ (excluding *de novo*)

### 2.3.2 For Autosomal Recessive Traits

- Two known carriers (Aa × Aa):
$$P(\text{affected child}) = \tfrac{1}{4} = 25\%$$
- Carrier × Affected (Aa × aa):
$$P(\text{affected child}) = \tfrac{1}{2} = 50\%$$
- Affected × Affected (aa × aa):
$$P(\text{affected child}) = 1 = 100\%$$

### 2.3.3 Conditional Probability (Bayesian Update)

If a known Aa × Aa couple already has **one unaffected child**, what is the probability that the *next* child is affected?

$$P(\text{affected} \mid \text{unaffected sibling}) = \frac{1/4}{3/4} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 33.3\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Warning:** Many students incorrectly answer "25%" again. Once an unaffected child is confirmed, the conditional probability of the next child being affected rises to **1/3**, NOT 1/4. This is a classic 2-mark differentiator in KTU answer keys.

## 2.4 Chromosome Rearrangements — Theoretical Framework

### 2.4.1 Deletions

- **Terminal deletion** — single break, segment distal to the break is lost.
- **Interstitial deletion** — two breaks, the internal segment is lost.
- **Ring chromosome** — two terminal deletions on opposite arms followed by fusion, forming a ring.
- **Cri-du-chat syndrome (5p−)** is the textbook example.

### 2.4.2 Duplications

- **Tandem duplication** — duplicated segment lies adjacent to the original.
- **Reverse tandem / Displaced duplication** — duplicated segment lies elsewhere in the same orientation or reverse orientation.
- Duplications are a major source of **evolutionary novelty** because extra gene copies can mutate into new functions.

### 2.4.3 Inversions

- **Paracentric inversion** — both breaks occur in **one arm**, centromere is **NOT** included.
- **Pericentric inversion** — breaks occur on **opposite sides** of the centromere, centromere **IS** included.
- Inversion carriers are usually phenotypically normal (no gain/loss of genetic material) but produce **inversion loop** structures at meiosis I, leading to characteristic gamete types.

### 2.4.4 Translocations

- **Reciprocal translocation (RT)** — exchange of segments between **non-homologous** chromosomes. Example: **t(9;22)** → Philadelphia chromosome → CML.
- **Robertsonian translocation (Rob)** — fusion of **two acrocentric** chromosomes (13, 14, 15, 21, 22) at the centromere, losing the short arms. Carriers have **45 chromosomes**.
- **Balanced** rearrangements have no net gain/loss of genetic material → phenotypically normal but produce unbalanced gametes.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| Phenomenon | Rule / Formula | Key Constant | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| AD Risk (Aa × aa) | $P = 0.5$ | — | Most-tested risk calculation |
| AR Risk (Aa × Aa) | $P = 0.25$ | — | Standard Mendelian ratio |
| Conditional risk after 1 unaffected child (AR) | $P = 1/3$ | — | KTU-favourite 2-mark trap |
| Affected × Affected AR | $P = 1.0$ | — | Signature of recessive pattern |
| Karyotype standard count | **46 chromosomes (diploid)** | 2n = 46 | Baseline for all rearrangements |
| Robertsonian carrier | **45 chromosomes** (balanced) | 2n − 1 = 45 | Trivalent at meiosis |
| Philadelphia chromosome | t(9;22)(q34;q11) | — | Classic translocation example |
| Cri-du-chat | 5p− | ~**5 Mb deleted** | Classic deletion example |
| Hardy–Weinberg (carrier frequency) | $2pq$ | $p^2 + 2pq + q^2 = 1$ | Population-level AR risk |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering link:** Chromosome rearrangements underlie the etiology of cancers diagnosed by **Fluorescence In Situ Hybridization (FISH)** and **karyotyping** — methodologies that combine molecular biology with optical/photonics engineering principles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mendelian Risk Ratios for AD Cross

Consider a heterozygous affected parent **Aa** (autosomal dominant) crossed with a homozygous normal partner **aa**.

**Step 1 — Set up parental gametes:**
- Parent 1 (Aa) produces gametes: A, a (each with probability 1/2)
- Parent 2 (aa) produces gametes: a (probability 1)

**Step 2 — Construct Punnett square:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Offspring genotypes:} \quad & \text{Aa (affected)}, \; \text{aa (normal)} \\
P(\text{Aa}) &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1 = \tfrac{1}{2} \\
P(\text{aa}) &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1 = \tfrac{1}{2} \\
\therefore P(\text{affected}) &= 0.5 = 50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**
- For every pregnancy, regardless of sex, the probability that the child is phenotypically affected is **1/2**, *provided* the trait shows **complete penetrance**.
- If penetrance is, say, **80%**, the effective risk becomes:
$$P_{\text{effective}} = 0.5 \times 0.8 = 0.40 = 40\%$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bayesian Conditional Probability in AR Pedigree

**Problem:** A phenotypically normal couple is known to be Aa × Aa. They already have one phenotypically normal child. What is the probability that the *next* child is affected?

**Step 1 — Prior probabilities (unconditional):**

$$
P(\text{AA}) = \tfrac{1}{4}, \quad P(\text{Aa}) = \tfrac{1}{2}, \quad P(\text{aa}) = \tfrac{1}{4}
$$

**Step 2 — Conditional probability of the first child being unaffected:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{unaffected} \mid \text{Aa} \times \text{Aa}) &= 1 - P(\text{aa}) \\
&= 1 - \tfrac{1}{4} = \tfrac{3}{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Bayes' Theorem:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{aa} \mid \text{child unaffected}) &= \frac{P(\text{aa}) \cdot P(\text{unaffected} \mid \text{aa})}{P(\text{unaffected})} \\
&= \frac{\tfrac{1}{4} \times 1}{\tfrac{3}{4}} = \frac{1}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Result:** The risk to the next child = **33.3%**, not 25%.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this happen?** The first child being unaffected is *evidence* — it rules out the 1/4 prior probability of being aa, redistributing probability mass among AA and Aa. The unconditional 1:3 ratio of "aa : not-aa" among unaffected children becomes the new effective 1:2 risk when conditioning on unaffected.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Segregation Ratios in a Robertsonian Translocation Carrier

A Robertsonian translocation carrier has the karyotype **45,XX,der(14;21)(q10;q10)**. Suppose the carrier's partner is chromosomally normal (45 vs 46).

**Step 1 — Identify the segregation modes at meiosis:**

The trivalent formed at meiosis I can segregate in **3 ways**:

1. **Alternate segregation** → balanced gametes (normal or carrier)
2. **Adjacent-1 segregation** → unbalanced gametes (trisomy 21 or monosomy 21)
3. **Adjacent-2 segregation** → very rare, produces other unbalanced types

**Step 2 — Theoretical outcomes of alternate segregation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gamete types (alternate):} \quad & \text{N (normal)} : \text{T (translocation carrier)} \\
\text{Frequency (empirical):} \quad & \sim 1/3 : \sim 1/3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical outcomes of adjacent-1 segregation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gamete types (adj-1):} \quad & \text{+21 (trisomy 21)} : \text{-21 (monosomy 21)} \\
\text{Outcome if fertilized by normal partner:} \quad & \text{Trisomy 21 (Down syndrome)} : \text{Monosomy 21 (lethal)} \\
\text{Combined with alternate segregation:} \quad & \text{Risk of live-born Down syndrome} \approx 10\text{–}15\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Practical interpretation:**
- Theoretical risk of live-born Down syndrome from a 14;21 Robertsonian carrier mother ≈ **10–15%**; from the father ≈ **2–5%**.
- This is the cytogenetic basis of **familial Down syndrome**, a classic KTU 14-mark question topic.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Diagnostic Fingerprints of Rearrangements

| Rearrangement | Cytogenetic Notation | Meiotic Configuration | Phenotypic Effect in Heterozygote |
|---|---|---|---|
| Terminal deletion | 46,XX,del(5)(p15) | Unpaired segment → loop | Variable; often severe (Cri-du-chat) |
| Interstitial deletion | 46,XY,del(2)(q13q21) | Loop with normal homolog | Variable; contiguous gene syndromes |
| Paracentric inversion | 46,XX,inv(3)(q21q26) | Inversion loop | Usually normal; acentric + dicentric gametes |
| Pericentric inversion | 46,XY,inv(9)(p11q13) | Inversion loop | Usually normal; duplication + deficiency gametes |
| Reciprocal translocation | 46,XX,t(9;22)(q34;q11) | Cross-shaped quadrivalent | Normal (balanced); CML if breakpoint disrupts gene |
| Robertsonian translocation | 45,XX,der(14;21)(q10;q10) | Trivalent | Normal carrier; Down syndrome offspring risk |
| Ring chromosome | 46,X,r(X)(p22q28) | Unpaired | Turner-like features if r(X) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering connection:** The **karyotype banding patterns (G-bands, Q-bands, R-bands)** that allow cytogeneticists to identify these rearrangements rely on differential dye-binding to AT-rich vs. GC-rich DNA regions. This is a direct application of analytical biochemistry within biomedical engineering.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — Pedigree of an Autosomal Dominant Trait

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeP1[Generation I - Father, Affected]
    nodeP2[Generation I - Mother, Normal]
    nodeC1[Generation II - Son 1, Affected]
    nodeC2[Generation II - Daughter 1, Normal]
    nodeC3[Generation II - Son 2, Affected]
    nodeC4[Generation II - Daughter 2, Normal]
    nodeG1[Generation III - Grandson 1, Affected]
    nodeG2[Generation III - Granddaughter 1, Normal]
    nodeG3[Generation III - Grandson 2, Affected]
    nodeP1 --- nodeP2
    nodeP1 --- nodeC1
    nodeP1 --- nodeC2
    nodeP1 --- nodeC3
    nodeP1 --- nodeC4
    nodeC1 --- nodeG1
    nodeC1 --- nodeG2
    nodeC3 --- nodeG3
```

> **Figure 4.1 — Pedigree showing vertical transmission pattern characteristic of autosomal dominant inheritance. Note affected individuals in every generation, both sexes affected, and male-to-male transmission (Father → Son 2).**

## 4.2 Diagram B — Pedigree of an Autosomal Recessive Trait

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeQ1[Generation I - Father, Carrier]
    nodeQ2[Generation I - Mother, Carrier]
    nodeQ3[Generation II - Son, Normal]
    nodeQ4[Generation II - Daughter, Affected]
    nodeQ5[Generation II - Daughter, Carrier]
    nodeQ6[Generation III - Grandson, Affected]
    nodeQ1 --- nodeQ2
    nodeQ2 --- nodeQ3
    nodeQ2 --- nodeQ4
    nodeQ2 --- nodeQ5
    nodeQ4 --- nodeQ6
```

> **Figure 4.2 — Pedigree of autosomal recessive inheritance. Note horizontal transmission (skipped generation), two unaffected carrier parents producing an affected daughter, and the affected daughter later producing an affected grandson (consanguinity context implied).**

## 4.3 Diagram C — Chromosome Rearrangement Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Chromosome Rearrangements]
    A --> B[Deletions]
    A --> C[Duplications]
    A --> D[Inversions]
    A --> E[Translocations]
    B --> B1[Terminal]
    B --> B2[Interstitial]
    B --> B3[Ring Formation]
    C --> C1[Tandem]
    C --> C2[Displaced]
    D --> D1[Paracentric - centromere NOT included]
    D --> D2[Pericentric - centromere INCLUDED]
    E --> E1[Reciprocal - non-homologous exchange]
    E --> E2[Robertsonian - acrocentric fusion]
    E --> E3[Insertional - segment moved within or between chromosomes]
```

> **Figure 4.3 — Hierarchical classification of structural chromosome rearrangements. Each terminal node represents a clinically/cytogenetically distinct category.**

## 4.4 Diagram D — Robertsonian Translocation Mechanism

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Chromosome 14]
    B[Chromosome 21]
    A --> A1[Break at centromere]
    B --> B1[Break at centromere]
    A1 --> F[Fusion: der 14 21]
    B1 --> F
    F --> R[Robertsonian Translocation Carrier - 45 chromosomes]
    R --> R1[Gamete N - Normal]
    R --> R2[Gamete T - Balanced Carrier]
    R --> R3[Gamete Plus 21 - Trisomy 21 Down Syndrome]
    R --> R4[Gamete Minus 21 - Lethal Monosomy]
```

> **Figure 4.4 — Formation of a Robertsonian translocation between chromosomes 14 and 21, and the four possible gametic outcomes. Alternate segregation (N or T) yields viable offspring; adjacent-1 segregation produces trisomy 21 (Down syndrome) or lethal monosomy 21.**

## 4.5 Diagram E — Inversion Loop at Meiosis I

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Paracentric Inversion - One Arm]
    P1 --> P1a[Break 1 in p arm]
    P1 --> P1b[Break 2 in p arm]
    P1a --> L[Inversion Loop at Meiosis I]
    P1b --> L
    L --> L1[Acentric fragment]
    L --> L2[Dicentric fragment]
    L --> L3[Normal chromatid]
    L --> L4[Inverted chromatid - balanced]
    P2[Pericentric Inversion - Crosses Centromere]
    P2 --> P2a[Break 1 in p arm]
    P2 --> P2b[Break 2 in q arm]
    P2a --> M[Inversion Loop - Centromere Included]
    P2b --> M
    M --> M1[Recombinant with duplication]
    M --> M2[Recombinant with deficiency]
    M --> M3[Normal]
    M --> M4[Inverted - balanced]
```

> **Figure 4.5 — Paracentric vs. pericentric inversion loops at meiosis I. Paracentric inversions produce acentric and dicentric fragments; pericentric inversions produce chromatids with duplication–deficiency imbalances.**
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between autosomal dominant and autosomal recessive inheritance with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Autosomal Dominant | Autosomal Recessive |
|---|---|---|
| Generations affected | Every generation (vertical) | Can skip generations (horizontal) |
| Affected parent | At least one parent usually affected | Both parents are usually unaffected carriers |
| Sex ratio | Equal | Equal |
| Recurrence risk (carrier × carrier) | Not applicable | 25% |
| Recurrence risk (affected het × normal) | 50% | Not applicable |
| Example | Huntington disease, Marfan syndrome | Cystic fibrosis, Sickle cell anemia, Phenylketonuria |
| Consanguinity effect | No special effect | Greatly increases recurrence risk |

*(Award 1 mark per correct row × 3 distinct points; or full 3 marks for a complete tabular answer.)*

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a Robertsonian translocation? Mention its clinical significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Robertsonian translocation is a type of **chromosomal rearrangement** in which two **acrocentric chromosomes** (13, 14, 15, 21, 22 in humans) fuse at their **centromeres** with loss of the short arm material. The carrier has only **45 chromosomes** instead of 46, but is phenotypically normal because the lost short arm material contains only redundant ribosomal RNA genes (1.5 marks).

**Clinical significance (1.5 marks):**
- Robertsonian translocation carriers (especially 14;21) are at increased risk of producing gametes leading to **Down syndrome (trisomy 21)** through adjacent-1 segregation.
- It accounts for a small but important fraction (~3–4%) of Down syndrome cases — the **familial form**.
- Carriers of 13;14 Robertsonian translocations have increased risk of **Patau syndrome (trisomy 13)**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Draw and explain the **characteristic pedigree pattern of autosomal recessive inheritance**. Discuss the effect of consanguinity on recurrence risk. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A phenotypically normal couple (both known carriers of cystic fibrosis, an AR disorder) already has **two unaffected children**. Using Bayesian probability, calculate the risk that their **third child** will be affected. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pedigree of AR inheritance (7 marks):**

A characteristic 3-generation pedigree should be drawn with:
- Two unaffected carrier parents in Generation I (half-filled symbols).
- An affected child (filled symbol) of either sex in Generation II — showing horizontal transmission / skipped generation.
- An affected grandchild in Generation III from the affected individual × a carrier partner.
- Both sexes equally affected.
- **No male-to-male transmission is required**, but both male and female should appear.

**Consanguinity effect (3 marks within part a):**
- Consanguineous mating (e.g., first-cousin marriage) increases the probability that both partners inherited the **same rare recessive allele** from a common ancestor.
- For a rare AR allele of frequency $q$, the inbreeding coefficient $F$ raises the probability of homozygosity from $q^2$ to approximately $q^2(1-F) + qF$.
- Practically, consanguinity raises the recurrence risk of AR disorders **2- to 3-fold** over the population baseline.

**(b) Bayesian calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Single-child unaffected:**
$$
P(\text{aa} \mid \text{1st child unaffected}) = \tfrac{1/4}{3/4} = \tfrac{1}{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Two consecutive unaffected children (independent events):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{unaffected} \mid \text{Aa} \times \text{Aa}) &= \tfrac{3}{4} \\
P(\text{both unaffected}) &= \left(\tfrac{3}{4}\right)^2 = \tfrac{9}{16}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Bayes' Theorem (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{aa} \mid \text{2 unaffected}) &= \frac{P(\text{aa}) \cdot P(\text{2 unaffected} \mid \text{aa})}{P(\text{2 unaffected})} \\
&= \frac{\tfrac{1}{4} \times 1}{\tfrac{9}{16}} = \frac{16}{36} = \frac{4}{9}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final answer:** The risk that the **third child** is affected = $\boxed{\dfrac{4}{9} \approx 44.4\%}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):** Many students mistakenly apply the unconditional 1/4 ratio repeatedly for each child. KTU examiners **deduct 2 marks** if the student does not explicitly state the conditional/independent events assumption and update the probability using Bayes' theorem. Always show the formula, not just the final answer.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper]**

**(a)** Classify the major types of **chromosome rearrangements** with neat diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **cytogenetic basis of the Philadelphia chromosome** and its role in **Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML)**. Discuss the diagnostic methods used to confirm the rearrangement. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of chromosome rearrangements (7 marks):**

Provide labelled diagrams and short descriptions (each ~1.5 marks):

1. **Deletions (Terminal & Interstitial):**
   - Single break (terminal) or two breaks (interstitial) → loss of segment.
   - Example: **Cri-du-chat syndrome — 46,XX,del(5)(p15).**

2. **Duplications (Tandem & Displaced):**
   - Same segment present twice on the same chromosome.
   - Example: **Charcot–Marie–Tooth disease type 1A — dup(17)(p12).**

3. **Inversions (Paracentric & Pericentric):**
   - Segment reversed end-to-end; paracentric excludes centromere, pericentric includes it.
   - Example: **inv(9)(p11q13)** — common variant.

4. **Translocations (Reciprocal & Robertsonian):**
   - Exchange of segments between non-homologous chromosomes (reciprocal) or fusion of two acrocentrics (Robertsonian).
   - Example: **t(9;22)(q34;q11)** — Philadelphia chromosome.
   - Example: **der(14;21)(q10;q10)** — Robertsonian.

**(b) Philadelphia chromosome and CML (7 marks):**

**Mechanism (3 marks):**
- The Philadelphia chromosome (Ph¹) is a **reciprocal translocation** between chromosomes **9q34** and **22q11**.
- Cytogenetic notation: **t(9;22)(q34;q11)**.
- This fuses the **BCR gene** (chromosome 22) with the **ABL1 gene** (chromosome 9), creating a chimeric **BCR-ABL1 fusion gene**.
- The fusion protein is a **constitutively active tyrosine kinase** that drives uncontrolled myeloid proliferation → CML.

**Diagnostic methods (4 marks):**
1. **Conventional karyotyping** — G-banding reveals the shortened chromosome 22 (Ph¹) in ~95% of CML cases.
2. **FISH (Fluorescence In Situ Hybridization)** — uses BCR (green) and ABL1 (red) probes; fusion signal confirms the translocation.
3. **RT-PCR / qPCR** — detects the BCR-ABL1 transcript at the mRNA level.
4. **Targeted therapy:** Imatinib (Gleevec) — a tyrosine kinase inhibitor that binds the BCR-ABL1 ATP-binding site, halting leukemic proliferation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B – Q B):** A common mark-loss area is failing to write the **exact ISCN notation** (e.g., t(9;22)(q34;q11)) — 1 mark is reserved for this. Also, students often confuse *reciprocal* and *Robertsonian* translocations when describing the Philadelphia chromosome; the Philadelphia chromosome is **reciprocal**, NOT Robertsonian. KTU examiners deduct a full 2 marks for this confusion.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Autosomal Dominant:** Vertical pedigree, 50% risk from affected het parent, no sex bias, **male-to-male transmission present**.
- **Autosomal Recessive:** Horizontal pedigree, 25% risk from carrier × carrier, consanguinity ↑ risk, **no male-to-male requirement**.
- **Conditional risk (AR, after 1 unaffected child):** 1/3 (NOT 1/4) — a **KTU favourite trap**.
- **Conditional risk (AR, after 2 unaffected children):** 4/9 (NOT 1/4 again).
- **Carrier frequency in population (Hardy–Weinberg):** $2pq$ for AR heterozygotes.
- **Deletions:** Loss of segment; Cri-du-chat (5p−) is the prototype.
- **Duplications:** Tandem or displaced; CMT1A is the prototype.
- **Inversions:** Paracentric (centromere excluded) vs. Pericentric (centromere included). Carriers phenotypically normal; reproductive risk via unbalanced gametes.
- **Translocations:**
  - **Reciprocal** — exchange of segments between non-homologues — Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22) → CML.
  - **Robertsonian** — fusion of two acrocentrics at centromere — 45-chromosome carrier — risk of familial Down syndrome.
- **Balanced** rearrangements → no net gain/loss of DNA → phenotypically normal carrier.
- **Unbalanced** rearrangements → gain/loss of genetic material → abnormal phenotype (syndromes, abortion, infertility).
- **Meiotic configurations:**
  - Inversion carriers → **inversion loop**.
  - Reciprocal translocation carriers → **quadrivalent / cross-shaped**.
  - Robertsonian carriers → **trivalent**.
- **ISCN notation is mandatory** in KTU 2024 cytogenetics answers (e.g., 46,XX,t(9;22)(q34;q11)).
- **Segregation in Robertsonian (14;21):** Alternate → N or T; Adjacent-1 → Trisomy 21 (Down) or lethal monosomy 21.
- **Ph¹ chromosome fusion gene:** BCR (chr 22) + ABL1 (chr 9) → BCR-ABL1 tyrosine kinase.
- **Diagnostic triad for chromosomal disorders:** Karyotype + FISH + molecular (PCR/NGS).
- **Engineering relevance:** FISH and karyotyping integrate molecular biology with optics, photonics, and image analysis — a cross-disciplinary KTU 2024 application point.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inversions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBB861 - CYTOGENETICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inversions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Chromosomal Inversion (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** A structural chromosomal aberration in which a single chromosome undergoes **two breaks** and the intervening segment is **reinserted in the reverse orientation (180° rotation)** within the same chromosome. No genetic material is gained or lost; only the linear sequence of genes is reversed.

Inversions are among the most common structural rearrangements in both plants and animals, and they are important in **evolutionary cytogenetics**, **speciation**, and **clinical genetics**.

### 1.2 Classification of Inversions (Based on Centromere Position)

| Feature | Paracentric Inversion | Pericentric Inversion |
|---|---|---|
| Centromere in inverted segment | **Absent** | **Present** |
| Breakpoints location | Both on the **same arm** of the chromosome | On **opposite arms** (one each side of centromere) |
| Origin (Greek etymology) | *para* = beside, *kentron* = center | *peri* = around, *kentron* = center |
| Arm ratio | **Unchanged** | **Altered** (diagnostic feature) |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Cytogenetic Distinction:** Because paracentric inversions keep the centromere outside the inverted segment, the arm ratio (q/p) remains identical to the normal chromosome. Pericentric inversions, by including the centromere, can change arm ratios and even shift centromere position from submetacentric to metacentric or vice versa — a feature used in **karyotype diagnosis**.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]  
> **"The Rearranged Subway Line" Analogy**  
> Imagine a subway line with stations A → B → C → D → E. A **paracentric inversion** is like rebuilding the line as A → B → **D → C** → E (two middle stations reversed, all on the same line segment). A **pericentric inversion** is like rebuilding a Y-shaped metro line so the branch order is reversed — a passenger boarding the line experiences the stations in a completely new order, but **no station is added or removed**.  
> The "passengers" (genes) all arrive; they just travel in a new order.

### 1.4 Standards and Diagnostic Metrics

- **Centromeric Index (CI):** $CI = \frac{p}{p+q} \times 100$  
  where **p = short arm length**, **q = long arm length**, both measured in micrometers ($\mu m$).
- **Arm Ratio (r):** $r = q / p$
- **Standard human karyotype resolution:** 400–850 band levels (ISCN 2020).
- **Minimum detectable inversion size:** $\sim 5 \text{ Mb}$ at standard G-banding.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Formation (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Induction of Double-Strand Breaks (DSBs):**  
   The chromosome suffers two simultaneous breaks, usually caused by ionizing radiation, transposons, replication stress, or recombination errors.
2. **Segment Excision:**  
   The fragment between the two breakpoints is physically excised as a linear piece.
3. **180° Rotation:**  
   The excised segment rotates end-over-end (inversion).
4. **Re-ligation:**  
   The rotated segment is reinserted into the chromosome by DNA repair machinery (NHEJ — Non-Homologous End Joining).
5. **Sequence Reversal:**  
   The result is a chromosome with reversed gene order but identical gene content.

### 2.2 Behaviour in Meiosis — The Inversion Loop

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Inversion Loop Formation:** When an organism is **heterozygous** for an inversion (one normal chromosome + one inverted chromosome), homologous pairing during prophase I of meiosis is impossible as a straight line. Instead, the chromosomes form an **inversion loop** (also called a *synaptic loop*) so that corresponding gene loci can align base-by-base.

- The loop is mandatory for **complete synapsis**; without it, crossing over would yield unbalanced gametes.
- The inverted chromosome appears as a "twisted" loop with the centromere either **outside** (paracentric) or **inside** (pericentric) the loop.

### 2.3 Consequences of Single Crossover Inside the Inversion Loop

#### (A) Paracentric Inversion Crossover Products

A single crossover within a paracentric inversion loop produces **four chromatids**, of which:
- **2 chromatids are non-recombinant** (1 normal + 1 inverted) → viable.
- **2 chromatids are recombinant** → structurally abnormal:
  - **Dicentric chromosome** (carries **two centromeres**) — pulled to both spindle poles during anaphase I → forms a **chromatin bridge**.
  - **Acentric fragment** (no centromere) — lost during cell division.

#### (B) Pericentric Inversion Crossover Products

A single crossover within a pericentric inversion loop produces:
- **2 non-recombinant chromatids** (1 normal + 1 inverted) → viable.
- **2 recombinant chromatids** with **duplications and deletions**:
  - One chromatid has a duplication of the distal segment and a deletion of the proximal segment.
  - The complementary chromatid has the reciprocal pattern.
- These chromatids are **not dicentric or acentric** — they segregate normally but produce **unbalanced gametes**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Centromeric Index | $CI = \dfrac{p}{p+q} \times 100$ | Used to classify chromosome morphology |
| Arm Ratio | $r = \dfrac{q}{p}$ | Indicates metacentric / submetacentric / acrocentric |
| Viable gametes (paracentric het., 1 CO inside loop) | $\dfrac{2}{4} = 50\%$ | Only the 2 non-recombinant chromatids |
| Viable gametes (pericentric het., 1 CO inside loop) | $\dfrac{2}{4} = 50\%$ | Only the 2 non-recombinant chromatids |
| Balanced-but-inv. gametes | $\dfrac{1}{4} = 25\%$ | Inversion transmitted to offspring |
| Recombination suppression (per unit length) | $\propto \dfrac{1}{\text{inversion length}}$ | Longer inversion → greater suppression |
| Crossing-over frequency in inversion loop | Same as normal bivalent | Loops do **not** suppress CO; they change outcomes |
| Position Effect | $\Delta E = f(d)$ | Gene expression altered by relocation near heterochromatin |

> [!NOTE]  
> **No vertical pipe (`|`) is used in the table cells** — all set operators are expressed with $\vert$ or $\mid$ inside math mode to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Evolutionary Speciation:** Inversions reduce recombination in heterokaryotypes, allowing locally adapted gene complexes to be protected from gene flow (the **"supergene" concept**, e.g., *Anopheles* mosquitoes, *Drosophila*).
- **Clinical Cytogenetics:** Pericentric inversion of **human chromosome 9** [inv(9)(p11q13)] is one of the most common benign variants (1–3% of the population).
- **Cancer Cytogenetics:** Inversions produce **chimeric oncogenes** (e.g., *Burkitt lymphoma* t(8;14); *CML* Philadelphia translocation involves an inversion-like mechanism in variant Ph).
- **Plant Breeding:** Inversions are used to maintain **balanced lethal systems** (e.g., *Oenothera lamarckiana*).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Arm Ratio Change Following a Pericentric Inversion

Consider a normal **submetacentric chromosome** with arms:
- Short arm = $p = 4 \ \mu m$
- Long arm = $q = 6 \ \mu m$

**Step 1 — Compute normal arm ratio:**

$$r_{normal} = \frac{q}{p} = \frac{6}{4} = 1.50$$

**Step 2 — Compute normal centromeric index:**

$$CI_{normal} = \frac{p}{p+q} \times 100 = \frac{4}{10} \times 100 = 40\%$$

**Step 3 — Apply a pericentric inversion that moves a 2-µm segment from the long arm to the short arm side (across the centromere):**

- New short arm: $p' = 4 + 2 = 6 \ \mu m$
- New long arm: $q' = 6 - 2 = 4 \ \mu m$

**Step 4 — Compute new arm ratio:**

$$r_{inv} = \frac{q'}{p'} = \frac{4}{6} = 0.67$$

**Step 5 — Compute new centromeric index:**

$$CI_{inv} = \frac{p'}{p'+q'} \times 100 = \frac{6}{10} \times 100 = 60\%$$

**Step 6 — Logical conclusion:**

$$\Delta r = r_{normal} - r_{inv} = 1.50 - 0.67 = 0.83$$

The chromosome has shifted from **submetacentric** ($r > 1.5$) toward **metacentric** ($r \approx 1.0$). This is the cytogenetic signature of a pericentric inversion.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Conversion logic explained:** A pericentric inversion that transfers genetic material from the long arm across the centromere to the short arm shortens the long arm and lengthens the short arm simultaneously, reducing the arm ratio. This is the diagnostic feature used in clinical karyotyping.

### 3.2 Derivation: Gamete Viability from an Inversion Heterozygote

Consider an organism heterozygous for a **paracentric inversion** with **one crossover** occurring within the inversion loop.

**Step 1 — Total chromatids after meiosis I:**

$$N_{total} = 4 \quad \text{(2 sister chromatids per homolog × 2 homologs)}$$

**Step 2 — Classify chromatids:**

- 1 chromatid: normal sequence → **viable**
- 1 chromatid: inverted sequence → **viable (balanced)**
- 1 chromatid: dicentric bridge → **inviable (meiotic bridge → breakage)**
- 1 chromatid: acentric fragment → **lost**

**Step 3 — Compute viable proportion:**

$$P_{viable} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.50 = 50\%$$

**Step 4 — Compute frequency of inversion-carrying viable gametes:**

$$P_{inv} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 = 25\%$$

**Step 5 — Effect of additional crossovers:**  
If $n$ crossovers occur **outside** the loop, they do not produce abnormal chromatids. The pattern remains:

$$P_{viable} = 50\%; \quad P_{inv} = 25\%; \quad P_{normal} = 25\%$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why only 50% viable?** Each chromatid entering the inversion loop has an independent chance of being the participant in a crossover. Statistically, half the chromatids emerge non-recombinant (and thus viable); the other half emerge as the dicentric/acentric pair, which are eliminated during gametogenesis.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Paracentric vs. Pericentric Crossover Outcomes

| Feature | Paracentric | Pericentric |
|---|---|---|
| Crossover products inside loop | Dicentric + Acentric | Duplication + Deletion (reciprocal) |
| Visible anaphase I bridge | **Yes** (chromatin bridge) | **No** (normal anaphase I) |
| Gamete viability | Reduced (~50% inviable) | Reduced (~50% unbalanced) |
| Offspring lethality | Embryonic lethality if bridge breaks | Partial duplication/deficiency syndrome |
| Recombination suppression | Strong (within loop) | Strong (within loop) |
| Suppresses crossing over near breakpoints? | **Yes** | **Yes** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Flowchart (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Chromosomal Inversions] --> B[Paracentric Inversion]
    A --> C[Pericentric Inversion]
    B --> D[Centromere OUTSIDE loop]
    C --> E[Centromere INSIDE loop]
    B --> F[Both breaks on SAME arm]
    C --> G[Breaks on OPPOSITE arms]
    B --> H[Arm ratio UNCHANGED]
    C --> I[Arm ratio CHANGED diagnostic]
    B --> J[CO product: Dicentric Acentric]
    C --> K[CO product: Duplication Deletion]
    H --> L[Often phenotypically silent]
    I --> M[Detectable by karyotype]
    J --> N[Anaphase I chromatin bridge]
    K --> O[No bridge normal segregation]
```

### 4.2 Inversion Loop & Crossover Outcomes (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHASE1[Prophase I Meiotic Pairing]
        N1[Normal Chromosome 1] --- N2[Normal Chromosome 2 sister]
        I1[Inverted Chromosome 1] --- I2[Inverted Chromosome 2 sister]
        N1 -. synapsis .-> I1
    end
    PHASE1 --> PHASE2[Inversion Loop Forms]
    PHASE2 --> PHASE3[Single Crossover Inside Loop]
    PHASE3 --> P1[Chromatid 1: Non-recombinant NORMAL]
    PHASE3 --> P2[Chromatid 2: Non-recombinant INVERTED]
    PHASE3 --> P3[Chromatid 3: ABNORMAL recombinant 1]
    PHASE3 --> P4[Chromatid 4: ABNORMAL recombinant 2]
    P1 --> VA[Viable gamete 25 percent]
    P2 --> VB[Viable balanced inversion 25 percent]
    P3 --> X1[Paracentric: Dicentric bridge]
    P3 --> X2[Pericentric: Duplication Deletion]
    P4 --> Y1[Paracentric: Acentric fragment lost]
    P4 --> Y2[Pericentric: Reciprocal Duplication Deletion]
```

### 4.3 Inversion Loop Schematic (ASCII Visualisation)

```
        Normal Homolog (───A───B───C───D───E───)
                              \        /
                               \      /
                                Loop region
                               /      \
                              /        \
        Inverted Homolog (───A───B───D───C───E───)
                  |        inversion breakpoint 1
                  inversion breakpoint 2

    Paracentric:  CEN outside loop (loop is on one arm only)
    Pericentric:   CEN inside loop (loop straddles centromere)
```

### 4.4 Functional Significance Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inversion Event] --> B[Reduced Recombination in Heterozygote]
    B --> C[Supergene Formation]
    C --> D[Local Adaptation Preserved]
    D --> E[Reproductive Isolation]
    E --> F[Speciation]
    A --> G[Position Effect on Genes]
    G --> H[Phenotypic Alteration without Gene Change]
    H --> I[Possible Disease or Variant Phenotype]
    A --> J[Balanced Rearrangement in Offspring]
    J --> K[Familial Transmission by Inversion Carrier]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
**Differentiate between paracentric and pericentric inversions with suitable diagrams.** (3 marks)

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Paracentric | Pericentric |
|---|---|---|
| Centromere | Outside inverted segment | Inside inverted segment |
| Breakpoints | Both on same arm | One on each arm |
| Arm ratio | Unchanged | Altered |
| Crossover product | Dicentric + acentric | Duplication + deletion |

*(1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for tabular differences. Diagram optional but earns full marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
**What is an inversion loop? Why is it necessary during meiosis in inversion heterozygotes?** (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**  
An inversion loop is a synapsed configuration formed during prophase I of meiosis in which one normal chromosome and its inverted homolog pair by twisting into a loop so that homologous gene loci can align base-by-base *(1.5 marks)*. It is **necessary** because linear side-by-side synapsis is impossible when the gene order differs; without a loop, the homologs would pair incorrectly and crossing over would yield only unbalanced gametes *(1.5 marks)*.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a) Describe the mechanism of formation of chromosomal inversions. Add a note on the types of inversions.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**  
A chromosomal inversion is a structural rearrangement in which a chromosome segment is excised, rotated 180°, and reinserted into the same chromosome. No DNA is gained or lost.

**Step 2 — Mechanism (4 marks):**

1. **Induction of breaks:** Two double-strand breaks occur in a single chromosome, caused by ionizing radiation, transposons, chemical mutagens, or replication errors.
2. **Excision of intermediate segment:** The segment between the two breakpoints is removed.
3. **Rotation:** The excised segment is rotated 180° on its own axis.
4. **Re-ligation:** The rotated segment is rejoined by the NHEJ (Non-Homologous End Joining) repair pathway.
5. **Outcome:** A chromosome with reversed gene order — a balanced rearrangement.

**Step 3 — Types (2 marks):**

- **Paracentric:** Inverted segment excludes the centromere.
- **Pericentric:** Inverted segment includes the centromere.
- Diagnostic difference: Pericentric inversions alter arm ratio; paracentric inversions do not.

---

**(b) Explain the genetic consequences of a single crossover within the inversion loop of a paracentric inversion heterozygote.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Loop formation (1 mark):**  
In prophase I, the normal and inverted homologs synapse by forming an inversion loop. The crossover event occurs *within* the loop.

**Step 2 — Four-chromatid outcome (2 marks):**  
After one crossover, the tetrad contains four chromatids:
- Chromatid 1: non-recombinant, **normal** order.
- Chromatid 2: non-recombinant, **inverted** order.
- Chromatid 3: recombinant, with **two centromeres (dicentric)**.
- Chromatid 4: recombinant, with **no centromere (acentric)**.

**Step 3 — Anaphase I behaviour (2 marks):**  
The dicentric chromatid is pulled toward both spindle poles, forming a **chromatin bridge**. The acentric fragment, lacking a centromere, is **lost** during cell division. Both chromatids are eventually destroyed or lost.

**Step 4 — Gamete outcome (2 marks):**  
- 50% viable gametes (1 normal + 1 inverted).
- 50% inviable (the dicentric–acentic pair).
- Offspring of inversion heterozygotes show **reduced fertility** and **partial zygotic lethality** when a bridge-breakage event leads to broken chromosomes.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a) Compare the effects of crossing over in paracentric and pericentric inversion heterozygotes.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Common feature (1 mark):**  
Both types of inversions, when heterozygous, form inversion loops in meiosis and **suppress recombination** in the inverted region because the recombinant chromatids are eliminated or unbalanced.

**Step 2 — Paracentric crossover (3 marks):**

- Products: **Dicentric chromatid + acentric fragment**.
- Anaphase I: **chromatin bridge** is visible; acentric fragment is lost.
- Result: **50% gametes are inviable** → reduced fertility, no viable recombinant offspring.

**Step 3 — Pericentric crossover (3 marks):**

- Products: Two chromatids with **reciprocal duplications and deletions**.
- Anaphase I: **no bridge**; chromosomes segregate normally.
- Result: 50% unbalanced gametes (each with partial trisomy + partial monosomy) → embryonic lethality or **deletion/duplication syndromes** in offspring.

| Parameter | Paracentric | Pericentric |
|---|---|---|
| Bridge formation | **Yes** | **No** |
| Acentric loss | **Yes** | **No** |
| Duplication/deletion | No | **Yes** |
| Type of lethality | Pre-zygotic (gametic) | Post-zygotic (embryonic) |

---

**(b) Discuss the evolutionary and clinical significance of inversions.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Evolutionary significance (3.5 marks):**

- **Supergene preservation:** Inversions suppress recombination in heterozygotes, allowing co-adapted gene complexes to be transmitted as a single unit (supergenes) — e.g., *Drosophila*'s *In(2L)t* and chromosomal polymorphisms.
- **Reproductive isolation:** When two populations accumulate different inversions, hybrids produce unbalanced gametes → hybrid sterility → **speciation** (e.g., *Anopheles gambiae* complex, *Drosophila willistoni* group).
- **Local adaptation:** Inversions can carry adaptive alleles to high frequency without disruption by gene flow (e.g., seaweed fly *Coelopa frigida*).

**Step 2 — Clinical significance (3.5 marks):**

- **Position effect:** A gene relocated near heterochromatin by inversion may show altered expression (e.g., *Aniridia* — *PAX6* gene affected by inversion).
- **Cancer:** Inversions can create chimeric oncogenes — e.g., *inv(16)* in AML produces *CBFB-MYH11* fusion.
- **Familial variants:** Inversion 9 [inv(9)(p11q13)] is a common **benign polymorphism** in 1–3% of humans.
- **Reproductive counselling:** Inversion carriers are at risk of producing unbalanced gametes, leading to **recurrent miscarriages** or **infertility**.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Valuation):**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse paracentric with pericentric** by centromere position — this is the most common 2-mark deduction in 14-mark answers.
> 2. **Always draw the inversion loop** when explaining crossing-over consequences — examiners award 1–2 marks for a correct labelled diagram.
> 3. **Distinguish "balanced" from "viable"** — a balanced inversion carrier is *viable* and *fertile*; a crossover inside the loop produces *viable* but *unbalanced* gametes.
> 4. **Don't claim inversions change gene content** — they rearrange but do **not** delete or duplicate genes (except in pericentric crossovers).
> 5. **Arm ratio derivation must include both p and q measurements** — partial derivation without units ($µm$) loses half the mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Inversion = reversed segment, no net loss/gain of DNA.**
- ✅ **Paracentric** → centromere outside the inverted segment → **arm ratio unchanged**.
- ✅ **Pericentric** → centromere inside the inverted segment → **arm ratio altered (diagnostic)**.
- ✅ **Inversion loop** is required for homologous synapsis in heterozygotes.
- ✅ **Paracentric crossover → dicentric + acentric → chromatin bridge → gamete loss.**
- ✅ **Pericentric crossover → duplication + deletion → unbalanced gametes → zygotic lethality.**
- ✅ **50% viable gametes / 50% inviable** in inversion heterozygotes (single CO inside loop).
- ✅ **Position effect** can alter gene expression without changing the gene itself.
- ✅ **Inversions preserve gene content → carriers are phenotypically normal but produce unbalanced gametes.**
- ✅ **Inversions drive speciation** via recombination suppression (supergene concept).
- ✅ **inv(9)** is the most common human inversion variant (benign polymorphism).
- ✅ **Inversions in cancer:** create chimeric oncogenes (e.g., *CBFB-MYH11* in AML).
- ✅ **Arm ratio formula:** $r = q/p$; **Centromeric index:** $CI = \dfrac{p}{p+q} \times 100$.
- ✅ **Suppression of recombination** in inversion region is the *evolutionary* key, not a structural error.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inheritance of complex traits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBB861 - CYTOGENETICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Inheritance of complex traits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Inheritance of complex traits** (also known as **quantitative genetics** or **polygenic inheritance**) refers to the mode of inheritance exhibited by phenotypic characters that are governed by the **cumulative, additive, and interactive effects of multiple genes** (often at different loci), frequently modulated by environmental factors. These traits display **continuous phenotypic variation** in natural populations, do not segregate in simple Mendelian ratios, and require **biometrical and statistical** rather than Mendelian, analytical methods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "Complex traits are characters whose expression is determined by the collective action of two or more genes (polygenes) at separate loci, often interacting with the environment. They show continuous variation, polygenic inheritance, and are studied using quantitative genetic parameters such as mean, variance, heritability, and selection differential."

### Key Terminology Table

| Term | KTU-Standard Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Polygenic Trait** | A phenotype influenced by many genes, each contributing a small effect. |
| **Quantitative Trait Locus (QTL)** | A specific chromosomal region containing one or more genes contributing to a quantitative trait. |
| **Continuous Variation** | Phenotypic values that show a smooth, unbroken distribution (e.g., height, weight). |
| **Discontinuous Variation** | Phenotypes falling into discrete, non-overlapping classes (e.g., Mendelian traits). |
| **Heritability** | The proportion of phenotypic variance attributable to genetic variance. |
| **Polygenes** | The individual genes that collectively contribute to a polygenic trait. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Volume Knob" Analogy for Complex Traits**

Imagine you are mixing the colour **white** in a bucket using drops of paint. A **Mendelian (simple) trait** is like a **toggle switch** — either the light is ON or OFF. A **complex trait** is like a **dimmer (volume) knob** — there are infinitely many positions between OFF and ON.

- Each paint drop = one gene (allele) contributing a tiny increment.
- The room's ambient light = environmental influence.
- The final shade of grey you see = the **phenotype**.

Just as the final brightness depends on:
1. **How many drops** of paint you added (number of contributing alleles),
2. **How large each drop** is (allelic effect size), and
3. **The lighting in the room** (environment),

…a complex trait like human height depends on:
1. **The number of segregating loci** (often >100 in humans),
2. **The effect size of each allele**, and
3. **Nutritional and environmental conditions** during development.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Check:** If a trait is governed by a single gene with complete dominance (e.g., Mendel's pea plant height, *Tt*), you get a **3:1** ratio. If it is governed by 10 genes, each with incomplete dominance, you may get **phenotypes that blend continuously** — the essence of polygenic inheritance.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Complex Traits

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Favourite Classification:** Complex traits are categorized based on the **relative contribution of genotype vs environment**.

| Category | Definition | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Polygenic** | Determined by many genes; environment minimal. | Kernel colour in wheat (Nilsson-Ehle) |
| **Multifactorial** | Determined by many genes + significant environment. | Human hypertension, diabetes |
| **Quantitative** | Measured on a numerical scale; polygenic. | Milk yield in cattle, crop yield |
| **Threshold** | Liabilities exceed a cutoff; binary expression. | Cleft palate, schizophrenia |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Pitfall:** Many students confuse "polygenic" and "multifactorial". **Polygenic** = many genes; **Multifactorial** = many genes + environment. Multifactorial is the **broader** category, polygenic is a **subset** when environment contribution is negligible.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Quantitative Genetics Constants & Metrics**

- **Heritability (h²):** Dimensionless ratio, ranges from **0 to 1** (or 0% to 100%).
- **Variance (σ²):** Measured in **(trait units)²** — e.g., cm² for height.
- **Standard Deviation (σ):** Square root of variance; same unit as trait.
- **Coefficient of Variation (CV):** Dimensionless ratio, expressed as a percentage.
- **Mean (μ):** Arithmetic average; same unit as trait.
- **Selection Differential (S):** Difference between mean of selected parents and population mean; same unit as trait.
- **Response to Selection (R):** h² × S; same unit as trait.
- **Generation Time:** Variable; humans ≈ **25–30 years**, *Drosophila* ≈ **10–14 days**, maize ≈ **3–4 months**.

---

## 1.5 Historical Milestones (Board Significance)

| Year | Scientist | Contribution |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1865** | **Gregor Mendel** | Established laws of inheritance (single-gene traits). |
| **1909** | **Wilhelm Johannsen** | Coined terms *gene*, *genotype*, *phenotype*. |
| **1909** | **Herman Nilsson-Ehle** | Proposed the **multiple-factor hypothesis** for kernel colour in wheat. |
| **1918** | **Ronald A. Fisher** | Reconciled Mendelism and biometry through the **infinitesimal model**. |
| **1930** | **Sewall Wright** | Developed **path coefficient analysis**; inbreeding coefficient (F). |
| **1943** | **Lush & Hazel** | Pioneered **heritability estimation** in animal breeding. |
| **1975** | **Geldermann** | Coined term **QTL** (Quantitative Trait Loci). |
| **1989** | **Lander & Botstein** | Developed **interval mapping** for QTL detection. |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Polygenic Inheritance

### 2.1.1 The Nilsson-Ehle Multiple-Factor Hypothesis (1909)

**Theoretical Steps:**

1. A quantitative trait is controlled by **multiple genes** (polygenes), each located at a different **locus** on the chromosome.
2. Each polygene has a **small, additive effect** on the phenotype.
3. The alleles at each locus are typically **incompletely dominant** (so heterozygotes produce an intermediate phenotype).
4. The **total phenotype** = sum of individual allelic contributions + environmental deviation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical formulation:**
>
> $$P = G + E$$
>
> $$P = G + E + (G \times E)$$
>
> Where **P** = Phenotype, **G** = Genotype, **E** = Environment, and **G × E** = Genotype-Environment interaction.

### 2.1.2 Fisher's Infinitesimal Model (1918)

**Theoretical Steps:**

1. A quantitative trait is influenced by an **infinite (very large) number of loci**, each with an infinitesimally small effect.
2. Allelic effects are **additive**.
3. The trait follows a **normal distribution** due to the **Central Limit Theorem** when many small effects sum.
4. Environmental deviations are also normally distributed and independent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Consequence:** The phenotypic distribution in a randomly mating population approaches a **bell-shaped (Gaussian) curve**, even if individual gene effects are non-normal.

### 2.1.3 The Additive-Dominance Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Falconer's Three-Component Model of Genotypic Value:**
>
> $$G = A + D$$
>
> Where:
> - **G** = Genotypic value (total deviation of genotype from population mean)
> - **A** = Additive genetic value (breeding value; due to average effect of alleles)
> - **D** = Dominance deviation (interaction between alleles at the same locus)

Full model with epistasis:
$$G = A + D + I$$
Where **I** = epistatic (interaction) deviation between alleles at different loci.

### 2.1.4 Phenotypic Variance Partitioning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The fundamental equation of quantitative genetics:**
>
> $$V_P = V_G + V_E + 2\,\text{Cov}(G,E)$$
>
> If genotype and environment are **independent** (a common assumption):
>
> $$V_P = V_G + V_E$$
>
> Further decomposition of **V_G**:
>
> $$V_G = V_A + V_D + V_I$$
>
> Where:
> - **V_P** = Phenotypic variance
> - **V_G** = Genotypic variance
> - **V_E** = Environmental variance
> - **V_A** = Additive genetic variance (transmitted to offspring)
> - **V_D** = Dominance variance
> - **V_I** = Epistatic variance
> - **Cov(G,E)** = Genotype-Environment covariance

### 2.1.5 Heritability — The Critical Concept

> [!WARNING]
> **High-Yield Concept:** Two types of heritability are universally tested in KTU exams. **Do not confuse them!**

#### Broad-Sense Heritability (H²)

$$H^2 = \frac{V_G}{V_P} = \frac{V_A + V_D + V_I}{V_P}$$

**Meaning:** The proportion of phenotypic variance due to **all genetic causes** (additive, dominance, epistatic).

#### Narrow-Sense Heritability (h²)

$$h^2 = \frac{V_A}{V_P}$$

**Meaning:** The proportion of phenotypic variance due to **additive genetic effects** only — the portion reliably passed from parent to offspring.

> [!TIP]
> **Selection Rule:** Only **h²** (narrow-sense) is used in the **breeder's equation** (R = h²S). Broad-sense heritability is used to describe the total genetic determination of a trait.

#### Estimates of Heritability from Twin & Pedigree Data

$$H^2_{\text{twin}} = \frac{2(V_M - V_D)}{V_P}$$

$$h^2_{\text{regression}} = 2 \times b_{\text{offspring, midparent}}$$

Where **V_M** = variance of MZ twin pairs, **V_D** = variance of DZ twin pairs, and **b** = regression coefficient.

---

## 2.2 Other Forms of Complex Inheritance

### 2.2.1 Pleiotropy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A single gene influences **multiple, seemingly unrelated** phenotypic traits.

**Types:**
- **Antagonistic Pleiotropy** — Gene has opposite effects on fitness at different life stages (e.g., sickle-cell allele: protective against malaria, harmful in homozygotes).
- **Synergistic Pleiotropy** — Gene effects on multiple traits reinforce each other.
- **Mosaic Pleiotropy** — Gene affects traits via independent pathways.

### 2.2.2 Epistasis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The interaction between alleles at **different loci** such that one gene masks or modifies the expression of another.

**Classical Ratios (Modified Mendelian Ratios):**

| Gene Interaction | Modified Ratio | Example |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Recessive Epistasis | **9 : 3 : 4** | Coat colour in mice |
| Dominant Epistasis | **12 : 3 : 1** | Summer squash shape |
| Complementary Genes | **9 : 7** | Flower colour in sweet pea |
| Duplicate Dominant Epistasis | **15 : 1** | Shepherd's purse capsule |
| Duplicate Recessive Epistasis | **9 : 7** | (Same as complementary) |
| Dominant & Recissive Interaction | **13 : 3** | — |

### 2.2.3 Penetrance and Expressivity

> [!NOTE]
> **Penetrance** = Percentage of individuals with a given genotype who actually express the associated phenotype.
>
> **Expressivity** = The degree or intensity of phenotypic expression among individuals of the same genotype.

$$P = \frac{\text{Number of individuals expressing phenotype}}{\text{Total number of individuals with genotype}} \times 100\%$$

**Example:** BRCA1 mutation has ~80% penetrance for breast cancer; polydactyly shows variable expressivity.

### 2.2.4 Multiple Alleles

**Definition:** A gene that has **more than two allelic forms** in a population.

**Classic Example:** ABO blood group system
- Gene **I** has three alleles: **I^A**, **I^B**, **i**
- I^A and I^B are codominant; both are dominant over i.
- Six genotypes → **four phenotypes** (A, B, AB, O).

| Genotype | Phenotype |
| :--- | :--- |
| I^A I^A or I^A i | Type A |
| I^B I^B or I^B i | Type B |
| I^A I^B | Type AB |
| i i | Type O |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE — It is the most heavily tested component across all KTU 2024 modules.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Application |
| :--: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $P = G + E$ | Phenotype decomposition | All complex traits |
| 2 | $V_P = V_G + V_E$ | Variance partitioning | Assuming G-E independence |
| 3 | $V_G = V_A + V_D + V_I$ | Genotypic variance split | Quantitative genetics |
| 4 | $H^2 = V_G / V_P$ | Broad-sense heritability | Total genetic determination |
| 5 | $h^2 = V_A / V_P$ | Narrow-sense heritability | Predicting breeding value |
| 6 | $R = h^2 \times S$ | Breeder's equation | Response to selection |
| 7 | $S = \mu_{\text{selected}} - \mu_{\text{pop}}$ | Selection differential | Artificial selection |
| 8 | $\Delta G = i \times h^2 \times \sigma_P$ | Genetic gain per generation | Plant/animal breeding |
| 9 | $V_M - V_D$ | MZ vs DZ variance difference | Twin heritability |
| 10 | $b = h^2 / 2$ (regression slope) | Parent-offspring regression | Estimating h² |
| 11 | $F = \frac{2pq(1 - F_0)}{1 - 2pq(1 - F_0)}$ | Inbreeding coefficient | Population genetics |
| 12 | $h^2 = 2 \times b_{\text{offspring-parent}}$ | Single-parent regression | h² estimation |
| 13 | $r_A = \frac{V_A}{\sqrt{V_{A1} \cdot V_{A2}}}$ | Genetic correlation | Multi-trait selection |
| 14 | $N_e = \frac{4 N_M N_F}{N_M + N_F}$ | Effective population size | Conservation genetics |

Where:
- **R** = response to selection
- **S** = selection differential
- **ΔG** = genetic gain
- **i** = selection intensity (standardized selection differential)
- **σ_P** = phenotypic standard deviation
- **F** = inbreeding coefficient
- **N_e** = effective population size
- **N_M, N_F** = number of male and female breeding individuals

> [!WARNING]

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Scientific Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Disciplinary Applications of Quantitative Genetics**

| Field | Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Plant Breeding** | Designing high-yield, drought-resistant crop varieties using marker-assisted selection (MAS). |
| **Animal Husbandry** | Predicting milk yield, growth rate, and disease resistance in livestock. |
| **Medical Genetics** | Calculating polygenic risk scores for diabetes, schizophrenia, cardiovascular disease. |
| **Pharmacogenomics** | Predicting drug response based on multi-gene profiles. |
| **Forensic Science** | DNA phenotyping for predicting externally visible characteristics. |
| **Evolutionary Biology** | Detecting selection signatures (e.g., selective sweeps) in natural populations. |
| **Conservation Biology** | Managing inbreeding depression in small populations using N_e. |
