| Cell culture harvest time | Lymphocytes: PHA-stimulated 48–72 h | **48–72 h** | Routine karyotype |
| Colcemid arrest window | Metaphase block | **15–30 min** | Chromosome spreading |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Medicine

Although this is a biological subject, the techniques rely heavily on engineering:

- **Optics & Photonics** — Confocal microscopy, fluorescence excitation/emission filters (FISH).
- **Microfluidics** — Microarray chip fabrication (Array-CGH).
- **Image Processing / AI** — Automated karyotyping uses CNN-based metaphase finding (e.g., CytoAI).
- **Signal Processing** — CGH ratio smoothing, Fourier-based banding analysis.
- **Bioinformatics Pipelines** — BWA-MEM, GATK for SV detection in cytogenomics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations

### 3.1.1 Derivation of the FISH Hybridization Stringency Window

We begin with the thermodynamic basis for probe–target duplex stability and convert it into a workable experimental rule.

**Step 1 — Start from the nearest-neighbor Tm equation:**

The empirical nearest-neighbor model states:

$$
T_m = T_m^{(NN)} + 16.6 \log_{10}[Na^+] - 0.41 \cdot L_{corr}
$$

For routine FISH probes, this is approximated by the simpler Wallace–Itakura form:

$$
T_m = 81.5 + 16.6 \log_{10}[Na^+] + 0.41(\%GC) - \frac{600}{L}
$$

**Step 2 — Insert typical FISH probe parameters:**

Let $[Na^+] = 0.3$ M, $\%GC = 50$, $L = 200{,}000$ bp (200 kb BAC probe).

**Step 3 — Evaluate term by term:**

$$
16.6 \log_{10}(0.3) = 16.6 \times (-0.5229) = -8.68
$$

$$
0.41 \times 50 = 20.5
$$

$$
\frac{600}{200{,}000} = 0.003
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back:**

$$
T_m = 81.5 + (-8.68) + 20.5 - 0.003
$$

$$
T_m \approx 93.32 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the hybridization rule of thumb:**

$$
T_{hyb} = T_m - 20 \text{ °C} \approx 73.3 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
T_{wash} \approx T_m - 25 \text{ °C} \approx 68.3 \text{ °C}
$$

> **Clinical interpretation:** This computed wash temperature (≈68 °C) matches the empirically optimized 65–72 °C / 0.1× SSC condition used in clinical FISH laboratories.

---

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Array-CGH Log2 Threshold

**Step 1 — Define the ratio for a single-copy gain (trisomy):**

The test sample carries three copies; the reference carries two.

$$
R = \frac{\text{Test intensity}}{\text{Reference intensity}} = \frac{3}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to a logarithmic scale to symmetrize gains and losses:**

$$
\log_2 R = \log_2\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Use the change-of-base identity $\log_2(3/2) = \log_{10}(3/2)/\log_{10}(2)$:**

$$
\log_2\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) = \frac{0.1761}{0.3010} \approx 0.5849
$$

$$
\boxed{\log_2\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) \approx +0.585}
$$

**Step 4 — Repeat for a single-copy loss:**

$$
\log_2\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -1.000
$$

**Step 5 — Generalization to $k$ extra copies:**

$$
\Delta_{log2}(k) = \log_2\left(\frac{2+k}{2}\right)
$$

For $k = 1$: $+0.585$; for $k = 2$: $+1.000$; for $k = 3$: $+1.322$.

---

### 3.1.3 Derivation of Band Resolution Scaling with Prometaphase Index

Let $r$ = bands per haploid genome at prometaphase. The fraction of the genome resolvable by classical banding:

$$
f_{classical} = \frac{r_{classical}}{r_{total}} \times 100\%
$$

Substituting the routine and high-resolution values:

$$
f_{classical}^{routine} = \frac{400}{3000} \times 100\% \approx 13.3\%
$$

$$
f_{classical}^{HR} = \frac{850}{3000} \times 100\% \approx 28.3\%
$$

This explains why ~70% of clinically significant microdeletions escape detection by karyotyping alone.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Karyotype | FISH | Array-CGH | NGS-Cytogenomics |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Resolution** | 5–10 Mb | 100 kb – 1 Mb | 50–100 kb | < 1 kb |
| **Detects translocations** | ✓ | ✓ (limited) | ✗ | ✓ |
| **Detects balanced rearrangements** | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ |
| **Detects CNVs** | Limited | Probe-dependent | ✓ | ✓ |
| **Detects SNVs** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ |
| **Sample throughput** | Low | Low–Medium | High | High |
| **Cost per sample** | Low | Medium | High | High |
| **Turnaround time** | 7–14 days | 1–3 days | 3–7 days | 5–14 days |
| **Cell requirement** | Dividing cells | Dividing or interphase | DNA only | DNA only |
| **Standard reference** | ISCN 2024 | ISCN 2024 | ISCN 2024 | ISCN 2024 |

---

## 3.3 Diagnostic Decision Algorithm (Numerical-Aided)

A clinical laboratory receives a sample with suspected microdeletion. The decision tree:

1. **If clinical suspicion is high (e.g., Williams syndrome 7q11.23) → Targeted FISH probe first (24–48 h).**
2. **If phenotype is non-specific → Array-CGH first (genome-wide screen).**
3. **If a balanced rearrangement is suspected (e.g., recurrent miscarriages) → Karyotype first.**
4. **If karyotype is normal but phenotype remains unexplained → LP-WGS / OGM.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Resolution Pyramid of Cytogenetic Approaches

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient Sample: Blood / Amniotic Fluid / Bone Marrow / Tissue] --> B[Cell Culture: PHA-Stimulated Lymphocytes 48-72 h]
    B --> C[Metaphase Arrest: Colcemid 15-30 min]
    C --> D[Hypotonic Treatment: 0.075 M KCl]
    D --> E[Fixation: 3:1 Methanol-Acetic Acid]
    E --> F{Banding Technique}
    F --> F1[G-Banding Giemsa Trypsin]
    F --> F2[Q-Banding Quinacrine]
    F --> F3[R-Banding Reverse Heat]
    F --> F4[C-Banding Constitutive Heterochromatin]
    E --> G[Direct / Interphase Prep]
    G --> H[FISH Probe Hybridization]
    H --> H1[CEP Centromere Enumeration Probe]
    H --> H2[WCP Whole Chromosome Paint]
    H --> H3[Locus-Specific BAC Probe]
    H --> H4[M-FISH / SKY 24-Color Karyotyping]
    E --> I[DNA Extraction]
    I --> J[Array-CGH or SNP Array]
    I --> K[LP-WGS Low-Pass Whole Genome Sequencing]
    I --> L[Optical Genome Mapping OGM]
    F1 --> M[ISCN 2024 Karyotype Report]
    F2 --> M
    F3 --> M
    F4 --> M
    H1 --> N[Targeted Cytogenetic Report]
    H2 --> N
    H3 --> N
    H4 --> N
    J --> O[Genome-Wide CNV Profile]
    K --> O
    L --> O
    M --> P[Clinical Interpretation]
    N --> P
    O --> P
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Modern Cytogenetics Laboratory

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SampleIntake["Sample Intake Bay"]
        SI1[Sample Receipt and Accessioning]
        SI2[Patient Consent Verification]
        SI3[Sample Type Triage: Blood / Amniotic / Tissue]
    end
    subgraph CellCultureSuite["Cell Culture Suite"]
        CC1[Lymphocyte Culture with PHA]
        CC2[Amniocyte in-situ Culture]
        CC3[Colcemid Synchronization]
        CC4[Harvesting and Slide Preparation]
    end
    subgraph ClassicalLab["Classical Cytogenetics Lab"]
        CL1[G-Banding Staining Station]
        CL2[Q / R / C-Banding Station]
        CL3[Metaphase Finder Automated Microscope]
        CL4[Image Analysis and Karyotyping Workstation]
    end
    subgraph MolecularLab["Molecular Cytogenetics Lab"]
        ML1[DNA Extraction BSL-2 Hood]
        ML2[FISH Probe Hybridization Oven]
        ML3[Fluorescence Microscopy with Multi-band Filters]
        ML4[Array-CGH Hybridization Station]
        ML5[NGS Library Prep and Sequencer]
    end
    subgraph Bioinformatics["Bioinformatics and Reporting"]
        BI1[Pipeline: BWA MEM, GATK, CNVkit]
        BI2[ISCN 2024 Nomenclature Engine]
        BI3[Variant Interpretation ACMG Guidelines]
        BI4[Authorized Signatory Review]
    end
    SampleIntake --> CellCultureSuite
    CellCultureSuite --> ClassicalLab
    CellCultureSuite --> MolecularLab
    ClassicalLab --> Bioinformatics
    MolecularLab --> Bioinformatics
    ML5 --> BI1
    BI1 --> BI2
    BI2 --> BI3
    BI3 --> BI4
```

## 4.3 Decision Algorithm — Choosing the Right Cytogenetic Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Patient with Suspected Chromosomal Disorder] --> Q1{Clinical Phenotype Specific?}
    Q1 -->|Yes: e.g. Down Syndrome Features| A1[Karyotype First]
    Q1 -->|No: Non-specific Dysmorphism| A2[Array-CGH First]
    Q1 -->|Recurrent Pregnancy Loss| A3[Karyotype Both Partners]
    A1 --> Q2{Karyotype Result?}
    Q2 -->|Abnormal| R1[ISCN Report and Genetic Counseling]
    Q2 -->|Normal but Suspicion Persists| A4[Targeted FISH for Suspected Locus]
    A2 --> Q3{Array-CGH Result?}
    Q3 -->|Pathogenic CNV Detected| R1
    Q3 -->|VUS of Uncertain Significance| A5[Parental Studies and LP-WGS]
    Q3 -->|Normal| A6[Consider NGS Gene Panel or Whole Exome]
    A4 --> R2[FISH Report ISCN Compatible]
    A5 --> A7[NGS Cytogenomic Pipeline]
    A6 --> R3[Exome / Genome Report]
    A7 --> R4[Integrated Cytogenomic Report]
    R4 --> R5[Multidisciplinary Board Review]
    R1 --> R5
    R2 --> R5
    R3 --> R5
    R5 --> End[Genetic Counseling and Management Plan]
```

## 4.4 Schematic of FISH Signal Patterns and Their Diagnostic Meaning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Interphase Nucleus Under FISH] --> B{Number and Position of Signals}
    B --> S1[Two Signals: Normal Disomy]
    B --> S2[Three Signals: Trisomy]
    B --> S3[One Signal: Monosomy or Deletion]
    B --> S4[Fused Yellow Signal: No Rearrangement]
    B --> S5[Split Red and Green Signal: Translocation]
    B --> S6[Loss of One Color: Deletion or Imbalance]
    S1 --> D1[Normal Karyotype]
    S2 --> D2[e.g. Trisomy 21, 18, 13 Sex Chromosome Trisomy]
    S3 --> D3[e.g. Turner 45,X DiGeorge 22q11.2 Deletion]
    S5 --> D4[e.g. BCR-ABL Philadelphia CML]
    S6 --> D5[e.g. Subtelomeric Microdeletion Syndrome]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**Define cytogenetics. List any four classical banding techniques used in chromosome analysis.**

**Model Answer:**
Cytogenetics is the branch of genetics that studies the structure, number, and behavior of chromosomes in relation to heredity and disease. The four classical banding techniques are:

1. **G-banding** – Trypsin–Giemsa staining; reveals AT-rich dark bands; resolution ~400–550 bands/haploid karyotype.
2. **Q-banding** – Quinacrine fluorescence; AT-rich regions fluoresce brightly; detects Y chromosome polymorphisms.
3. **R-banding** – Reverse banding after heat denaturation; GC-rich regions appear dark.
4. **C-banding** – Ba(OH)₂ treatment; stains constitutive heterochromatin at centromeres and Yq.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**What is FISH? Mention any two of its clinical applications.**

**Model Answer:**
FISH (Fluorescence In Situ Hybridization) is a molecular cytogenetic technique in which a fluorescently labeled DNA probe is hybridized to denatured chromosomes or interphase nuclei to detect specific DNA sequences.

Two clinical applications:

1. **Rapid prenatal aneuploidy screening** — Enumeration probes for chromosomes 13, 18, 21, X, Y on uncultured amniocytes or chorionic villi (results in 24–48 h).
2. **Detection of BCR-ABL fusion in Chronic Myeloid Leukemia** — Dual-color, dual-fusion probe on bone-marrow metaphases confirms the Philadelphia translocation t(9;22)(q34;q11).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently (a) confuse FISH probes (CEP vs. WCP vs. locus-specific), (b) miscalculate log2 thresholds in Array-CGH, and (c) omit ISCN 2024 nomenclature in their final answer. Each of these can cost **2–3 marks** per sub-question.

### **Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the principle, procedure, and clinical applications of **Fluorescence In Situ Hybridization (FISH)**. Add a note on the different types of FISH probes.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the principle of **Comparative Genomic Hybridization (CGH)** and derive the log₂ ratio threshold values that distinguish a single-copy gain from a single-copy loss.

---

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Principle (2 marks):**
FISH relies on the complementary base-pairing between a fluorescently labeled **probe DNA** (40 kb – 1 Mb) and a denatured **target chromosome or nuclear DNA**. The hybridized duplex fluoresces under specific excitation wavelengths, allowing direct visualization of the locus of interest on metaphase chromosomes or interphase nuclei.

**Procedure (3 marks):**

1. **Slide preparation** — Drop metaphase spreads or interphase nuclei onto a clean glass slide.
2. **Denaturation** — Heat slide to **73 °C** in **70% formamide / 2× SSC** for 2–5 min.
3. **Probe hybridization** — Apply 10–20 µL labeled probe mixture; incubate at **37 °C** overnight (16–20 h) in a humidified chamber.
4. **Stringent washes** — Wash in **0.4× SSC / 0.3% NP-40 at 73 °C** for 2 min, followed by **2× SSC / 0.1% NP-40 at room temperature**.
5. **Counterstaining and visualization** — Apply DAPI counterstain; observe under an epifluorescence microscope with appropriate filter sets.

**Types of FISH Probes (1 mark):**

| Probe Type | Target | Application |
|---|---|---|
| CEP (centromere enumeration) | α-satellite repeats | Aneuploidy screening |
| WCP (whole chromosome paint) | Entire chromosome arm | Translocations, marker chromosomes |
| Locus-specific (BAC/PAC) | Single locus | Microdeletion syndromes |
| Telomere-specific | Subtelomeric regions | Idiopathic MR screening |
| M-FISH / SKY | All 24 chromosomes | Complex karyotypes |

**Clinical Applications (1 mark):**
- Prenatal aneuploidy detection (13, 18, 21, X, Y).
- Microdeletion syndromes (DiGeorge, Williams, Prader-Willi, Angelman).
- Cancer cytogenetics (BCR-ABL, MYC rearrangements, HER2 amplification).
- Bone-marrow transplant chimerism monitoring (XX/XY FISH).

---

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Principle of CGH (2 marks):**
Comparative Genomic Hybridization co-hybridizes differentially fluorochrome-labeled **test (patient) DNA** and **reference (normal) DNA** onto a normal metaphase spread. The fluorescence ratio along each chromosome reflects the relative copy number in the test genome.

**Derivation of log₂ thresholds (5 marks):**

Step 1 — Define the test/reference intensity ratio:

$$
R = \frac{I_{test}}{I_{ref}}
$$

Step 2 — For a single-copy gain (trisomy, $3:2$ ratio):

$$
\log_2 R = \log_2\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) = \frac{\log_{10}(3/2)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{0.1761}{0.3010} \approx +0.585
$$

Step 3 — For a single-copy loss (monosomy, $1:2$ ratio):

$$
\log_2 R = \log_2\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -1.000
$$

Step 4 — General formula for $k$ extra copies:

$$
\Delta = \log_2\left(\frac{2+k}{2}\right)
$$

Step 5 — Interpretation rule:
- $\Delta \geq +0.585$ → **Gain**
- $\Delta \leq -1.000$ → **Loss**
- Intermediate values → **Normal disomy** (with noise tolerance typically −0.3 to +0.3)

**Conclusion:** Array-CGH achieves a log₂-based digital readout that resolves copy-number imbalances as small as ~50–100 kb across the entire genome in a single hybridization.

---

### **Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, describe the **karyotyping procedure** for human peripheral blood lymphocytes. Add a note on the **ISCN nomenclature** with one example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **classical karyotyping**, **FISH**, and **Array-CGH** in terms of resolution, detectable abnormalities, sample requirement, cost, and clinical utility.

---

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Procedure of Lymphocyte Karyotyping (5 marks):**

1. **Blood collection** — 2–5 mL heparinized peripheral blood.
2. **Lymphocyte culture** — Set up RPMI-1640 medium + **10% fetal bovine serum** + **PHA (phytohemagglutinin, 1%)**; incubate at **37 °C** for **48–72 h**.
3. **Colcemid arrest** — Add **colcemid (0.1 µg/mL)** for **15–30 min** to arrest cells in metaphase.
4. **Hypotonic treatment** — 0.075 M KCl for 12–15 min at 37 °C to swell nuclei.
5. **Fixation** — 3:1 methanol:acetic acid (3–4 changes).
6. **Slide preparation** — Drop fixed cell suspension onto chilled, humidified slides; air-dry.
7. **G-banding** — Trypsin treatment (10–30 sec) followed by Giemsa staining.
8. **Microscopy** — Capture 15–20 metaphases; analyze 5 with karyotyping software.
9. **Reporting** — Use ISCN 2024 nomenclature.

**ISCN Nomenclature (2 marks):**

**Example:** 47,XY,+21

- Total chromosome count: 47
- Sex chromosome constitution: XY
- Gain of an entire chromosome 21 → indicates **Down syndrome (Trisomy 21)**

Another example: 46,XX,del(5)(p15)

- 46 chromosomes; female; deletion on chromosome 5 short arm at band p15 → consistent with **Cri-du-chat syndrome**.

---

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

| Parameter | Karyotyping | FISH | Array-CGH |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Resolution** | 5–10 Mb | 100 kb – 1 Mb | 50–100 kb |
| **Genome-wide coverage** | Yes (all chromosomes) | No (targeted) | Yes (whole genome) |
| **Detects aneuploidy** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| **Detects balanced translocations** | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Detects unbalanced rearrangements** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| **Detects CNVs / microdeletions** | Limited | Probe-dependent | ✓ |
| **Sample requirement** | Dividing cells | Dividing/interphase | DNA only |
| **Turnaround time** | 7–14 days | 1–3 days | 3–7 days |
| **Cost per sample** | Low (~₹3,000) | Moderate (~₹5,000/probe) | High (~₹15,000) |
| **Best clinical use** | First-tier test, balanced rearrangements | Targeted rapid diagnosis (CML, microdeletion) | First-tier for unexplained MR/MCA |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Each technique has distinct strengths. Modern diagnostic algorithms use karyotype as a screening step, FISH for rapid confirmation of suspected rearrangements, and Array-CGH as the gold standard for detecting sub-microscopic copy-number variants.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Cytogenetics** correlates chromosome structure with inherited and acquired human diseases.
- ✅ **G-banding** is the workhorse of routine karyotype analysis (~400–550 bands).
- ✅ **High-resolution prometaphase banding** pushes resolution to ~850 bands but still misses < 5 Mb events.
- ✅ **FISH probes** are of **5 major types**: CEP, WCP, locus-specific, telomere, and M-FISH/SKY.
- ✅ **FISH wash stringency** is governed by $T_m = 81.5 + 16.6 \log[Na^+] + 0.41\%GC - 600/L$.
- ✅ **Array-CGH log₂ thresholds**: gain at **+0.585**, loss at **−1.000** for single-copy changes.
- ✅ **CGH cannot detect balanced translocations or inversions** — a frequent exam trap.
- ✅ **NGS-based cytogenomics (LP-WGS, OGM)** detects SVs ≥ 500 bp and is the modern frontier.
- ✅ **ISCN 2024** is the standardized reporting language — use it in every answer.
- ✅ **Numerical aberrations** (Trisomy 21, Turner 45,X, Klinefelter 47,XXY) are detected by all cytogenetic platforms.
- ✅ **Structural aberrations** (Philadelphia t(9;22), DiGeorge del(22q11.2), Cri-du-chat del(5p)) require banding + FISH/CGH confirmation.
- ✅ **Cancer cytogenetics** relies on interphase FISH for rapid BCR-ABL, MYC, HER2 detection.
- ✅ **Prenatal diagnosis** uses rapid aneuploidy FISH on uncultured amniocytes (24–48 h).
- ✅ **Optical Genome Mapping** is the newest FDA-cleared platform for genome-wide SV detection at single-molecule resolution.

> **End of Module 4 Topic Notes — Cytogenetic Approaches for Studying Human Diseases**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBB861 - CYTOGENETICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cancer cytogenetics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cancer Cytogenetics** is the specialized branch of cytogenetics that studies the chromosomal and genomic alterations occurring in neoplastic (cancerous) cells, encompassing the structural and numerical aberrations of chromosomes that drive oncogenesis, tumor progression, metastasis, and therapeutic response.

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Cancer cytogenetics deals with the systematic analysis of chromosome-level mutations—including aneuploidies, translocations, deletions, duplications, and amplifications—that characterize malignant transformation. It bridges classical cytogenetics with molecular oncology to identify diagnostic, prognostic, and predictive biomarkers.*

According to the **International System for Human Cytogenomic Nomenclature (ISCN 2020)**, cancer karyotypes are described using a standardized notation that captures both clonal evolution and chromosomal architecture.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Damaged Library Books" Analogy

Imagine a vast library containing 46 unique "instruction books" (chromosomes) that together guide how a cell behaves. Each book has chapters (genes) that tell the cell when to grow, when to rest, and when to die.

- **Healthy cells** = The library is perfectly organized, with 23 pairs of books neatly arranged on shelves (diploid karyotype: 46,XX or 46,XY).
- **Cancer cells** = The library falls into chaos:
  - **Missing books** (monosomy) → critical instructions are lost
  - **Extra books** (trisomy, polysomy) → instructions are over-amplified
  - **Torn pages swapped between books** (translocations) → wrong instructions get activated
  - **Chapters deleted** (deletions) → safety controls are removed

**Cancer cytogeneticists** are the "library detectives" who read these broken books (using microscopes, fluorescent probes, and sequencing) to figure out exactly which instructions went wrong—and importantly, how to fix them.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Cancer is fundamentally a **chromosomal disease**. Over 90% of solid tumors and nearly 100% of hematological malignancies display detectable cytogenetic abnormalities.

## 1.3 Historical Milestones in Cancer Cytogenetics

| Year | Discovery | Significance |
|------|-----------|--------------|
| 1914 | Boveri's "Theory of Cancer" | Proposed chromosome imbalance causes cancer |
| 1960 | Philadelphia Chromosome (Nowell & Hungerford) | First consistent cancer-specific translocation t(9;22) |
| 1973 | Rowley identifies t(9;22) as translocation | Refined CML understanding |
| 1980s | Oncogene identification (MYC, RAS) | Linked cytogenetics to molecular oncology |
| 1990s | FISH and CGH technologies | Enabled detection of sub-microscopic aberrations |
| 2000s+ | NGS and chromosomal microarrays | Genome-wide resolution of cancer karyotypes |

## 1.4 Why Cancer Cytogenetics Matters in Modern Medicine

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clinical Triad of Cancer Cytogenetics:**
> 1. **Diagnosis** — Specific translocations define leukemia subtypes (e.g., t(15;17) for Acute Promyelocytic Leukemia)
> 2. **Prognosis** — Cytogenetic abnormalities stratify risk (e.g., del(5q) in MDS has favorable prognosis)
> 3. **Therapy Selection** — Targeted drugs depend on cytogenetic context (e.g., Imatinib for BCR-ABL positive CML)

The **clonal evolution theory** (Nowell, 1976) posits that cancer arises from a single transformed cell that acquires successive genetic changes, leading to heterogeneous subpopulations—a process that **chromosomal instability (CIN)** accelerates dramatically.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Categories of Cancer Chromosomal Aberrations

### A. Numerical Aberrations (Aneuploidies)

These involve deviation from the normal diploid number (2n = 46 in humans).

**Classification of Numerical Aberrations:**

$$\text{Aneuploidy Types} = \begin{cases} \text{Hyperdiploid} & : 47-57 \text{ chromosomes} \\ \text{Near-tetraploid} & : 82-94 \text{ chromosomes} \\ \text{Near-triploid} & : 60-78 \text{ chromosomes} \\ \text{Hypodiploid} & : 35-45 \text{ chromosomes} \end{cases}$$

**Specific Numerical Aberrations:**
- **Monosomy** — Loss of one chromosome (2n−1)
- **Trisomy** — Gain of one chromosome (2n+1)
- **Tetrasomy** — Gain of two extra copies (2n+2)
- **Nullisomy** — Loss of both homologs (lethal in autosomes)

### B. Structural Aberrations

These involve physical alterations to chromosome architecture.

| Aberration Type | Description | Cancer Example |
|-----------------|-------------|----------------|
| **Deletion (del)** | Loss of chromosome segment | del(5q) in MDS |
| **Duplication (dup)** | Extra copy of segment | dup(1q) in neuroblastoma |
| **Inversion (inv)** | Segment reversed end-to-end | inv(16) in AML-M4Eo |
| **Translocation (t)** | Segment moved between chromosomes | t(9;22) in CML |
| **Ring chromosome (r)** | Both ends fused, central deletion | r(17) in medulloblastoma |
| **Isochromosome (i)** | Mirror-image arms | i(17q) in CML-AP |
| **Dicentric (dic)** | Two centromeres | dic(9;20) in ALL |

## 2.2 Mechanism of Chromosomal Translocations

Translocations arise primarily through:
1. **Double-strand DNA breaks (DSBs)** — ionizing radiation, replication stress
2. **Misrepair by Non-Homologous End Joining (NHEJ)** — error-prone repair pathway
3. **Spatial proximity in the nucleus** — "chromosome territories" facilitate translocations

> [!NOTE]
> **Reciprocal vs. Robertsonian Translocations:**
> - **Reciprocal** = Exchange of segments between non-homologous chromosomes (e.g., t(9;22))
> - **Robertsonian** = Fusion of two acrocentric chromosomes at centromere (rare in cancer, common in constitutional disorders)

## 2.3 Oncogenes and Tumor Suppressors at Chromosomal Level

### The Classic Two-Hit Hypothesis (Knudson, 1971)

For tumor suppressor genes (TSGs), both alleles must be inactivated:

$$\text{Cancer Phenotype} = f(\text{Mutation}_1 + \text{Mutation}_2)$$

**Mechanisms of TSG Inactivation:**
- **Loss of Heterozygosity (LOH)** — Loss of wild-type allele
- **Promoter hypermethylation** — Epigenetic silencing
- **Point mutations** — Inactivation of remaining allele
- **Deletion** — Physical removal via chromosomal deletion

### Functional Consequences of Translocations

**Two Primary Outcomes:**
1. **Creation of chimeric fusion genes** — e.g., BCR-ABL from t(9;22)
2. **Deregulation of proto-oncogenes** — e.g., MYC overexpression in Burkitt lymphoma t(8;14)

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Reference Tables

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential Reference Data for KTU Examinations:**

| Parameter | Formula/Value | Units | Application |
|-----------|---------------|-------|-------------|
| **Normal Human Diploid Number** | $2n = 46$ | chromosomes | Reference karyotype baseline |
| **Haploid Number** | $n = 23$ | chromosomes | Gamete constitution |
| **DNA Content (Normal G1)** | $6.6 \text{ pg}$ | picograms | Flow cytometry reference |
| **DNA Content (G2/M)** | $13.2 \text{ pg}$ | picograms | Doubled pre-mitosis |
| **DNA Index (DI)** | $\text{DI} = \frac{\text{Tumor G0/G1 Peak}}{\text{Diploid G0/G1 Peak}}$ | ratio | Ploidy assessment |
| **S-Phase Fraction (SPF)** | $\text{SPF} = \frac{\text{S-phase cells}}{\text{Total tumor cells}} \times 100\%$ | percent | Proliferation marker |
| **Mitotic Index (MI)** | $\text{MI} = \frac{\text{Number of mitoses}}{\text{Total cells counted}} \times 1000$ | per mille | Cell division activity |
| **Chromosome Instability Index** | $\text{CIN} = \frac{\text{Aberrant metaphases}}{\text{Total metaphases analyzed}}$ | fraction | Genomic stability marker |

### Critical Reference: Common Cancer-Defining Translocations

| Translocation | Fusion Gene | Cancer Type | Clinical Relevance |
|---------------|-------------|-------------|---------------------|
| t(9;22)(q34;q11) | **BCR-ABL1** | Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML) | Imatinib/Gleevec target |
| t(15;17)(q22;q12) | **PML-RARA** | Acute Promyelocytic Leukemia (APL/AML-M3) | ATRA (All-trans retinoic acid) |
| t(8;14)(q24;q32) | **MYC-IgH** | Burkitt Lymphoma | Aggressive B-cell neoplasm |
| t(14;18)(q32;q21) | **IGH-BCL2** | Follicular Lymphoma | Anti-apoptotic pathway |
| t(11;22)(q24;q12) | **EWSR1-FLI1** | Ewing Sarcoma | Pediatric bone tumor |
| t(2;13)(q35;q14) | **PAX3-FOXO1** | Alveolar Rhabdomyosarcoma | Muscle lineage tumor |
| t(12;21)(p13;q22) | **ETV6-RUNX1** | B-ALL (children) | Favorable prognosis |
| t(4;11)(q21;q23) | **MLL-AFF1 (AF4)** | Infant ALL | Poor prognosis |
| inv(16)(p13.1q22) | **CBFB-MYH11** | AML-M4Eo | Favorable prognosis |
| t(6;9)(p23;q34) | **DEK-NUP214** | AML | Poor prognosis |
| del(5q) | **RPS14, miR-145** | MDS (5q- syndrome) | Lenalidomide-responsive |
| del(13q14) | **RB1, miR-15/16** | CLL | Favorable when sole aberration |
| del(17p13) | **TP53** | CLL, AML, many cancers | Poor prognosis, chemo-refractory |
| i(17q) | **TP53 loss** | CML blast crisis | Disease progression marker |

## 2.5 Solid Tumor Cytogenetics — Key Patterns

> [!NOTE]
> **Differences Between Hematological and Solid Tumor Cytogenetics:**

| Feature | Hematological Cancers | Solid Tumors |
|---------|----------------------|--------------|
| **Karyotype simplicity** | Often single dominant clone | Complex, heterogeneous |
| **Recurrent translocations** | Common and diagnostic | Less frequent |
| **Mitotic cells available** | Bone marrow easily sampled | Often difficult to culture |
| **Ploidy** | Mostly near-diploid | Frequently aneuploid |
| **CIN prevalence** | Moderate | Very high |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering and Clinical Utility

Cancer cytogenetics has direct applications in:
- **Companion Diagnostics** — Tests paired with targeted therapies
- **Minimal Residual Disease (MRD) Monitoring** — Detecting low-level cancer cells
- **Pre-implantation Genetic Diagnosis (PGD)** for hereditary cancer syndromes (BRCA1/2, TP53)
- **Forensic identification** of cancer cell lines (STR profiling for authenticity)
- **Drug development pipelines** targeting fusion oncoproteins
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1: Determining DNA Ploidy from Flow Cytometry Data

> **Problem:** A flow cytometry analysis of a tumor sample shows the tumor G0/G1 peak at channel **180**, while the normal diploid reference (peripheral blood lymphocytes) peaks at channel **120**. Calculate the **DNA Index (DI)** and determine the ploidy classification.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Recall the DNA Index formula from the Formula Sheet:

$$\text{DI} = \frac{\text{Tumor G0/G1 Peak Channel}}{\text{Diploid G0/G1 Peak Channel}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the measured values:

$$\text{DI} = \frac{180}{120}$$

**Step 3:** Perform the division:

$$\text{DI} = 1.5$$

**Step 4:** Interpret using standard classification rules:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ploidy Classification:}\\
\text{DI} &= 0.90 - 1.10 \rightarrow \text{Diploid (normal)} \\
\text{DI} &= 1.11 - 1.89 \rightarrow \text{Hyperdiploid (aneuploid)} \\
\text{DI} &= 1.90 - 2.10 \rightarrow \text{Tetraploid} \\
\text{DI} &> 2.10 \rightarrow \text{Hypertetraploid (polyploid)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Since $\text{DI} = 1.5$, the tumor is classified as **Hyperdiploid Aneuploid** (chromosome count approximately $\approx 69$, i.e., near-triploid).

**Step 6:** Estimate chromosome number:

$$2n_{\text{estimated}} = 46 \times \text{DI} = 46 \times 1.5 = 69 \text{ chromosomes}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Significance:** Hyperdiploid ALL (chromosomes 51–67) in pediatric patients carries a **favorable prognosis**, while hypodiploid ALL (< 44 chromosomes) has **poor prognosis**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2: Chromosomal Instability Index Calculation

> **Problem:** During karyotyping of a breast cancer specimen, 100 metaphases were analyzed. Of these, 62 showed structural aberrations, 18 had numerical abnormalities, and 10 displayed both types. Calculate the **Chromosome Instability Index (CIN)**.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Define the formula:

$$\text{CIN} = \frac{\text{Total aberrant metaphases}}{\text{Total metaphases analyzed}}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate total aberrant metaphases using set union (avoiding double counting):

$$N_{\text{aberrant}} = N_{\text{structural}} + N_{\text{numerical}} - N_{\text{both}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:

$$N_{\text{aberrant}} = 62 + 18 - 10 = 70 \text{ metaphases}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the CIN formula:

$$\text{CIN} = \frac{70}{100} = 0.70 \text{ or } 70\%$$

**Step 5:** Interpret the result:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CIN Interpretation Scale:}\\
\text{CIN} &< 0.20 \rightarrow \text{Low instability (genomically stable)} \\
\text{CIN} &= 0.20 - 0.50 \rightarrow \text{Moderate instability} \\
\text{CIN} &> 0.50 \rightarrow \text{High instability (chromosomally unstable)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Conclusion: This tumor exhibits **High Chromosomal Instability (CIN-High)** phenotype, typical of aggressive solid tumors.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Problem 3: Loss of Heterozygosity (LOH) Quantification

> **Problem:** In a paired normal-tumor comparison at microsatellite marker **D17S796** (located near TP53 on 17p13), the following allele peak heights were obtained:
> 
> - **Normal tissue:** Allele A = 800 RFU, Allele B = 820 RFU  
> - **Tumor tissue:** Allele A = 750 RFU, Allele B = 120 RFU  
> 
> Calculate the LOH score and interpret.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Apply the standard LOH formula:

$$\text{LOH Score} = \frac{(\text{Tumor Allele A} / \text{Tumor Allele B})}{(\text{Normal Allele A} / \text{Normal Allele B})}$$

**Step 2:** Compute tumor allele ratio:

$$\text{Tumor ratio} = \frac{750}{120} = 6.25$$

**Step 3:** Compute normal allele ratio:

$$\text{Normal ratio} = \frac{800}{820} = 0.9756$$

**Step 4:** Calculate LOH score:

$$\text{LOH Score} = \frac{6.25}{0.9756} \approx 6.41$$

**Step 5:** Apply interpretation criteria:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LOH Score} &= 0 - 1.5 \rightarrow \text{No LOH (heterozygous retained)} \\
\text{LOH Score} &> 2.0 \text{ or } < 0.5 \rightarrow \text{LOH present} \\
\text{LOH Score} &= 2.0 - 5.0 \rightarrow \text{Suggestive LOH} \\
\text{LOH Score} &> 5.0 \rightarrow \text{Definite LOH}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Conclusion: **Definite LOH at 17p13 (TP53 locus)** — Allele B is lost in tumor tissue. This finding suggests **TP53 inactivation**, a hallmark of many malignancies.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Problem 4: Karyotype Nomenclature Interpretation (ISCN 2020)

> **Problem:** A patient's bone marrow karyotype is reported as: **46,XY,t(9;22)(q34;q11.2)[15]/47,sl,+8[5]**
> 
> Interpret this nomenclature completely.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Identify the stemline karyotype (most frequent clone):

$$46,\text{XY},t(9;22)(q34;q11.2)[15]$$

- **46** = Total chromosome count
- **XY** = Male sex chromosomes
- **t(9;22)** = Reciprocal translocation between chromosomes 9 and 22
- **(q34;q11.2)** = Breakpoints at 9q34 and 22q11.2
- **[15]** = Found in 15 metaphases examined

**Step 2:** Identify the sideline (subclone):

$$47,\text{sl},+8[5]$$

- **47** = One extra chromosome (aneuploid)
- **sl** = "Sideline" — derived from stemline
- **+8** = Trisomy 8 (extra copy of chromosome 8)
- **[5]** = Found in 5 metaphases examined

**Step 3:** Interpretation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total metaphases} &= 15 + 5 = 20 \\
\text{Stemline frequency} &= \frac{15}{20} = 75\% \\
\text{Sideline frequency} &= \frac{5}{20} = 25\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Clinical Correlation:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This karyotype is diagnostic of **Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML)** in **accelerated phase** or **chronic phase with clonal evolution**:
> - **t(9;22)(q34;q11.2)** → **Philadelphia chromosome (Ph)** → **BCR-ABL1 fusion**
> - **+8 (Trisomy 8)** → Additional cytogenetic abnormality, indicating **clonal evolution** and **disease progression**

---

## 3.5 Analytical Problem 5: Mitotic Index and Proliferation Assessment

> **Problem:** A pathologist counts **1500 cells** in a tumor section and identifies **84 mitoses**. Calculate the Mitotic Index (MI) and classify the proliferative activity.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Apply the formula:

$$\text{MI} = \frac{\text{Mitoses counted}}{\text{Total cells}} \times 1000$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$\text{MI} = \frac{84}{1500} \times 1000$$

**Step 3:** Calculate:

$$\text{MI} = 0.056 \times 1000 = 56 \text{ mitoses per 1000 cells}$$

**Step 4:** Interpret using tumor grading criteria:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MI} &< 10 \rightarrow \text{Low proliferative activity (Grade 1)} \\
\text{MI} &= 10-30 \rightarrow \text{Moderate proliferative activity (Grade 2)} \\
\text{MI} &> 30 \rightarrow \text{High proliferative activity (Grade 3)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Conclusion: **MI = 56**, indicating **High proliferative activity (Grade 3 tumor)** — associated with aggressive clinical behavior.

---

## 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Diagnostic Methods in Cancer Cytogenetics

| Method | Resolution | Target | Turnaround | Clinical Use |
|--------|-----------|--------|-----------|--------------|
| **Conventional Karyotyping** | $\sim 5-10 \text{ Mb}$ | Whole genome | 7-14 days | Clonal aberration detection |
| **FISH (Fluorescence In Situ Hybridization)** | $\sim 100-500 \text{ kb}$ | Specific loci | 1-3 days | Known translocation confirmation |
| **CGH (Comparative Genomic Hybridization)** | $\sim 5-10 \text{ Mb}$ | Copy number changes | 3-5 days | Solid tumor profiling |
| **Array CGH / SNP arrays** | $\sim 50-100 \text{ kb}$ | Copy number + LOH | 3-5 days | High-resolution CNV detection |
| **Karyotyping + Spectral Karyotyping (SKY)** | Chromosome-level | All chromosomes (24 colors) | 7-10 days | Complex rearrangements |
| **NGS (Next-Generation Sequencing)** | Single base | Mutations, fusions, CNVs | 5-10 days | Comprehensive genomic profiling |
| **Optical Genome Mapping (OGM)** | $\sim 500 \text{ bp}$ | Structural variants | 3-5 days | Complex structural variants |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Cancer Cytogenetics and List Its Three Main Clinical Applications. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Cancer cytogenetics is the branch of cytogenetics that studies chromosomal alterations (numerical and structural aberrations) in neoplastic cells to understand the genetic basis of cancer initiation, progression, and response to therapy.

**Three Main Clinical Applications:**
1. **Diagnosis** — Identification of cancer-specific chromosomal abnormalities (e.g., Philadelphia chromosome in CML)
2. **Prognosis** — Risk stratification based on cytogenetic findings (e.g., del(17p) confers poor prognosis in CLL)
3. **Therapeutic Guidance** — Selection of targeted therapies (e.g., Imatinib for BCR-ABL1 positive CML)

---

### Question 2: What is the Philadelphia Chromosome? Mention Its Molecular Consequence. `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Philadelphia Chromosome (Ph)** is a structurally abnormal chromosome 22 resulting from a reciprocal translocation between chromosomes 9 and 22, designated **t(9;22)(q34;q11.2)**.

**Molecular Consequence:**
- The translocation juxtaposes the **ABL1 proto-oncogene** (from chromosome 9) with the **BCR gene** (from chromosome 22)
- This creates a **BCR-ABL1 fusion gene** that encodes a **constitutively active tyrosine kinase** protein (p210 in CML, p190 in Ph+ ALL)
- The fusion protein drives uncontrolled myeloid proliferation by activating multiple downstream signaling pathways (RAS, JAK-STAT, PI3K-AKT)
- Discovered by **Nowell and Hungerford (1960)** as the first consistent cancer-specific chromosomal abnormality

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A: Comprehensive Discussion on Cancer Cytogenetics

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the different types of chromosomal aberrations observed in cancer cells. Provide at least four specific examples with their associated malignancies.

> **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the molecular mechanisms of oncogene activation through chromosomal translocations, using BCR-ABL1 and MYC-IgH as detailed examples.

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Introduction:**
Cancer cells display two principal classes of chromosomal aberrations: **Numerical (Aneuploidies)** and **Structural Rearrangements**. These alterations arise due to defects in DNA repair, mitotic checkpoint failure, and telomere dysfunction.

**A. Numerical Aberrations:**

**1. Monosomy** — Loss of one chromosome
   - *Example:* **Monosomy 7** in Myelodysplastic Syndrome (MDS) and AML — confers poor prognosis

**2. Trisomy** — Gain of one chromosome
   - *Example:* **Trisomy 8** in AML, MDS, and CML progression
   - *Example:* **Trisomy 12** in Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL)

**3. Hyperdiploidy** — Gain of multiple chromosomes (51–67)
   - *Example:* **Hyperdiploid B-ALL** in pediatric patients — favorable prognosis

**B. Structural Aberrations:**

**1. Reciprocal Translocation**
   - *Example:* **t(9;22)(q34;q11.2)** — Philadelphia chromosome in CML (BCR-ABL1 fusion)

**2. Deletion**
   - *Example:* **del(5q)** — "5q- syndrome" in MDS, responsive to Lenalidomide
   - *Example:* **del(17p13)** — TP53 loss in CLL, poor prognosis

**3. Inversion**
   - *Example:* **inv(16)(p13.1q22)** — CBFB-MYH11 fusion in AML-M4Eo

**4. Isochromosome**
   - *Example:* **i(17q)** — Loss of 17p in CML blast crisis

**5. Ring Chromosome**
   - *Example:* **r(17)** in Medulloblastoma

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Mechanism of Oncogene Activation via Translocations:**

Chromosomal translocations activate proto-oncogenes through two principal mechanisms:

**Mechanism 1: Creation of Fusion Genes (Chimeric Oncoproteins)**

**Example — BCR-ABL1 in CML:**
- The **t(9;22)** translocation fuses the **BCR** gene (chromosome 22q11.2) with the **ABL1** gene (chromosome 9q34)
- The BCR-ABL1 fusion protein has constitutive tyrosine kinase activity (compared to tightly regulated wild-type ABL1)
- Three isoforms exist: **p210** (CML), **p190** (Ph+ ALL), **p230** (neutrophilic CML)
- The fusion protein activates downstream pathways: **RAS-MAPK**, **JAK-STAT**, **PI3K-AKT**
- Clinical target: **Imatinib (Gleevec)** — specific BCR-ABL1 kinase inhibitor

**Mechanism 2: Deregulation of Proto-oncogene Expression**

**Example — MYC-IgH in Burkitt Lymphoma:**
- The **t(8;14)(q24;q32)** translocation places the **MYC** proto-oncogene (chromosome 8q24) under the control of the **Immunoglobulin Heavy Chain (IgH)** enhancer (chromosome 14q32)
- Normally, MYC expression is tightly regulated; the IgH enhancer causes **constitutive overexpression**
- This drives uncontrolled B-cell proliferation
- Variant translocations: **t(2;8)** — MYC with kappa light chain; **t(8;22)** — MYC with lambda light chain

**Three-Signal Model of MYC Oncogenesis:**
1. Sustained proliferative signaling
2. Evasion of growth suppressors
3. Enabling replicative immortality

---

### Question B: Alternative Comprehensive Question on Cancer Cytogenetics

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the Philadelphia chromosome in detail — its discovery, formation, molecular consequence, and clinical significance in CML management.

> **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast conventional karyotyping, FISH, and NGS in cancer cytogenetics. Discuss advantages, limitations, and clinical applications of each.

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**1. Historical Discovery (1960):**
- Discovered by **Peter Nowell and David Hungerford** at the University of Pennsylvania
- First recurrent chromosomal abnormality identified in any human cancer
- Initially described as a "minute chromosome" — later identified as a deleted chromosome 22

**2. Formation Mechanism:**
- **Reciprocal translocation t(9;22)(q34;q11.2)**
- Double-strand breaks occur in chromosomes 9 (at ABL1 locus) and 22 (at BCR locus)
- **Non-Homologous End Joining (NHEJ)** mis-repairs these breaks, creating the derivative chromosomes der(9) and der(22)
- The **derivative chromosome 22** = **Philadelphia Chromosome (Ph)**

**3. Molecular Consequence:**
- **BCR-ABL1 fusion gene** formed on derivative chromosome 22
- Fusion protein: Constitutively active **non-receptor tyrosine kinase**
- Altered substrate specificity and constitutive activation
- Activates multiple oncogenic signaling cascades:
  - **RAS-MAPK pathway** → proliferation
  - **JAK-STAT pathway** → survival
  - **PI3K-AKT pathway** → anti-apoptosis
  - **MYC activation** → cell cycle progression

**4. Clinical Significance:**
- **Diagnostic marker** for CML (>95% of cases)
- Confirmed by karyotyping, FISH, or RT-PCR for BCR-ABL1 transcript
- **Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitors (TKIs)** revolutionized treatment:
  - **Imatinib** (1st generation)
  - **Dasatinib, Nilotinib** (2nd generation)
  - **Ponatinib, Asciminib** (3rd generation)
- **Monitoring:** Quantitative RT-PCR for BCR-ABL1 transcript levels (IS - International Scale)
- **Resistance mechanisms:** BCR-ABL1 kinase domain mutations (T315I), gene amplification

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Conventional Karyotyping | FISH | NGS |
|---------|------------------------|------|-----|
| **Resolution** | 5-10 Mb | 100-500 kb | Single base pair |
| **Targets** | Whole genome | Specific loci | Mutations, fusions, CNVs |
| **Turnaround** | 7-14 days | 1-3 days | 5-10 days |
| **Cell requirement** | Dividing cells | Any cell | Any cell |
| **Detects** | Balanced and unbalanced aberrations | Known specific changes | All sequence variants |
| **Cost** | Moderate | Low per probe | High |
| **Expertise** | Cytogeneticist | Cytogeneticist | Molecular pathologist |

**Conventional Karyotyping:**
- **Advantages:** Genome-wide view, detects novel/unexpected aberrations, identifies clonal evolution
- **Limitations:** Requires dividing cells, limited resolution (5-10 Mb), culture artifacts
- **Applications:** Initial diagnostic workup of hematological malignancies

**FISH:**
- **Advantages:** High sensitivity, rapid, works on interphase nuclei, detects cryptic aberrations
- **Limitations:** Only targets known regions, probe design dependent, misses novel fusions
- **Applications:** Confirming specific translocations (BCR-ABL1, PML-RARA), MRD monitoring, HER2 amplification in breast cancer

**NGS:**
- **Advantages:** Comprehensive mutation profiling, detects rare variants, identifies novel fusion partners
- **Limitations:** High cost, complex bioinformatics, longer turnaround, doesn't preserve spatial context
- **Applications:** Targeted cancer panels, whole-exome/genome sequencing, liquid biopsy

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> ⚠️ **Pitfall 1: Confusing Balanced vs Unbalanced Translocations**
> - Reciprocal (balanced) translocations like t(9;22) do NOT change chromosome NUMBER but create FUSION genes
> - Students often incorrectly state that "translocations change chromosome count"
> 
> ⚠️ **Pitfall 2: ISCN Notation Errors**
> - Breakpoints must be cited accurately: t(9;22)(q34;q11.2) — NOT t(9;22)(q34;q11)
> - Semicolons separate chromosomes, commas separate breakpoints
> - Always include the number of metaphases examined in square brackets
> 
> ⚠️ **Pitfall 3: Mixing up Gene Roles**
> - BCR-ABL1 = **fusion oncoprotein** (driver mutation)
> - TP53 mutations = **loss of tumor suppressor** (NOT an oncogene activation)
> - Students often confuse the mechanistic categories
> 
> ⚠️ **Pitfall 4: Missing the Clinical Correlation**
> - KTU examiners EXPECT clinical relevance in cancer cytogenetics answers
> - Always mention: Diagnosis + Prognosis + Therapy implications
> - Example: "t(15;17) PML-RARA → APL → ATRA therapy"
> 
> ⚠️ **Pitfall 5: Forgetting Chromosome Position Specificity**
> - Philadelphia chromosome = **22q11.2** BCR + **9q34** ABL1 (specific bands!)
> - Generic answers without breakpoint positions lose marks

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY — CANCER CYTOGENETICS**

### Core Concepts:
- ☐ Cancer cytogenetics = study of chromosomal changes in malignant cells
- ☐ Two major aberration classes: **Numerical (Aneuploidies)** + **Structural (Translocations, Deletions, Inversions)**
- ☐ Normal human karyotype: **46,XX or 46,XY** (2n = 46)
- ☐ Philadelphia chromosome = **t(9;22)(q34;q11.2)** — first cancer-specific translocation (1960)
- ☐ BCR-ABL1 fusion protein = constitutive tyrosine kinase → CML

### Critical Translocations (MUST MEMORIZE):
- ☐ **t(9;22)(q34;q11.2)** → BCR-ABL1 → **CML** (Imatinib)
- ☐ **t(15;17)(q22;q12)** → PML-RARA → **APL/AML-M3** (ATRA)
- ☐ **t(8;14)(q24;q32)** → MYC-IgH → **Burkitt Lymphoma**
- ☐ **t(14;18)(q32;q21)** → IgH-BCL2 → **Follicular Lymphoma**
- ☐ **t(11;22)(q24;q12)** → EWSR1-FLI1 → **Ewing Sarcoma**
- ☐ **inv(16)(p13.1q22)** → CBFB-MYH11 → **AML-M4Eo** (favorable)
- ☐ **t(12;21)(p13;q22)** → ETV6-RUNX1 → **Childhood B-ALL** (favorable)
- ☐ **t(4;11)(q21;q23)** → MLL-AFF1 → **Infant ALL** (poor prognosis)

### Key Deletions:
- ☐ **del(5q)** → 5q- syndrome (MDS, Lenalidomide-responsive)
- ☐ **del(13q14)** → CLL (favorable when sole aberration)
- ☐ **del(17p13)** → TP53 loss (poor prognosis, chemo-refractory)

### Essential Formulas:
- ☐ **DNA Index (DI)** = Tumor peak / Normal peak (channel ratio)
- ☐ **S-Phase Fraction (SPF)** = S-phase cells / Total cells × 100%
- ☐ **Mitotic Index (MI)** = Mitoses / Total cells × 1000
- ☐ **CIN Index** = Aberrant metaphases / Total metaphases
- ☐ **Estimated Chromosome Number** = 46 × DI

### Three Pillars of Clinical Application:
- ☐ **Diagnosis** — Disease-specific chromosomal signatures
- ☐ **Prognosis** — Risk stratification (favorable vs. adverse cytogenetics)
- ☐ **Therapy** — Targeted drug selection (TKIs, ATRA, etc.)

### Two-Hit Hypothesis (Knudson 1971):
- ☐ Tumor suppressors require BOTH alleles to be inactivated
- ☐ First hit: germline/somatic mutation; Second hit: LOH/deletion/methylation

### Methods Comparison:
- ☐ Karyotyping: Whole genome, low resolution, dividing cells needed
- ☐ FISH: Targeted, rapid, interphase analysis possible
- ☐ NGS: Base-pair resolution, comprehensive, expensive
- ☐ CGH/Array: Copy number variants, no structural info

### ISCN 2020 Notation Rules:
- ☐ Comma separates items: 46,XY
- ☐ Semicolon separates chromosomes in translocations: t(9;22)
- ☐ Brackets indicate metaphase count: [20]
- ☐ "sl" = sideline (subclone); "idem" = same as previous clone
- ☐ +/- signs precede chromosome numbers for gains/losses: +8, -7

### Exam Strategy Tips:
- ☐ Always cite **breakpoint bands** in translocations (q34, q11.2, etc.)
- ☐ Connect every abnormality to its **clinical context**
- ☐ Mention **prognostic impact** (favorable vs adverse)
- ☐ Discuss **targeted therapies** when applicable
- ☐ Use **ISCN notation** correctly for karyotype interpretation

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBB861 - CYTOGENETICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fluorescence In Situ Hybridization (FISH)** is a molecular cytogenetic technique that uses fluorescently-labeled DNA (or RNA) probes to detect, localize, and quantify specific nucleic acid sequences *in situ* — that is, directly within fixed cells, tissue sections, or chromosome preparations mounted on a glass slide.

The term breaks down cleanly:

- **Fluorescence**: emission of visible light by a substance (fluorochrome) that has absorbed light of a shorter wavelength.
- **In situ**: literally "in its original place" — Latin.
- **Hybridization**: complementary base-pairing between a labeled single-stranded **probe** and its target single-stranded **DNA/RNA** sequence inside the cell.

> [!NOTE]
> **First developed** in the late 1970s–early 1980s as a non-radioactive alternative to isotopic in situ hybridization. Pioneered using biotin-labeled probes detected with fluorescent avidin. Modern multi-color FISH (MFISH) was developed in the 1990s, enabling simultaneous visualization of all 24 human chromosomes in distinct pseudo-colors.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Chromosomal GPS Highlighter"

Imagine a giant **photocopy of a thick book** (the chromosome) where you need to find a particular **sentence** (a specific gene) hidden among millions of words. Classical staining (G-banding) lets you read the chapters by their visual pattern, but it cannot pick out a single sentence.

FISH solves this by:

1. Manufacturing a synthetic, glowing **highlight pen** (the fluorescent probe) whose ink contains the *exact wording* of the sentence you want to find.
2. **Stripping** the original book so it can bond with the highlight pen (denaturation of chromosomal DNA).
3. Pressing the glowing pen against the page — it sticks *only* where that sentence exists (complementary base pairing).
4. Shining the right **color of light** to make the highlight glow, then taking a photograph through a **special filter**.

That glow — a discrete, colored dot on a dark chromosome — tells you: *"Your sentence is here."* Multiple glowing pens in different colors reveal several sentences at once.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The technique relies on a few measurable parameters:

- **Probe size**: typically **100 kb – 1 Mb** (locus-specific) up to whole chromosomes (chromosome painting, ~50–250 Mb).
- **Hybridization temperature**: usually **37 °C – 42 °C** for overnight incubation; posthybridization washes at **42 °C – 72 °C**.
- **Stringency** is controlled by the **percentage of formamide (typically 50 %)** in the hybridization mix; higher formamide = higher stringency.
- **Resolution of FISH**: ~ **100 kb – 1 Mb** for metaphase chromosomes, but can reach **~50–200 kb** on interphase nuclei, and **single-gene level** on extended DNA fibers (Fiber-FISH).
- **Fluorochrome Stokes shift**: typically **20–100 nm** between excitation and emission peaks.

## 1.4 Why FISH Matters in Modern Cytogenetics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> FISH bridges the gap between classical karyotyping (resolution ~5–10 Mb) and molecular DNA sequencing. It can detect:
> - **Microdeletions/microduplications** invisible to G-banding.
> - **Translocations** masked by similar banding patterns.
> - **Aneuploidies** directly in interphase cells (no need for dividing cells).
> - **Gene amplifications** (e.g., HER2/*ERBB2* in breast cancer).

This makes FISH indispensable in **cancer diagnostics, prenatal screening, preimplantation genetics, and microbial ecology**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Molecular Principle

The reaction at the heart of FISH is the **Watson–Crick base-pairing** between two complementary, antiparallel single-stranded polynucleotides:

$$
\text{Probe (ssDNA)} + \text{Target (ssDNA)} \;\xrightarrow[\text{cooling}]{T_m}\, \text{Probe:Target heteroduplex}
$$

For a perfect 100 % match, the thermodynamic stability of the duplex is governed by its **melting temperature** $T_m$, given approximately by:

$$
T_m = 81.5 + 16.6 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{[\text{Na}^+]}{1}\right) + 0.41(\%GC) - \frac{600}{L}
$$

where:

- $[\text{Na}^+]$ = molar monovalent cation concentration (mol/L)
- $\%GC$ = percentage of guanine + cytosine bases
- $L$ = length of the probe in base pairs

The hybridization is performed at **T_opt ≈ T_m − 25 °C**, allowing the probe to find and anneal to its complement while still tolerating modest mismatches.

## 2.2 Probe Types — The Three Pillars of FISH

| Probe Category | Target | Typical Size | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Centromere Repeat (CEP)** | Alpha-satellite DNA at centromere | ~0.1–1 Mb repeats | Enumerate chromosomes (aneuploidy) |
| **Locus-Specific (LSI)** | A defined gene/region | 100 kb – 1 Mb | Detect deletions, duplications, translocations (e.g., BCR-ABL) |
| **Whole Chromosome Paint (WCP)** | Entire chromosome arm(s) | 50–250 Mb | Identify translocation partners, marker chromosomes |
| **Telomere** | Subtelomeric repeats | ~100–300 kb | Detect cryptic telomeric rearrangements |

## 2.3 Fluorochromes Used in FISH

> [!NOTE]
> A fluorochrome absorbs light at an **excitation wavelength** $\lambda_{ex}$ and emits at a longer **emission wavelength** $\lambda_{em}$ (Stokes shift).

| Fluorochrome | $\lambda_{ex}$ (nm) | $\lambda_{em}$ (nm) | Pseudo-Color |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DAPI** | 345 | 455 | Blue |
| **FITC** | 495 | 519 | Green |
| **Spectrum Green** | 497 | 524 | Green |
| **Spectrum Orange** | 559 | 588 | Orange |
| **Texas Red** | 596 | 615 | Red |
| **Cy5** | 650 | 670 | Far-red |

By combining 3–5 spectrally distinct fluorochromes with combinatorial labeling and binary ratio-mixing, **Multi-Color FISH (MFISH)** can theoretically resolve $2^n - 1$ targets from $n$ fluorochromes. With 5 fluorochromes: $2^5 - 1 = 31$ targets — enough to paint all 24 human chromosomes.

## 2.4 Signal Generation & Detection

Two principal **labeling chemistries** exist:

1. **Direct labeling** — fluorochrome conjugated directly to the probe (e.g., nick-translation with fluorochrome-dUTP). Signal visualized immediately.
2. **Indirect labeling** — probe tagged with a **hapten** (e.g., biotin or digoxigenin), then detected by a fluorescent **affinity reagent** (avidin-FITC or anti-DIG-rhodamine). Amplifies signal through multiple binding events.

The signal is visualized via **epifluorescence microscopy** with bandpass filters specific to each fluorochrome. Digital images are captured with a **cooled CCD camera** and pseudo-colored, then overlaid.

## 2.5 Stringency — The Most Critical Practical Variable

Stringency controls how *tolerantly* the probe accepts mismatches:

$$
\text{Stringency} \propto T,\; \% \text{formamide} \propto \frac{1}{[\text{Na}^+]}
$$

- **High stringency** = high temperature, high formamide, low salt → only perfectly matched duplexes survive → cleaner signals.
- **Low stringency** = lower temperature, less formamide, higher salt → mismatches tolerated → risk of cross-hybridization.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Parameter | Unit | Engineering / Diagnostic Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| Probe–Target duplex stability | $T_m = 81.5 + 16.6\log_{10}[\text{Na}^+] + 0.41\,(\%GC) - 600/L$ | °C | Sets hybridization temperature |
| Hybridization temperature | $T_{hyb} \approx T_m - 25$ | °C | Practical annealing condition |
| Optical resolution (Abbe limit) | $d = \dfrac{0.61\,\lambda}{\text{NA}}$ | nm | Limits how close two signals can be distinguished |
| Spectral separation | $\Delta\lambda = \lambda_{em} - \lambda_{ex}$ (Stokes shift) | nm | Determines filter design |
| MFISH target capacity | $N = 2^n - 1$ | dimensionless | Chromosomes paintable with $n$ fluorochromes |
| Formamide stringency rule | Each 1 % formamide $\equiv \Delta T \approx 0.6\,^\circ\text{C}$ | °C | Wash-stringency tuning |
| Fiber-FISH resolution | $1\;\mu\text{m} \approx 2\text{–}3 \text{ kb}$ (extended DNA) | kb | Highest-resolution FISH variant |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering utility**: Optical filter design (bandpass selection), CCD quantum efficiency, and formamide-based stringency control are direct applications of physical chemistry and optical engineering in cytogenetic diagnostics. The Abbe diffraction limit directly constrains how close two FISH signals can be resolved on a single chromosome — driving the development of **super-resolution FISH (SR-FISH)** and **structured illumination microscopy**.

## 2.7 Why "Why & How" — The Logic Behind Each Step

- **Why denature?** Double-stranded target DNA must be unwound so the single-stranded probe can access its complementary sequence.
- **Why formamide?** Formamide lowers the $T_m$ of the duplex by **~0.6 °C per 1 %** added, allowing hybridization at non-damaging temperatures (37–42 °C instead of >70 °C).
- **Why dextran sulfate?** A high-molecular-weight polymer that **excludes volume**, raising the effective local probe concentration and accelerating hybridization kinetics by **~10×**.
- **Why Cot-1 DNA?** Repetitive sequences (e.g., Alu, LINE1) would cause the probe to stick non-specifically everywhere; adding unlabeled **Cot-1 DNA** competes these out — a process called **chromosomal in situ suppression (CISS)**.
- **Why DAPI counterstain?** DAPI binds AT-rich minor groove of all DNA, painting every chromosome blue so that the *specific* FISH signals stand out against a full karyotype.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step FISH Protocol

> [!NOTE]
> This is the canonical KTU-expected workflow. Each step is annotated with the *why* and *how*.

### Step 1 — Slide Preparation

Cells arrested in metaphase with **colcemid** (a microtubule inhibitor) are subjected to **hypotonic treatment** (0.075 M KCl) to swell them, then fixed in **methanol:acetic acid (3:1)**. Drops are splashed onto a chilled, grease-free slide and aged (overnight at 37 °C or 60 °C for 1 h).

- **Why?** Metaphase chromosomes are maximally condensed → easier to identify individual chromosomes and individual loci.
- **Hypotonic KCl?** Causes nuclei to swell, spreading chromosomes apart without rupturing them.

### Step 2 — Probe Preparation & Labeling

The probe DNA (plasmid, BAC, cosmid, or PCR product) is labeled with a fluorochrome by **nick translation**, **random priming**, or **PCR incorporation**. Nick translation uses DNase I to nick the DNA, then DNA polymerase I to extend and replace nucleotides with fluorochrome-conjugated dUTPs.

$$
\text{DNA} + \text{dNTPs (incl. Fluor-dUTP)} \xrightarrow[\text{DNA pol I}]{\text{DNase I}} \text{Fluor-labeled probe fragments (200–500 bp)}
$$

The fragment size is critical — probes too long diffuse poorly into fixed chromatin; probes too short give weak, non-specific signals.

### Step 3 — Denaturation of Probe and Target

- **Probe denaturation**: Probe mix heated to **72–75 °C for 5–10 min**, then placed on ice to prevent re-annealing.
- **Target denaturation**: Slides incubated in **70 % formamide / 2× SSC at 70–73 °C for 2–5 min**, then dehydrated through an **ethanol series** (70 %, 85 %, 100 %).

$$
\text{dsDNA} \xrightarrow[\text{or heat}]{70\% \text{formamide, }70^\circ\text{C}} \text{ssDNA}
$$

Formamide disrupts the hydrogen bonds without the harsh alkaline hydrolysis that would destroy chromosome morphology.

### Step 4 — Hybridization

The denatured probe is applied to the slide under a coverslip, sealed with rubber cement, and incubated in a humidified chamber at **37 °C for 4–16 hours (overnight)**.

$$
\text{Probe (ss)} + \text{Target (ss)} \xrightarrow[37^\circ\text{C}]{\text{2× SSC, 50\% formamide, 10\% dextran sulfate}} \text{Heteroduplex (probe:target)}
$$

The **dextran sulfate** accelerates hybridization ~10× by volume exclusion. **Cot-1 DNA** is included for probes containing repetitive elements to suppress non-specific binding (**CISS hybridization**).

### Step 5 — Post-Hybridization Washes

Slides washed in **50 % formamide / 2× SSC at 42 °C** (3 × 5 min), then **2× SSC at 42 °C** (3 × 5 min).

This is where **stringency** is enforced — only duplexes with sufficient complementarity survive the denaturing wash.

### Step 6 — Counterstaining & Mounting

A drop of **DAPI/antifade** (e.g., Vectashield) is applied under a coverslip. DAPI stains all chromatin blue; antifade reagents (e.g., DABCO, *p*-phenylenediamine) suppress photobleaching.

### Step 7 — Microscopy & Image Acquisition

Slides examined on an **epifluorescence microscope** equipped with:

- **Mercury or LED light source** (specific emission spectra).
- **Filter cubes** — excitation filter (selects $\lambda_{ex}$), dichroic mirror (reflects $\lambda_{ex}$, transmits $\lambda_{em}$), emission filter (selects $\lambda_{em}$).
- **CCD camera** captures each fluorochrome channel separately; software **pseudo-colors** and merges channels.

For **multi-color FISH**, sequential images through different filter sets are captured and registered.

### Step 8 — Analysis & Interpretation

- **Metaphase FISH**: count signals on each chromosome; identify translocations, deletions, duplications.
- **Interphase FISH**: count discrete nuclear signals for aneuploidy screening.
- **Fiber-FISH**: stretched DNA fibers give kb-level resolution mapping.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Probe Melting Temperature

> [!TIP]
> **Sample problem** (typical KTU Part-A flavor): A 25-mer oligonucleotide probe has 48 % GC content. The hybridization buffer contains 0.05 M Na⁺. Estimate the melting temperature $T_m$.

**Solution** (every step explicit):

$$
T_m = 81.5 + 16.6 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.05}{1}\right) + 0.41\,(\%GC) - \frac{600}{L}
$$

Compute each term:

$$
\log_{10}(0.05) = -1.301
$$

$$
16.6 \times (-1.301) = -21.60
$$

$$
0.41 \times 48 = 19.68
$$

$$
\frac{600}{25} = 24.00
$$

Therefore:

$$
T_m = 81.5 + (-21.60) + 19.68 - 24.00
$$

$$
T_m = 81.5 - 21.60 = 60.90
$$

$$
60.90 + 19.68 = 80.58
$$

$$
80.58 - 24.00 = 56.58\;^\circ\text{C}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_m \approx 56.6\;^\circ\text{C}}
$$

Therefore the hybridization should be performed at approximately:

$$
T_{hyb} = T_m - 25 \approx 31.6\;^\circ\text{C}
$$

In practice this is often raised to 37 °C to accelerate kinetics, with formamide titration used to compensate.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Probe

| Clinical Question | Best Probe Type | Expected Normal Signal | Diagnostic Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Trisomy 21 (Down syndrome) | CEP 13/21 dual-color | 2 red + 2 green signals | 3 red signals ⇒ Trisomy 21 |
| BCR-ABL (CML) | Dual-color LSI BCR + ABL | 2 fusion (yellow) on normal | 1 fusion, 1 red, 1 green ⇒ Philadelphia t(9;22) |
| HER2/*ERBB2* amplification | LSI HER2 / CEP17 ratio | HER2/CEP17 ≈ 1.0 | Ratio ≥ 2.0 ⇒ HER2-positive |
| 22q11.2 deletion (DiGeorge) | LSI TUPLE1 / ARSA | 2 orange + 2 green | 1 orange, 2 green ⇒ microdeletion |
| Whole-genome aneuploidy (PGS) | Multi-color CEP | 2 signals per chromosome | Extra/missing signal per chromosome |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FISH Workflow — End-to-End Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Metaphase Cell Suspension] --> B[Hypotonic KCl Swelling]
    B --> C[Methanol:Acetic Acid Fixation]
    C --> D[Drop on Glass Slide & Aging]
    D --> E[Target Denaturation 70 percent Formamide 73 C 2 min]
    F[Probe DNA Preparation] --> G[Nick Translation with Fluorochrome dUTP]
    G --> H[Probe Denaturation 75 C 5 min on Ice]
    H --> I[Apply Probe to Slide under Coverslip]
    E --> I
    I --> J[Hybridization 37 C Overnight 4 to 16 hr]
    J --> K[Stringency Washes 42 C Formamide SSC]
    K --> L[DAPI Counterstain and Antifade Mount]
    L --> M[Epifluorescence Microscopy]
    M --> N[CCD Capture Multi Channel]
    N --> O[Software Merge and Pseudocolor]
    O --> P[Interpretation Signal Counting and Karyotype]
```

    P --> P2[Locus Specific LSI]

    P1 --> U1[Aneuploidy Screening Trisomy Monosomy]

```

    A --> A1[One or Two Targets per Cell]

    G --> G1[Detect Specific mRNA Transcripts in Fixed Cells]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — 3-Mark Questions**

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Fluorescence In Situ Hybridization (FISH). Mention any two of its clinical applications.**

**Model Answer:**

Fluorescence In Situ Hybridization (FISH) is a molecular cytogenetic technique in which a fluorescently-labeled single-stranded DNA (or RNA) probe is hybridized to a complementary nucleic acid target sequence *in situ* within fixed cells or chromosome preparations on a glass slide, allowing the localization and enumeration of specific genetic loci via fluorescence microscopy.

**Two clinical applications:**
1. **Prenatal aneuploidy screening** — rapid detection of trisomies 13, 18, and 21 on uncultured amniocytes or chorionic villus samples using centromere-specific (CEP) probes.
2. **Cancer diagnostics** — detection of HER2/*ERBB2* gene amplification in breast carcinoma using locus-specific probes to guide trastuzumab (Herceptin) therapy.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the three major types of FISH probes and state one diagnostic use of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Probe Type | Target | Diagnostic Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Centromere-repeat (CEP)** | Alpha-satellite DNA | Enumerate chromosomes (e.g., trisomy 21) |
| **Locus-specific (LSI)** | Defined gene region | Detect HER2 amplification or BCR-ABL translocation |
| **Whole chromosome paint (WCP)** | Entire chromosome | Identify origin of marker / translocation chromosomes |

---

## **PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall** — Students commonly lose marks on FISH questions for:
> 1. Confusing **probe denaturation** (75 °C, 5 min) with **target denaturation** (70–73 °C, 2–5 min in formamide).
> 2. Forgetting the role of **Cot-1 DNA / CISS** in suppressing repetitive-sequence hybridization.
> 3. Omitting **stringency** parameters (formamide %, temperature, salt concentration).
> 4. Failing to distinguish **metaphase FISH** from **interphase FISH** applications.
> 5. Misnaming fluorochromes (writing "FISH" instead of "FITC"; "Texas red" instead of "Texas Red").

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]`

**(a)** Describe in detail the **principle and step-by-step procedure** of Fluorescence In Situ Hybridization (FISH). *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **different types of FISH probes** with suitable examples and their diagnostic applications. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Principle & Procedure:**

FISH rests on the principle of **complementary base pairing** between a fluorescently-labeled single-stranded probe and its complementary single-stranded DNA target within fixed cells or chromosomes on a slide. The hybridized duplex is visualized by **epifluorescence microscopy**, where excitation light at the fluorochrome's absorption wavelength causes emission at a longer wavelength that is captured through specific emission filters and recorded by a CCD camera.

**Step-by-step procedure (with technical detail):**

1. **Slide preparation**: Cultured lymphocytes are arrested at metaphase with colcemid, swollen in 0.075 M KCl, fixed in methanol:acetic acid (3:1), dropped onto slides, and aged.
2. **Probe labeling**: Probe DNA (BAC, plasmid, cosmid, or PCR product) is labeled with fluorochrome-conjugated dUTPs via **nick translation**, generating 200–500 bp fragments.
3. **Denaturation**: Probe heated to 75 °C for 5 min and snap-cooled on ice; slide immersed in 70 % formamide / 2× SSC at 73 °C for 2–5 min to denature chromosomal DNA, then dehydrated through cold ethanol series.
4. **Hybridization**: Probe applied under a sealed coverslip; incubated overnight at 37 °C in humidified chamber in hybridization buffer containing 50 % formamide, 2× SSC, 10 % dextran sulfate, and unlabeled **Cot-1 DNA** to suppress repetitive-sequence binding.
5. **Stringency washes**: Three washes in 50 % formamide / 2× SSC at 42 °C followed by three washes in 2× SSC at 42 °C remove non-specifically bound probe.
6. **Counterstaining & mounting**: DAPI/antifade (e.g., Vectashield) applied; coverslip sealed.
7. **Microscopy & image capture**: Epifluorescence microscope with appropriate filter sets for each fluorochrome; CCD camera captures sequential channel images; software merges and pseudo-colors them.
8. **Interpretation**: Signal counts and locations are recorded on the metaphase spread or interphase nucleus.

**(b) Probe Types with Examples:**

1. **Centromere-repeat probes (CEP)** — Target alpha-satellite DNA at the centromere. *Example*: CEP 21 labeled in Spectrum Orange used to confirm trisomy 21 in prenatal diagnosis; dual CEP X/Y for sex-chromosome aneuploidy screening.

2. **Locus-specific probes (LSI)** — Hybridize to a single defined genomic locus (100 kb – 1 Mb). *Example*: LSI *HER2* (orange) co-hybridized with CEP 17 (green) in breast cancer; the HER2/CEP17 ratio ≥ 2.0 indicates HER2 amplification and trastuzumab eligibility. Dual-color LSI for **BCR** (green) and **ABL** (red) in CML reveals the Philadelphia translocation via fusion signals.

3. **Whole chromosome painting probes (WCP)** — A composite of probes spanning an entire chromosome. *Example*: Painting chromosome 9 and chromosome 22 in different colors reveals the t(9;22) translocation in CML on metaphase spreads.

4. **Subtelomeric / telomere probes** — Target sub-telomeric repetitive regions. *Example*: Used to detect cryptic telomeric rearrangements in idiopathic mental retardation.

5. **Centromeric multi-color cocktails** — Combinations of CEP probes for chromosomes 13, 18, 21, X, Y simultaneously used in rapid prenatal aneuploidy screening (less than 24 h, without cell culture).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the **clinical applications of FISH** in (i) cancer genetics, (ii) prenatal diagnosis, and (iii) preimplantation genetic diagnosis. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** What are the **advantages and limitations of FISH** compared to conventional karyotyping? Add a note on **Multi-Color FISH (MFISH)** and **Spectral Karyotyping (SKY)**. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Clinical applications:**

**(i) Cancer genetics:**
FISH detects chromosome aberrations in tumor cells that conventional banding often misses due to poor chromosome morphology.

- **HER2/neu amplification** in breast cancer: HER2/CEP17 ratio ≥ 2.0 → trastuzumab (Herceptin) therapy eligibility.
- **BCR-ABL fusion** in chronic myeloid leukemia: dual-color LSI BCR + ABL shows one fusion (Philadelphia), one red, and one green signal on metaphase/interphase nuclei.
- **MYC translocation** t(8;14) in Burkitt lymphoma.
- **1p/19q co-deletion** in oligodendroglioma — predictive of response to chemotherapy.

**(ii) Prenatal diagnosis:**
- **Rapid aneuploidy detection (RAD)** on uncultured amniocytes or chorionic villi using CEP probes for chromosomes 13, 18, 21, X, Y — results available within **24–48 hours**, avoiding the 10–14 days needed for karyotyping.
- **Microdeletion syndromes**: e.g., 22q11.2 deletion (DiGeorge) using LSI TUPLE1; Williams syndrome (7q11.23 deletion); Prader-Willi/Angelman (15q11-13).
- **Detection of maternal cell contamination** in chorionic villus samples via FISH with CEP X and Y.

**(iii) Preimplantation Genetic Diagnosis (PGD):**
- Performed on **single blastomeres** from 3-day embryos or **trophectoderm cells** from 5-day blastocysts.
- **Polar body biopsy** FISH for chromosomal aneuploidy in IVF.
- Used to select embryos free from **translocations** (e.g., reciprocal translocation carriers) or aneuploidies (advanced maternal age), increasing implantation rates and reducing miscarriage.

**(b) Advantages, Limitations, and variants of FISH:**

**Advantages over karyotyping:**
1. Higher resolution (sub-band level, down to 100 kb).
2. Works on **interphase nuclei** — no need for dividing cells.
3. Faster (24–48 h vs 10–14 days).
4. Can detect cryptic aberrations invisible to banding.
5. Multi-target analysis via multiple fluorochromes.

**Limitations:**
1. Limited only to the **loci targeted by the probes**; cannot scan the entire genome (except CGH/SKY).
2. Subject to **probe hybridization failure** and signal overlap.
3. Cannot detect **balanced rearrangements** unless probes span the breakpoint.
4. **Photobleaching** of fluorochromes limits signal longevity.
5. Interphase interpretation can be complicated by **signal splitting** and **nuclear truncation artifacts**.
6. Higher per-test cost than banding for whole-genome screening.

**Multi-Color FISH (MFISH)**: Combinatorially labels each chromosome with a unique mixture of 5 fluorochromes, theoretically resolving $2^5 - 1 = 31$ targets — enough for all 24 human chromosomes. Each chromosome appears in a unique pseudo-color.

**Spectral Karyotyping (SKY)**: A variant of MFISH that uses **spectral imaging** — a single exposure through an interferometer captures the entire emission spectrum for each pixel; Fourier transformation then assigns each chromosome a unique spectral signature. SKY offers faster acquisition but requires specialized spectral imaging hardware.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — FISH**

- **FISH = Fluorescence + In Situ + Hybridization.** Probe + target = complementary duplex detected by fluorescence.
- **Three probe pillars**: CEP (centromere repeat), LSI (locus specific), WCP (whole chromosome paint).
- **Resolutions**: metaphase ≈ 1 Mb; interphase ≈ 50–200 kb; fiber-FISH ≈ 1–500 kb.
- **Denaturation**: 70 % formamide / 2× SSC at 73 °C for ~2–5 min for slides; 75 °C for 5 min for probes.
- **Hybridization**: 37 °C overnight in 50 % formamide / 2× SSC / 10 % dextran sulfate.
- **Stringency is controlled by**: temperature, formamide %, salt concentration.
- **Cot-1 DNA + CISS** suppresses repetitive-sequence background.
- **Counterstain**: DAPI (blue), with antifade mounting medium.
- **Fluorochrome trio**: DAPI (blue), FITC (green), Texas Red (red); add Spectrum Orange and Cy5 for MFISH.
- **MFISH capacity**: $N = 2^n - 1$ targets from $n$ fluorochromes.
- **Major clinical uses**: cancer (HER2, BCR-ABL), prenatal aneuploidy (RAD), PGD, microdeletion syndromes.
- **Abbe diffraction limit** sets the floor for optical signal resolution: $d = 0.61\lambda/\text{NA}$.
- **$T_m$ formula** (Wallace-Itakura rule for short probes, Marmur for long):
  $T_m = 81.5 + 16.6\log_{10}[\text{Na}^+] + 0.41(\%GC) - 600/L$.
- **Formamide rule of thumb**: 1 % formamide lowers effective $T_m$ by ≈ 0.6 °C.
- **Variants to know**: MFISH, SKY, CGH, Fiber-FISH, RNA-FISH, SR-FISH (super-resolution).
- **Pitfall**: Probe denaturation ≠ target denaturation — different temperatures, different times.
- **Pitfall**: FISH detects only the regions targeted by the probes; not a whole-genome scan (unlike sequencing or CGH).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
