topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBB861 - CYTOGENETICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Comparative genomic hybridization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Comparative Genomic Hybridization (CGH)** is a fluorescence-based molecular cytogenetic technique developed by **Kallioniemi et al. in 1992** that enables the genome-wide detection of **Copy Number Variations (CNVs)** — i.e., chromosomal regions of **deletion (loss)** or **amplification (gain)** — in a single hybridization experiment, without the need for prior culturing of the test sample.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> CGH is essentially a *competitive* in-situ hybridization. Equal amounts of **test (tumour) DNA** and **reference (normal) DNA** are labeled with two spectrally distinct fluorochromes (commonly **green — FITC** and **red — Texas Red / Rhodamine**), denatured, and co-hybridized onto a normal human **metaphase spread** (classical CGH) or onto a **DNA microarray** (Array-CGH / aCGH).

The relative fluorescence intensity ratio along each chromosome directly reflects the **relative DNA copy number** in the test genome vs. the reference genome.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cytogenetic Principle:**
> - Test : Reference = **1 : 1** → equal green and red → chromosomal region appears **yellow** (no CNV).
> - Test > Reference → **gain / amplification** → region appears **green**.
> - Test < Reference → **loss / deletion** → region appears **red**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: "The Photocopy Machine of the Genome"**
>
> Imagine you have **two sheets of paper** — a *normal page* (reference DNA) and a *photocopied page* that may have some sections smudged (deleted) or stamped multiple times (amplified). Now imagine you place both sheets under a **green-tinted lamp** and a **red-tinted lamp** side-by-side, and you photograph them together.
>
> - Where both sheets look identical → the photograph shows **yellow** (green + red equally).
> - Where the second sheet has been stamped multiple times (more ink) → it absorbs more green → looks **green**.
> - Where the second sheet is missing ink (deleted region) → only the red shines through → looks **red**.
>
> CGH does exactly this for the entire genome in a single "photograph." The "ink" is fluorescence, and the "sheets" are DNA samples. This is why CGH is a **one-shot, genome-wide** scanning tool — no chromosome-by-chromosome PCR or FISH needed.

### 1.3 Physical & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard CGH Thresholds (KTU Board Standard):**
> - **Log₂ Ratio Threshold:** **> +0.5** → interpreted as a **GAIN (amplification)**.
> - **Log₂ Ratio Threshold:** **< −0.5** → interpreted as a **LOSS (deletion)**.
> - **Ratio Window:** values between **−0.5 and +0.5** → interpreted as **balanced (no CNV)**.
> - **Hybridization Quality Index (HQI):** ≥ **80%** is considered acceptable.
> - **Resolution (Metaphase CGH):** approximately **5–10 Mb**.
> - **Resolution (Array-CGH):** approximately **1 kb – 100 kb** depending on probe spacing.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Theoretical Framework

CGH rests on **three foundational principles** of molecular biology and optics:

1. **DNA Complementarity (Hybridization Principle):**
   - Single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) from the test sample seeks out and binds to its **complementary sequence** on the metaphase chromosome / array target via Watson-Crick base pairing (A–T, G–C).

2. **Competitive Kinetics:**
   - Both test and reference DNA compete *simultaneously* for the same target sites. The relative *amount* of each labeled DNA that binds is therefore proportional to its **relative concentration** in the hybridization mixture.

3. **Fluorescence Stoichiometry:**
   - The fluorochrome (FITC or Texas Red) is covalently linked to the DNA via **nick translation** (using DNase I + DNA Polymerase I). The fluorescence intensity emitted by a bound DNA fragment is **directly proportional to the mass of DNA** at that locus.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Stepwise Breakdown

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational Phases of CGH (KTU Board-Relevant Steps):**

1. **DNA Isolation & Purification**
   - Extract high-molecular-weight genomic DNA from the *test* (e.g., tumour) and *reference* (e.g., normal lymphocyte) samples.
   - Purity check: **A₂₆₀/A₂₈₀ ≈ 1.8–2.0** (Nucleic acid purity standard).

2. **Differential Fluorochrome Labeling (Nick Translation)**
   - **Test DNA** → labeled with **Green** fluorochrome (FITC-dUTP or Spectrum Green).
   - **Reference DNA** → labeled with **Red** fluorochrome (Texas Red-dUTP or Spectrum Red).
   - Fragment size target: **300–3000 bp** (optimal for hybridization).

3. **Hybridization**
   - Mix equal amounts (typically **200–400 ng each**) of labeled DNAs with **Cot-1 DNA** (to block repetitive sequences).
   - Co-denature and co-hybridize onto a normal metaphase spread (or BAC/PAC array).

4. **Washing & Counterstaining**
   - Stringency washes remove non-specific binding.
   - Counterstain chromosomes with **DAPI (4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole)** → blue fluorescence for chromosome identification.

5. **Image Capture & Analysis**
   - Capture multi-color fluorescence images via **CCD camera** mounted on a fluorescence microscope.
   - Software (e.g., **ISIS**, **MetaSystems**) computes the **green-to-red intensity ratio** along the chromosomal axis.

6. **Interpretation**
   - Ratio profile plotted along each chromosome → reveals gains (green-skewed) and losses (red-skewed).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Mathematical Formulas for CGH (Exam-Critical)**

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $R_{i}$ | Fluorescence intensity ratio at locus *i* | Dimensionless |
| $I_{G,i}$ | Green intensity at locus *i* (Test DNA) | Arbitrary fluorescence units (AFU) |
| $I_{R,i}$ | Red intensity at locus *i* (Reference DNA) | Arbitrary fluorescence units (AFU) |
| $\text{Log}_{2}R$ | Logarithmic copy-number ratio | Unitless |
| $\text{HQI}$ | Hybridization Quality Index | % (must be ≥ 80%) |
| $A_{260}/A_{280}$ | DNA purity ratio | ≈ 1.8–2.0 |
| Fragment size | Nick-translation product | 300–3000 bp |
| Resolution$_{MCGH}$ | Metaphase CGH resolution | 5–10 Mb |
| Resolution$_{aCGH}$ | Array CGH resolution | 1–100 kb |

**Key Equations:**

$$
R_i = \frac{I_{G,i}}{I_{R,i}}
$$

$$
\text{Log}_2 R_i = \log_2 \left( \frac{I_{G,i}}{I_{R,i}} \right)
$$

**Interpretation Rule:**
$$
\text{Log}_2 R_i \begin{cases} > +0.5 & \Rightarrow \text{Gain / Amplification (Copy Number } > 2) \\ < -0.5 & \Rightarrow \text{Loss / Deletion (Copy Number } < 2) \\ \in [-0.5,\, +0.5] & \Rightarrow \text{Balanced (Normal Copy Number = 2)} \end{cases}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not use absolute-value bars `|` inside markdown tables.** In LaTeX inside tables, always use `\vert` or `\mid` if needed.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Medical Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering & Biomedical Utility of CGH:**
>
> - **Cancer Cytogenetics:** Detection of oncogene amplifications (e.g., *MYCN* in neuroblastoma, *ERBB2/HER2* in breast cancer) and tumour suppressor deletions (e.g., *RB1*, *TP53*).
> - **Prenatal Diagnosis:** Detects unbalanced chromosomal aberrations in foetal DNA obtained from amniotic fluid or chorionic villi, even when cells cannot be cultured.
> - **Constitutional Disorders:** Diagnosis of microdeletion/microduplication syndromes (e.g., DiGeorge 22q11.2 deletion, Williams 7q11.23 deletion, Prader-Willi/Angelman 15q11–13).
> - **Forensic Genetics:** Rarely used, but informative in identifying genetic mosaicism in decomposed samples.
> - **Optical Engineering:** CGH drove demand for **multi-band-pass fluorescence filter sets**, **cooled-CCD imaging sensors**, and **automated karyotyping software platforms** — direct contributions to biomedical-instrumentation engineering.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Log₂ Copy Number Ratio

We derive the mathematical relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio and the actual **DNA copy number** at a given locus.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-Step Derivation — From Fluorescence to Copy Number**

Let:
- $n_T$ = copy number of the locus in the **test** sample (e.g., tumour).
- $n_R$ = copy number of the locus in the **reference** (normal) sample (always $n_R = 2$ for autosomes in a diploid cell).

Because hybridization kinetics are mass-driven and both DNAs compete equally:

$$
\frac{I_{G,i}}{I_{R,i}} = \frac{n_T}{n_R}
$$

Since the reference is diploid ($n_R = 2$):

$$
R_i = \frac{n_T}{2}
$$

Now we express in **logarithmic (base-2) form**, because a copy number doubling should give a symmetric, easily readable value:

$$
\text{Log}_2 R_i = \log_2 \left( \frac{n_T}{2} \right) = \log_2 (n_T) - \log_2 (2) = \log_2 (n_T) - 1
$$

We can now tabulate the expected values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } n_T = 0 \;(\text{homozygous deletion}) &: \quad \text{Log}_2 R_i = \log_2(0) - 1 = -\infty \\
\text{If } n_T = 1 \;(\text{heterozygous deletion}) &: \quad \text{Log}_2 R_i = \log_2(1) - 1 = 0 - 1 = -1 \\
\text{If } n_T = 2 \;(\text{normal diploid}) &: \quad \text{Log}_2 R_i = \log_2(2) - 1 = 1 - 1 = 0 \\
\text{If } n_T = 3 \;(\text{trisomy / single-copy gain}) &: \quad \text{Log}_2 R_i = \log_2(3) - 1 \approx 1.585 - 1 = 0.585 \\
\text{If } n_T = 4 \;(\text{two-copy gain}) &: \quad \text{Log}_2 R_i = \log_2(4) - 1 = 2 - 1 = 1 \\
\text{If } n_T = 8 \;(\text{high-level amplification}) &: \quad \text{Log}_2 R_i = \log_2(8) - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold Derivation — Why ±0.5?**
>
> The standard ±0.5 threshold corresponds to the midpoint between the *normal* copy-number state (Log₂R = 0) and the *single-copy change* state:
>
> - For **gain**: midpoint between Log₂R = 0 (n=2) and Log₂R ≈ 0.585 (n=3) → threshold ≈ **+0.5**.
> - For **loss**: midpoint between Log₂R = 0 (n=2) and Log₂R = −1 (n=1) → threshold ≈ **−0.5**.
>
> These are universally adopted in KTU reference answers and in clinical CGH reporting guidelines.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** In a CGH experiment, the green (test) fluorescence intensity at locus X is measured as $I_G = 2400$ AFU, and the red (reference) intensity is $I_R = 1200$ AFU.

**(a)** Compute the raw ratio $R$.
**(b)** Compute the Log₂ ratio.
**(c)** Interpret the result.

**Solution:**

**(a) Raw Ratio:**
$$
R = \frac{I_G}{I_R} = \frac{2400}{1200} = 2.0
$$

**(b) Logarithmic (base-2) Ratio:**
$$
\text{Log}_2 R = \log_2(2.0) = \log_2\left(\frac{n_T}{n_R}\right) = \log_2\left(\frac{n_T}{2}\right)
$$

Setting this equal to 1 and solving for $n_T$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_2\left(\frac{n_T}{2}\right) &= 1 \\
\frac{n_T}{2} &= 2^1 = 2 \\
n_T &= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Interpretation:**
$$
\text{Log}_2 R = 1.0 > +0.5 \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{COPY-NUMBER GAIN (low-level amplification, } n_T = 4 \text{ copies)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight (KTU):**
> Students often forget to convert the raw ratio into Log₂ form. Always state both $R$ *and* $\text{Log}_2 R$ in board answers, and explicitly compare against the **±0.5 threshold**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Variants of CGH

| Feature | Metaphase CGH (MCGH) | Array CGH (aCGH) | NGS-based CGH |
|---|---|---|---|
| Target | Metaphase chromosomes | DNA microarray (BAC / oligo) | Whole-genome sequencing reads |
| Resolution | 5–10 Mb | 1–100 kb | < 1 kb (single-exon level) |
| Throughput | Low | Medium–High | High |
| Sample DNA needed | ~1 µg | ~250 ng | ~100 ng |
| Detection of balanced translocations | **No** | **No** | Indirectly (via read-depth) |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High |
| KTU Exam Frequency | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of a CGH Experiment

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps the **functional workflow** of a comparative genomic hybridization experiment from sample input to interpreted output, showing the data and material flow between the optical, biochemical, and computational modules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sample Input Test Genomic DNA] --> B[DNA Quantification Spectrophotometry]
    C[Reference Input Normal Lymphocyte DNA] --> B
    B --> D{Nick Translation Reaction}
    D -->|Test DNA + FITC dUTP| E[Green Labeled Test DNA Fragments 300 to 3000 bp]
    D -->|Reference DNA + Texas Red dUTP| F[Red Labeled Reference DNA Fragments 300 to 3000 bp]
    E --> G[Co Hybridization Chamber]
    F --> G
    H[Cot 1 Blocking DNA] --> G
    I[Metaphase Spread Target / BAC Array] --> G
    G --> J[Stringency Washing]
    J --> K[DAPI Counterstain Blue Chromosomes]
    K --> L[Multi Band Fluorescence Microscope CCD Camera]
    L --> M[Digital Image Acquisition]
    M --> N[Image Analysis Software ISIS MetaSystems]
    N --> O[Compute Green Red Ratio per Chromosomal Locus]
    O --> P[Log2 Ratio Plot Karyotype View]
    P --> Q{Threshold Check}
    Q -->|Log2R greater than plus 0.5| R[Interpretation GAIN Amplification]
    Q -->|Log2R less than minus 0.5| S[Interpretation LOSS Deletion]
    Q -->|Log2R in range minus 0.5 to plus 0.5| T[Interpretation BALANCED Normal]
    R --> U[Clinical Report]
    S --> U
    T --> U
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Three-Color Chromosome View (Block Schematic)

The following block diagram represents how the **software interprets** the multi-fluorochrome image at the level of a single chromosome ideogram.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Chromosome 1 Ideogram] --> B[Bin 1p36 Locus]
    A --> C[Bin 1q21 Locus]
    A --> D[Bin 1q44 Locus]
    B --> B1[Green intensity high]
    B1 --> B2[Log2R equals plus 0.9]
    B2 --> B3[Call GAIN]
    C --> C1[Green intensity equals Red]
    C1 --> C2[Log2R equals 0.0]
    C2 --> C3[Call NORMAL]
    D --> D1[Red intensity high]
    D1 --> D2[Log2R equals minus 1.1]
    D2 --> D3[Call LOSS]
```

### 4.3 Engineering Block View of the Imaging Station

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph OPT[Optical Module]
        A1[Mercury Arc Lamp] --> A2[Excitation Filter Cube]
        A2 --> A3[Dichroic Mirror]
        A3 --> A4[Objective Lens 100x Oil]
        A4 --> A5[Emission Path to Camera]
    end
    subgraph BIO[Biochemical Module]
        B1[Hybridized Slide] --> B2[Immersion Oil Contact]
        B2 --> A4
    end
    subgraph DIG[Digital Module]
        A5 --> C1[Cooled CCD Sensor]
        C1 --> C2[Analog to Digital Converter 12 bit]
        C2 --> C3[Frame Grabber Card]
        C3 --> C4[Workstation Storage]
        C4 --> C5[CGH Analysis Software]
    end
    C5 --> D1[Output Profile Plot and Karyotype Report]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - The **Optical Module** produces the multi-color excitation/emission pathway.
> - The **Biochemical Module** holds the hybridized metaphase slide as the physical specimen.
> - The **Digital Module** converts photon counts into pixel intensities → quantitative ratio data.
> - All three modules operate **in series**; failure in any one collapses the experiment.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> *Modeled after KTU 2024 Scheme ESE / Continuous Evaluation pattern.*

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define Comparative Genomic Hybridization (CGH). Mention the two fluorochromes commonly used.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Comparative Genomic Hybridization (CGH) is a molecular cytogenetic technique that detects **genome-wide copy number variations (CNVs)** — chromosomal gains and losses — by competitively hybridizing differentially labeled test and reference DNAs onto a normal metaphase chromosome spread (or DNA microarray).

The two fluorochromes commonly used are:

1. **FITC (Fluorescein isothiocyanate)** → emits **green** fluorescence, used to label **test (tumour) DNA**.
2. **Texas Red** (or Spectrum Red / Rhodamine) → emits **red** fluorescence, used to label **reference (normal) DNA**.

The green-to-red fluorescence intensity ratio along each chromosome reflects the **relative DNA copy number** in the test vs. reference genome.

*(Marks: 1 for definition, 2 for fluorochromes with colors and roles.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*What is the significance of the **Log₂ ratio threshold of ±0.5** in CGH interpretation?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Log₂ ratio** is defined as:
$$
\text{Log}_2 R = \log_2 \left( \frac{I_{G,\text{test}}}{I_{R,\text{reference}}} \right)
$$

A normal diploid locus has $n_T = 2$ copies, giving $\text{Log}_2 R = 0$.

- A **single-copy gain** ($n_T = 3$) gives $\text{Log}_2 R \approx +0.585$.
- A **single-copy loss** ($n_T = 1$) gives $\text{Log}_2 R = -1$.

The midpoint between normal (0) and gain (+0.585) is **+0.5**, and the midpoint between normal (0) and loss (−1) is **−0.5**.

Therefore, by convention:
- **Log₂R > +0.5** → **Gain/Amplification**.
- **Log₂R < −0.5** → **Loss/Deletion**.
- **−0.5 ≤ Log₂R ≤ +0.5** → **Balanced (normal copy number)**.

This threshold minimizes false-positive calls while preserving sensitivity for single-copy CNVs.

*(Marks: 1 for formula, 1 for threshold values, 1 for interpretation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### ▶ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4, Set A]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **principle, procedure, and workflow** of Classical (Metaphase) Comparative Genomic Hybridization. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Metaphase CGH** and **Array CGH** with respect to resolution, throughput, principle, advantages, and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Principle, Procedure & Workflow of Metaphase CGH (7 Marks):**

**Principle (2 Marks):**
- CGH is based on the **competitive co-hybridization** of two differentially labeled genomic DNAs (test and reference) onto a normal **metaphase chromosome spread**.
- The **ratio of fluorescence intensities** at each chromosomal locus is quantitatively proportional to the **relative DNA copy number** in the test sample.

**Procedure (5 Marks):**

1. **DNA Extraction:** Isolate high-molecular-weight DNA from test (tumour) and reference (normal) samples. Verify purity by $A_{260}/A_{280} = 1.8$–$2.0$.

2. **Nick Translation:** Label test DNA with **FITC-dUTP (green)** and reference DNA with **Texas Red-dUTP (red)** using DNase I + DNA Polymerase I. Fragment size: **300–3000 bp**.

3. **Probe Preparation:** Mix labeled DNAs with **unlabeled Cot-1 DNA** (to suppress repetitive-sequence hybridization).

4. **Co-Hybridization:** Denature probe mix and apply to a normal metaphase spread. Incubate at **37 °C for 48–72 hours** in a humidified chamber.

5. **Washing:** Stringency washes in formamide/SSC to remove non-specific binding.

6. **Counterstaining:** Mount with **DAPI/antifade** for chromosome identification (blue band).

7. **Image Capture:** Use a **CCD-equipped fluorescence microscope** with appropriate filter sets.

8. **Software Analysis:** Compute the **green/red fluorescence ratio** along each chromosome; generate an idiogram with **Log₂R profile**.

9. **Interpretation:** Apply ±0.5 threshold → declare gains, losses, or normal.

**(b) Comparison Table (7 Marks):**

| Parameter | Metaphase CGH | Array CGH (aCGH) |
|---|---|---|
| **Target** | Metaphase chromosome spread | Glass slide spotted with BACs / oligonucleotides |
| **Resolution** | 5–10 Mb | 1 kb – 100 kb |
| **Principle** | Ratio of fluorescence along chromosomes | Ratio of fluorescence at arrayed loci |
| **Throughput** | Low (1 sample/slide) | High (multiple samples per slide) |
| **Detects CNVs** | Yes | Yes (more sensitive) |
| **Detects balanced translocations** | No | No |
| **Detects mosaicism** | Limited (≥ 30–50%) | Better (≥ 10–20%) |
| **Sample DNA needed** | ~1 µg | ~250 ng |
| **Automated analysis** | Semi-automated | Fully automated |
| **Cost per sample** | Low | Moderate–High |
| **Clinical use** | Decreasing | Gold-standard for CNV detection |

*(Marks distribution: (a) 2 + 5, (b) 1 mark per key point × 7 rows.)*

---

### ▶ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4, Set B]**

**(a)** Explain the **role of CGH in cancer cytogenetics** with suitable examples of oncogene amplifications and tumour suppressor gene deletions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A CGH experiment at locus **8q24** (harbouring the *MYC* oncogene) yields the following fluorescence values:
$I_G = 3600$ AFU (test), $I_R = 900$ AFU (reference).
Compute the **raw ratio**, the **Log₂ ratio**, and **interpret the cytogenetic finding** with reference to the ±0.5 threshold. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Role of CGH in Cancer Cytogenetics (7 Marks):**

CGH is indispensable for identifying **somatic copy-number alterations (CNAs)** that drive malignancy:

**1. Oncogene Amplifications (3 Marks):**
- **MYCN amplification at 2p24** — classic in **neuroblastoma**; > 10 copies indicates poor prognosis.
- **ERBB2 (HER2) amplification at 17q12** — predictive marker in **breast cancer**; guides trastuzumab therapy.
- **MYC amplification at 8q24** — common in **Burkitt lymphoma**, medulloblastoma, and other carcinomas.
- **EGFR amplification at 7p11.2** — frequent in **glioblastoma multiforme** and **non-small-cell lung cancer**.

**2. Tumour Suppressor Gene Deletions (2 Marks):**
- **TP53 deletion at 17p13** — seen in > 50% of human cancers.
- **RB1 deletion at 13q14** — hallmark of **retinoblastoma** and **osteosarcoma**.
- **CDKN2A deletion at 9p21** — common in melanoma and pancreatic cancer.
- **PTEN deletion at 10q23** — frequent in prostate and endometrial cancers.

**3. Diagnostic & Prognostic Utility (2 Marks):**
- CGH provides a **whole-genome, single-experiment** view of tumour genome instability.
- Helps classify tumours, predict therapy response (e.g., HER2-targeted drugs), and monitor disease progression.
- Limitation: cannot detect **balanced translocations** (e.g., BCR-ABL in CML) or **point mutations**.

**(b) Numerical Interpretation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute Raw Ratio:**
$$
R = \frac{I_G}{I_R} = \frac{3600 \text{ AFU}}{900 \text{ AFU}} = 4.0
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Log₂ Ratio:**
$$
\text{Log}_2 R = \log_2(4.0) = \log_2\left(2^2\right) = 2.0
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Copy Number $n_T$ (diploid reference $n_R = 2$):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Log}_2 R &= \log_2\left(\frac{n_T}{n_R}\right) = \log_2\left(\frac{n_T}{2}\right) = 2.0 \\
\frac{n_T}{2} &= 2^2 = 4 \\
n_T &= 8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Threshold Check & Interpretation:**

$$
\text{Log}_2 R = 2.0 \;\;\gg\;\; +0.5 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \boxed{\textbf{HIGH-LEVEL AMPLIFICATION at 8q24}}
$$

Since 8q24 harbours the ***MYC* proto-oncogene**, this finding is consistent with **MYC amplification** — an event commonly observed in **Burkitt lymphoma**, **neuroblastoma**, **medulloblastoma**, and aggressive breast carcinomas. Clinically, MYC amplification is associated with **poor prognosis** and may inform treatment intensification.

*(Marks: (a) 3 + 2 + 2 = 7, (b) 2 + 2 + 1 + 2 = 7.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state the Log₂ conversion explicitly.** Students write the raw ratio only and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up fluorochromes** — always state **test = green**, **reference = red** (or vice versa, but be consistent and label clearly).
> 3. **Forgetting Cot-1 DNA** in the procedure step → significant loss of process marks.
> 4. **Citing wrong resolution** — students often claim MCGH has kb-level resolution; correct is **5–10 Mb**.
> 5. **Not linking gain to clinical outcome** — e.g., MYCN amplification → poor prognosis in neuroblastoma. KTU examiners reward clinical correlation.
> 6. **Confusing CGH with FISH or SKY** — CGH is competitive, whole-genome; FISH uses single locus; SKY uses spectral karyotyping with 5 fluorochromes.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Revision)**
>
> ✅ CGH = **Comparative Genomic Hybridization** — developed by **Kallioniemi et al., 1992**.
> ✅ Detects **copy-number gains and losses (CNVs)** genome-wide in **one experiment**.
> ✅ Test DNA labeled with **FITC (green)**; Reference DNA labeled with **Texas Red (red)**.
> ✅ Both DNAs competitively hybridize to **normal metaphase chromosomes** (MCGH) or **DNA arrays** (aCGH).
> ✅ Chromosomal counterstain = **DAPI (blue)** for karyotype identification.
> ✅ Nick translation enzymes = **DNase I + DNA Polymerase I**.
> ✅ Fragment size after nick translation = **300–3000 bp**.
> ✅ Cot-1 DNA used to **block repetitive sequences**.
> ✅ Hybridization temperature = **37 °C for 48–72 h**.
> ✅ **Log₂R > +0.5** = Gain; **Log₂R < −0.5** = Loss; otherwise Normal.
> ✅ **MCGH resolution = 5–10 Mb**; **aCGH resolution = 1–100 kb**.
> ✅ CGH **CANNOT** detect **balanced translocations** or **point mutations**.
> ✅ **Clinical Applications:** Cancer CNV profiling, microdeletion syndromes, prenatal diagnosis.
> ✅ **Key Examples:** MYCN (2p24, neuroblastoma), HER2/ERBB2 (17q12, breast cancer), MYC (8q24, Burkitt lymphoma), TP53 loss (17p13).
> ✅ Threshold midpoints derived from: midpoint of Log₂(3/2) ≈ +0.5 (gain) and midpoint of Log₂(1/2) = −1 (loss).
> ✅ Software: **ISIS, MetaSystems, GenePix, BlueFuse**.
> ✅ Upgrade path: MCGH → aCGH → **NGS-based CNV analysis** (shallow whole-genome sequencing).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Array Comparative Genomic Hybridization (aCGH)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Array Comparative Genomic Hybridization (aCGH)** is a high-resolution, genome-wide molecular cytogenetic technique that enables the simultaneous detection of **Copy Number Variations (CNVs)** — including microdeletions, microduplications, and aneuploidies — by competitively hybridizing differentially labeled **test (patient) DNA** and **reference (control) DNA** onto a solid substrate (glass slide or silicon chip) spotted with thousands of known genomic DNA sequences (BACs, PACs, oligonucleotides, or SNPs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** aCGH bridges the resolution gap between conventional **karyotyping** (~5–10 Mb resolution) and **FISH** (single locus), allowing detection of unbalanced chromosomal aberrations as small as **50–100 kb** across the entire genome in a single experiment.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Photo Copy Machine" Analogy for aCGH:**
>
> Imagine a student has two notebooks — one *original* (reference DNA) and one *suspected duplicate* (test/patient DNA). The student wants to know if any *pages are missing* (deletions) or *extra pages were inserted* (duplications) in the suspected duplicate.
>
> Instead of checking page-by-page manually, the student places both notebooks side-by-side on a scanner. Each page is labeled with a different colored highlighter (e.g., **green** for original, **red** for suspected). A computer then compares the intensity of each color at every "page location" (probe spot on the array).
> - **Equal green and red** = normal copy number (2 copies).
> - **More red than green** = duplication (extra copies in patient).
> - **More green than red** = deletion (missing copies in patient).
>
> This is precisely how aCGH works — a genome-wide, high-resolution "scanner" for genomic imbalances.

### Key Terminology Box

> [!NOTE]
> **Essential Vocabulary for aCGH:**
> - **CNV (Copy Number Variation):** Segments of DNA (>1 kb) present in variable copy numbers compared to a reference genome.
> - **Test DNA:** Patient/sample DNA under investigation.
> - **Reference DNA:** Pooled normal DNA from healthy individuals (typically 5–10 donors).
> - **Log2 Ratio:** The logarithmic measure of fluorescence intensity ratio (Cy5/Cy3 or test/reference); values around 0 indicate normal, +0.5 suggests duplication, -1.0 suggests deletion.
> - **BAC/PAC array:** Bacterial/P1-derived Artificial Chromosome clones (~100–300 kb) used as probes.
> - **Oligonucleotide array:** Short synthetic DNA probes (50–80 bp) offering higher resolution.

### Standard Metrics and Resolution

- **Typical Resolution:** 50–100 kb (oligo arrays), 1–2 Mb (BAC arrays).
- **Dynamic Range:** Detects single-copy gains/losses from 200 kb to entire chromosome arms.
- **Standard Labeling Dyes:** Cy3 (green) and Cy5 (red) — or in dual-color equivalents, Alexa 555/Alexa 647.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle of aCGH

The fundamental principle of aCGH rests on **quantitative two-color fluorescence comparison**. Equal amounts of test and reference genomic DNA are independently labeled with distinct fluorochromes and co-hybridized to a microarray containing immobilized DNA probes that represent specific genomic loci.

The **fluorescence intensity ratio** at each probe spot is a direct quantitative measure of the relative copy number of that locus in the test sample versus the reference.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

The aCGH workflow can be broken down into six discrete operational stages:

1. **Genomic DNA Extraction & Quality Assessment**
   - High-molecular-weight DNA is isolated from the test sample (patient) and a reference (normal control).
   - **Quality Check:** Purity assessed by A260/A280 ratio (target: **1.8–2.0**), integrity by gel electrophoresis.

2. **DNA Labeling (Random Priming or Nick Translation)**
   - Test DNA is labeled with **Cy5 (red)** fluorochrome.
   - Reference DNA is labeled with **Cy3 (green)** fluorochrome.
   - Alternatively, a **dye-swap** experiment is performed to control for dye bias.

3. **Hybridization to the Array**
   - Labeled DNAs are co-precipitated with Cot-1 DNA (blocks repetitive sequences) and applied onto the microarray slide.
   - Hybridization typically occurs at **65 °C for 16–24 hours** in a humidified chamber.

4. **Washing and Scanning**
   - Stringent washes remove non-specific binding.
   - The slide is scanned at two wavelengths (e.g., **532 nm for Cy3, 635 nm for Cy5**) using a dual-laser microarray scanner.

5. **Image Acquisition and Quantification**
   - Software (e.g., Agilent Feature Extraction, BlueFuse) converts raw fluorescence intensities into numerical values for each probe.

6. **Data Normalization and Interpretation**
   - Raw ratios are normalized (typically to the median log2 ratio of all autosomal probes = 0).
   - A moving-average window identifies regions of contiguous deviation.

## 2.3 The Log2 Ratio — Quantitative Core of aCGH

The most critical equation in aCGH interpretation is the **Logarithmic Fluorescence Intensity Ratio**:

$$R = \log_2 \left( \frac{I_{\text{test}}}{I_{\text{reference}}} \right)$$

Where:
- $I_{\text{test}}$ = fluorescence intensity of the test (patient) DNA at a given probe.
- $I_{\text{reference}}}$ = fluorescence intensity of the reference DNA at the same probe.
- $R$ = log2 ratio.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Thresholds (Standard KTU/Clinical Criteria):**
> - $R \approx 0$ → Normal copy number (2 copies in diploid genome).
> - $R \approx +0.58$ → Single-copy gain (3 copies → trisomy).
> - $R \approx -1.0$ → Single-copy loss (1 copy → monosomy).
> - $R \approx +1.0$ → Double gain (4 copies).
> - $R \approx -\infty$ → Homozygous deletion (0 copies).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula / Threshold | Interpretation | Unit |
|----------|--------------------|----------------|------|
| **Log2 Ratio** | $R = \log_2(I_{\text{test}} / I_{\text{ref}})$ | Quantitative copy number estimate | Dimensionless |
| **Normal CN** | $R \in [-0.25, +0.25]$ | Disomic (2 copies) | Ratio |
| **Single Gain** | $R \approx +0.58$ | Trisomic segment | Ratio |
| **Single Loss** | $R \approx -1.0$ | Monosomic segment | Ratio |
| **Homozygous Del** | $R < -2.0$ | Biallelic loss | Ratio |
| **A260/A280 Purity** | Target: 1.8–2.0 | DNA quality | Absorbance ratio |
| **Hybridization Temp** | 65 °C | Optimal stringency | °C |
| **Cot-1 DNA Block** | 25–100 µg | Suppresses Alu/LINE repeats | µg |
| **Resolution (Oligo)** | 50–100 kb | Modern high-density arrays | Kilobases |
| **Resolution (BAC)** | 1–2 Mb | Older lower-resolution arrays | Megabases |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering/Clinical Utility

- **Clinical Cytogenetics:** First-tier test for patients with intellectual disability, multiple congenital anomalies, and autism spectrum disorders.
- **Cancer Genomics:** Identifying oncogene amplifications (e.g., *MYCN* in neuroblastoma, *ERBB2* in breast cancer) and tumor suppressor deletions (e.g., *PTEN*, *CDKN2A*).
- **Prenatal Diagnostics:** Detection of pathogenic CNVs in chorionic villus samples and amniotic fluid.
- **Microbial Genetics:** Comparative genomic studies of pathogenic vs. non-pathogenic bacterial strains.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Expected Log2 Ratios for Copy Number States

The fundamental assumption of aCGH is that fluorescence intensity at a probe is **directly proportional to the number of DNA copies** at that locus in the hybridized sample.

Let:
- $n_{\text{test}}$ = number of copies of the locus in the test genome.
- $n_{\text{ref}}$ = number of copies of the locus in the reference genome (typically 2 in diploid).
- $k$ = a proportionality constant encompassing labeling efficiency, hybridization kinetics, and scanner gain.

**Step 1: Define fluorescence intensities.**

$$I_{\text{test}} = k \cdot n_{\text{test}}$$

$$I_{\text{ref}} = k \cdot n_{\text{ref}}$$

**Step 2: Form the intensity ratio.**

$$\frac{I_{\text{test}}}{I_{\text{ref}}} = \frac{k \cdot n_{\text{test}}}{k \cdot n_{\text{ref}}} = \frac{n_{\text{test}}}{n_{\text{ref}}}$$

The proportionality constant $k$ cancels out — this is the elegance of ratiometric measurement, as it inherently controls for systematic biases.

**Step 3: Apply base-2 logarithm.**

$$R = \log_2 \left( \frac{n_{\text{test}}}{n_{\text{ref}}} \right)$$

**Step 4: Evaluate for each copy number state (assuming $n_{\text{ref}} = 2$).**

*Case 1 — Normal disomy ($n_{\text{test}} = 2$):*

$$R = \log_2 \left( \frac{2}{2} \right) = \log_2(1) = 0$$

*Case 2 — Single-copy gain / Trisomy ($n_{\text{test}} = 3$):*

$$R = \log_2 \left( \frac{3}{2} \right) = \log_2(1.5)$$

Converting to log form:

$$R = \frac{\ln(1.5)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{0.4055}{0.6931} \approx 0.585$$

*Case 3 — Single-copy loss / Monosomy ($n_{\text{test}} = 1$):*

$$R = \log_2 \left( \frac{1}{2} \right) = \log_2(0.5) = -1.000$$

*Case 4 — Homozygous deletion ($n_{\text{test}} = 0$):*

$$R = \log_2 \left( \frac{0}{2} \right) = \log_2(0) = -\infty$$

*Case 5 — Double gain / Tetrasomy ($n_{\text{test}} = 4$):*

$$R = \log_2 \left( \frac{4}{2} \right) = \log_2(2) = 1.000$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Interpreting aCGH Output

A clinical aCGH report for a patient with unexplained intellectual disability returns the following normalized data on chromosome 15:

| Probe Region | Log2 Ratio (R) | Interpretation |
|--------------|----------------|----------------|
| 15q11.1–q11.2 | +0.61 | Copy number gain |
| 15q11.2–q13.1 | -0.98 | Copy number loss |
| 15q13.2–qter | -0.05 | Normal copy number |

**Diagnostic Inference:**

The contiguous loss spanning 15q11.2–q13.1 with $R \approx -1.0$ indicates a **single-copy deletion (monosomy)** of approximately 4–5 Mb in this region. This corresponds to the classic **Prader-Willi Syndrome (PWS)** or **Angelman Syndrome (AS)** critical region, depending on methylation status and parental origin.

**Calculation of deleted copy number from the ratio:**

$$R = \log_2 \left( \frac{n_{\text{test}}}{2} \right) = -0.98$$

$$2^{-0.98} = \frac{n_{\text{test}}}{2}$$

$$n_{\text{test}} = 2 \times 2^{-0.98} = 2 \times 0.508 = 1.016 \approx 1 \text{ copy}$$

This confirms a **hemizygous deletion** — consistent with a clinically significant 15q11.2–q13.1 microdeletion.

## 3.3 Mathematical Justification of Resolution Limits

The theoretical minimum detectable copy number change depends on the **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** of the microarray platform:

$$R_{\text{detectable}} \geq \mu_{\text{noise}} + z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma_{\text{noise}}$$

Where:
- $\mu_{\text{noise}}$ = mean log2 ratio of normal probes.
- $\sigma_{\text{noise}}$ = standard deviation of normal probes.
- $z_{\alpha}$ = z-score corresponding to confidence level (e.g., $z_{0.95} = 1.96$).

For a typical high-quality oligonucleotide array, $\sigma_{\text{noise}} \approx 0.1$, so the detection threshold is approximately $\pm 0.2$ log2 units, comfortably resolving the theoretical $+0.58$ and $-1.0$ signals.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the aCGH Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the end-to-end operational architecture of an aCGH experiment, from sample preparation to clinical interpretation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient Sample - Blood or Tissue] --> B[Genomic DNA Extraction]
    C[Reference Pool DNA - 5-10 Normal Donors] --> D[Reference DNA Extraction]
    B --> E[Quality Check - A260 A280 Ratio 1.8-2.0]
    D --> F[Quality Check - Reference]
    E --> G[Test DNA Labeling with Cy5 Red Dye]
    F --> H[Reference DNA Labeling with Cy3 Green Dye]
    G --> I[Co-precipitation with Cot-1 DNA]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Hybridization on Microarray Slide at 65 C 16-24 hours]
    J --> K[Stringent Washes to Remove Non-specific Binding]
    K --> L[Dual Laser Scanning at 532 nm and 635 nm]
    L --> M[Image Acquisition and Feature Extraction]
    M --> N[Normalization - Median Log2 Set to Zero]
    N --> O[Log2 Ratio Calculation per Probe]
    O --> P[Copy Number Calling Algorithm]
    P --> Q{Pathogenic CNV Detected}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Clinical Report with Deleted or Duplicated Region Coordinates]
    Q -- No --> S[Report - No Pathogenic CNV Identified]
    R --> T[Confirmation by FISH or MLPA if Required]
    S --> U[Archive Data for Future Reference]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Conceptual Detection Principle

The following diagram represents the **ratiometric comparison** principle that underpins aCGH, visualized at the level of fluorescence intensity at a single probe spot.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Patient DNA - 2 Copies Normal] --> P2[Cy5 Labeling - Red]
    P3[Reference DNA - 2 Copies Normal] --> P4[Cy3 Labeling - Green]
    P2 --> H[Hybridize to Probe Spot]
    P4 --> H
    H --> R1[Equal Red and Green Intensity]
    R1 --> O1[Log2 Ratio approximately 0 - Normal]
    
    P5[Patient DNA - 3 Copies Duplication] --> P6[Cy5 Labeling - Brighter Red]
    P3 --> P4
    P6 --> H2[Hybridize to Probe Spot]
    P4 --> H2
    H2 --> R2[Excess Red over Green]
    R2 --> O2[Log2 Ratio approximately plus 0.58 - Gain]
    
    P7[Patient DNA - 1 Copy Deletion] --> P8[Cy5 Labeling - Dim Red]
    P3 --> P4
    P8 --> H3[Hybridize to Probe Spot]
    P4 --> H3
    H3 --> R3[Excess Green over Red]
    R3 --> O3[Log2 Ratio approximately minus 1.0 - Loss]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Comparison of aCGH Resolution Spectrum

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[Conventional Karyotyping - Resolution 5-10 Mb] -->|Limited to| L[Whole Chromosome Anomalies]
    F[FISH - Targeted Locus] -->|Single Locus| M[Known Microdeletion Syndromes Only]
    A[Array CGH - Resolution 50-100 kb] --> N[Whole Genome - All CNVs Simultaneously]
    A --> O[Microdeletions]
    A --> P[Microduplications]
    A --> Q[Aneuploidies]
    A --> R[Unbalanced Translocations]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight for KTU Students:** The progression from karyotype → FISH → aCGH illustrates a classic **engineering trade-off** — sacrificing locus-specificity (FISH) and gross visualization (karyotype) in exchange for **unbiased, genome-wide resolution** (aCGH). This is analogous to the trade-off between spatial and frequency resolution in signal processing.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Array Comparative Genomic Hybridization (aCGH). Mention any two clinical applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Array Comparative Genomic Hybridization (aCGH) is a molecular cytogenetic technique used to detect genome-wide copy number variations (CNVs) by competitively hybridizing differentially labeled patient (test) and reference DNAs onto a microarray platform spotted with thousands of known genomic DNA probes. The fluorescence intensity ratio at each probe is quantified to identify microdeletions, microduplications, and aneuploidies. **(2 marks)**

**Clinical applications (any two):**
- Detection of pathogenic CNVs in patients with intellectual disability, multiple congenital anomalies, and autism spectrum disorders. **(1 mark)**
- Cancer genomic profiling — identifying oncogene amplifications (e.g., *MYCN*, *ERBB2*) and tumor suppressor deletions (e.g., *PTEN*, *CDKN2A*). **(1 mark)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is the significance of a Log2 ratio of +0.58 and -1.0 in aCGH interpretation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The log2 ratio is the base-2 logarithm of the ratio of test-to-reference fluorescence intensities at a given probe, providing a quantitative estimate of relative copy number at that locus. **(1 mark)**

- A log2 ratio of **+0.58** indicates a **single-copy gain (trisomy)** — three copies in the test sample compared to two in the reference, calculated as $\log_2(3/2) \approx 0.585$. **(1 mark)**
- A log2 ratio of **-1.0** indicates a **single-copy loss (monosomy)** — one copy in the test sample compared to two in the reference, calculated as $\log_2(1/2) = -1.0$. **(1 mark)**

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> Students commonly lose marks on aCGH questions by:
> 1. **Confusing the dyes** (forgetting which dye labels test vs. reference).
> 2. **Skipping the math** — many answers state the log2 thresholds but do not derive them; KTU 2024 marking schemes award marks for the derivation.
> 3. **Omitting Cot-1 DNA** — failure to mention blocking of repetitive sequences is a frequent 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Confusing resolution** — students often state resolution as 5 Mb when the question demands 50–100 kb.
> 5. **Writing aCGH and FISH interchangeably** — aCGH is genome-wide; FISH is locus-specific.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Describe the principle and complete step-by-step procedure of Array Comparative Genomic Hybridization (aCGH). Add a note on the interpretation of log2 ratios.**

**Part (a) — Principle and Procedure (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (3 marks):**
aCGH is based on the principle of **competitive co-hybridization** of two differentially labeled genomic DNA samples (test and reference) to a microarray of immobilized DNA probes representing the entire genome. The ratio of fluorescence intensities at each probe reflects the relative copy number of that locus. Regions with equal intensities in both samples indicate normal copy number; deviation indicates a copy number gain or loss.

**Procedure (4 marks):**

1. **DNA Extraction:** High-molecular-weight genomic DNA is isolated from the patient sample and a reference pool of normal individuals. Quality is assessed by A260/A280 ratio (1.8–2.0).
2. **Labeling:** Test DNA is labeled with **Cy5 (red)** and reference DNA with **Cy3 (green)** using random priming or nick translation. A dye-swap replicate may be performed to control for dye-incorporation bias.
3. **Hybridization:** Labeled DNAs are co-precipitated with **Cot-1 DNA** to block repetitive elements, denatured, and hybridized to the microarray slide at **65 °C for 16–24 hours**.
4. **Washing, Scanning, and Analysis:** Stringent washes remove non-specific binding. The slide is scanned at 532 nm (Cy3) and 635 nm (Cy5). Software extracts fluorescence intensities, computes the log2 ratio for each probe, normalizes data, and calls copy number states.

**Part (b) — Log2 Ratio Interpretation (7 marks)**

The log2 ratio is computed as $R = \log_2(I_{\text{test}} / I_{\text{ref}})$.

**Derivation of expected values:**

| Copy Number State | $n_{\text{test}}$ / $n_{\text{ref}}$ | Log2 Ratio | Interpretation |
|-------------------|--------------------------------------|------------|----------------|
| Normal disomy | 2/2 | 0.00 | Two copies (normal) |
| Single gain | 3/2 | +0.58 | Trisomy / duplication |
| Single loss | 1/2 | -1.00 | Monosomy / deletion |
| Double gain | 4/2 | +1.00 | Tetrasomy |
| Homozygous deletion | 0/2 | $-\infty$ | Biallelic loss |

**Clinical interpretation:** A contiguous stretch of probes with $R \geq +0.58$ defines a microduplication; $R \leq -1.0$ defines a microdeletion. **Deletions are generally more pathogenic** because they unmask recessive alleles and disrupt haploinsufficient genes.

**Advantages over karyotyping and FISH:**
- Higher resolution (50–100 kb vs. 5–10 Mb for karyotype).
- Genome-wide coverage in a single experiment (vs. locus-specific FISH).
- Objective, software-driven quantification.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Explain the construction of an aCGH microarray. Compare aCGH with conventional karyotyping and FISH with respect to resolution, scope, and clinical utility.**

**Part (a) — Microarray Construction and Comparison Table (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Microarray Construction (4 marks):**
aCGH microarrays are constructed by immobilizing thousands of DNA probes on a solid substrate (glass or silicon). The two main types are:
- **BAC/PAC arrays:** Large-insert clones (100–300 kb) spotted at ~1 Mb resolution across the genome. Older platform.
- **Oligonucleotide arrays:** Short synthetic probes (50–80 bp) printed at high density (e.g., Agilent 4x180K, 1M arrays), offering **50–100 kb resolution** and superior signal-to-noise ratio.

Modern arrays may also include **SNP probes** that simultaneously detect copy-neutral regions of homozygosity (ROH) and uniparental disomy (UPD) — a feature not possible in classical aCGH.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Karyotyping | FISH | aCGH |
|---------|-------------|------|------|
| **Resolution** | 5–10 Mb | Single locus (~100–200 kb) | 50–100 kb (oligo) / 1 Mb (BAC) |
| **Scope** | Whole genome | Targeted | Whole genome |
| **Detection of CNVs** | Only large | Only known target | All CNVs simultaneously |
| **Balanced translocations** | Yes | Yes (if probe spans) | **No** |
| **Mosaicism detection** | Limited | Limited (with manual scoring) | Quantitative |
| **Turnaround time** | 1–2 weeks | 1–3 days | 3–5 days |
| **Automation** | Low | Low | High |

**Part (b) — Clinical Utility and Limitations of aCGH (7 marks)**

**Clinical Utility (4 marks):**
- **First-tier diagnostic test** for unexplained intellectual disability, autism, and multiple congenital anomalies, replacing karyotyping in many labs.
- **Cancer cytogenetics:** Profiles solid tumors and hematological malignancies for clinically actionable copy number changes.
- **Prenatal diagnosis:** Detects pathogenic CNVs in chorionic villus samples and cell-free fetal DNA.

**Limitations of aCGH (3 marks):**
- **Cannot detect balanced rearrangements** (reciprocal translocations, inversions) — no copy number change occurs.
- **Cannot detect point mutations or small indels** — those require sequencing-based methods.
- **Cannot identify the chromosomal origin of supernumerary marker chromosomes** unless followed by FISH or karyotype.
- **Reference DNA choice can affect sensitivity** — ethnic-specific CNVs may be falsely flagged.
- **Copy-neutral events (ROH, UPD)** are not detected by classical aCGH — only by SNP arrays.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for aCGH (KTU 2024):**

- **Full Form:** Array Comparative Genomic Hybridization.
- **Principle:** Competitive co-hybridization of differentially labeled test and reference DNAs to a genome-wide DNA microarray.
- **Test DNA Dye:** **Cy5 (Red).**
- **Reference DNA Dye:** **Cy3 (Green).**
- **Blocking Agent:** **Cot-1 DNA** (suppresses Alu/LINE repeats).
- **Hybridization Conditions:** **65 °C for 16–24 hours.**
- **Resolution:** **50–100 kb (oligonucleotide arrays);** 1–2 Mb (BAC arrays).
- **Log2 Ratio Thresholds:**
  - $\approx 0$ → Normal (2 copies)
  - $\approx +0.58$ → Single gain (3 copies)
  - $\approx -1.0$ → Single loss (1 copy)
- **Major Advantage:** Genome-wide detection of unbalanced CNVs in one experiment; objective, quantitative.
- **Major Limitation:** **Cannot detect balanced rearrangements** (no copy number change) or point mutations.
- **Differential Diagnosis:** Differs from FISH in being **unbiased/genome-wide**; differs from karyotype in **higher resolution and objectivity**; differs from sequencing in focusing on **CNVs, not base-level mutations**.
- **Clinical Application:** First-tier test for intellectual disability, multiple congenital anomalies, autism, and cancer cytogenetics.
- **SNP Arrays:** A variant of aCGH that additionally detects **copy-neutral loss of heterozygosity (LOH)** and uniparental disomy.
- **Mathematical Core:** $R = \log_2(I_{\text{test}} / I_{\text{ref}})$ — remember that the proportionality constant cancels, making the ratio inherently self-normalizing.
- **Pitfall:** Students must NOT confuse aCGH with sequencing — aCGH detects *gains and losses*; sequencing detects *base-level variants*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🧬 Multi-Color Fluorescence In Situ Hybridization (M-FISH)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Multi-Color Fluorescence In Situ Hybridization (M-FISH)** is an advanced molecular cytogenetic technique that simultaneously hybridizes multiple, whole-chromosome painting probes, each tagged with a spectrally distinct fluorochrome (or a unique combinatorial ratio of fluorochromes), onto a single metaphase spread. It enables the **simultaneous, differential painting of all 24 human chromosomes (22 autosomes + X + Y) in a single hybridization reaction**, allowing the visualization and identification of complex chromosomal rearrangements, translocations, and aneuploidies that cannot be resolved by conventional banding or single-color FISH.

---

## 🔭 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

Imagine you are an air traffic controller looking down at a busy airport at night. Each airplane (representing a chromosome) has its own unique set of colored landing lights (fluorochromes). Instead of one color per plane, you assign each plane a **unique combination of red, green, blue, yellow, and cyan lights** in specific ratios. A Boeing 737 might have `2 red + 1 green`, an Airbus 320 might have `1 red + 3 green`, and so on.

By scanning the sky with a hyperspectral camera (the microscope + filter wheel system), you can decode every plane's identity from the unique "fingerprint" of its light pattern — even if 24 planes are landing at the exact same instant. **M-FISH does exactly this with chromosomes inside a single cell nucleus.**

| Component | Real-World Analogy | M-FISH Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Airplanes | Chromosomes | 22 autosomes + X + Y |
| Colored lights | Fluorochrome labels | Fluorophores (e.g., FITC, Cy3, Cy5, Texas Red) |
| Light combinations | Unique color codes | Combinatorial labeling ratios |
| Hyperspectral camera | Imaging system | Epifluorescence microscope + CCD + filter wheel |
| Airport tarmac | Metaphase spread | Fixed chromosomes on glass slide |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Conventional FISH uses **1 fluorochrome = 1 target** (limited multiplexing). M-FISH uses **combinatorial fluorochrome ratios = N targets**, where $N = 2^n - 1$ (where $n$ is the number of distinct fluorophores used).

---

## 🎯 Why M-FISH? The Limitations of Conventional Cytogenetics

Before diving deeper, it is critical to understand *why* this technique was invented in the late 1990s (Schröck et al., 1996 — SKY; Speicher et al., 1996 — M-FISH).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:** Standard **G-banding** (resolution ~5–10 Mb) and **single-color chromosome painting** (1 fluorochrome/target) **cannot resolve:**
> 1. **Cryptic translocations** (too small for band-level resolution)
> 2. **Complex karyotypes** (e.g., in cancer cells with >3 translocations)
> 3. **Marker chromosomes** (unknown origin)
> 4. **Insertions and small duplications** invisible to banding
> 5. **Interphase analysis** of non-dividing cells

M-FISH solves all five by assigning each chromosome a **unique spectral "barcode."**

---

## 🧪 Fundamental Building Blocks of M-FISH

To master M-FISH for KTU exams, you must know the **5 essential components** of the workflow:

### 1️⃣ The Probe Set
- **Whole Chromosome Painting (WCP) probes** for all 24 chromosomes
- Each probe is a **DNA library** (originally from flow-sorted or microdissected chromosomes)
- Probes are **directly labeled** (fluorochrome attached to dUTP/dCTP) or **indirectly labeled** (haptens like biotin/digoxigenin, then detected with fluorochrome-conjugated antibodies)

### 2️⃣ The Fluorochromes
The classical 5-fluorophore set (Speicher et al., 1996):

| Fluorophore | Excitation Max (nm) | Emission Max (nm) | Color Channel |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Spectrum Aqua** | 349 | 447 | Blue |
| **FITC** | 494 | 519 | Green |
| **Cy3** | 550 | 570 | Orange-Red |
| **Texas Red** | 595 | 615 | Red |
| **Cy5** | 650 | 670 | Far-Red |

### 3️⃣ The Microscope
- **Epifluorescence microscope** (100× oil-immersion objective, NA ≥ 1.3)
- **Automated filter wheel** with 5+ bandpass filter sets
- **Cooled CCD camera** (high quantum efficiency, low noise)
- **Motorized stage** (for automated scanning)

### 4️⃣ The Image Analysis Software
- **Dedicated M-FISH software** (e.g., Applied Spectral Imaging's SkyVision, MetaSystems' Isis, Leica CW4000)
- Performs **pixel-by-pixel spectral classification** using a combinatorial algorithm
- Assigns a **pseudocolor** to each chromosome based on its fluorochrome signature

### 5️⃣ The Combinatorial Labeling Algorithm
- This is the **mathematical heart of M-FISH** (covered in Section 2)

# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis — How M-FISH Works

## 📐 The Combinatorial Labeling Principle

The genius of M-FISH lies in a simple combinatorial mathematical fact:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** With $n$ distinct fluorophores, each chromosome painting probe is labeled with a **unique combination of fluorochrome ratios**. The number of unique, distinguishable targets is:
> $$N_{\text{targets}} = 2^n - 1$$
> For $n = 5$ fluorophores → $N = 2^5 - 1 = \mathbf{31}$ unique color codes.
> This is more than enough to identify all **24 human chromosomes**.

Each chromosome is assigned a **binary code** (presence = 1, absence = 0 of each fluorochrome), excluding the all-zero code (no fluorochrome = no detection).

### Binary Encoding Table (5-Fluorophore M-FISH)

| Chromosome | Aqua | FITC | Cy3 | Texas Red | Cy5 | Binary Code | Decimal |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 10000 | 16 |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 01000 | 8 |
| 3 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 00100 | 4 |
| 4 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 00010 | 2 |
| 5 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 00001 | 1 |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| 22 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 11111 | 31 |
| X | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 11011 | 27 |
| Y | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 10110 | 22 |

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, the **fluorochrome ratio** (not just presence/absence) is varied continuously, yielding theoretically **unlimited** distinguishable targets from just 5 fluorophores. This is called **ratio labeling**.

---

## 🔄 Step-by-Step M-FISH Protocol (Operational Logic)

The M-FISH workflow can be broken into **6 sequential phases**:

### Phase 1: Slide Preparation
- **Lymphocyte culture** (peripheral blood) → arrested at metaphase with **colchicine** (0.1 µg/mL, 30 min)
- **Hypotonic treatment** (0.075 M KCl, 37°C, 15 min) → swells cells
- **Fixation** (3:1 methanol:acetic acid)
- **Drop** onto cleaned glass slide → air-dry
- **Aging** (overnight at 37°C or 1 hr at 60°C)

### Phase 2: Probe Mixture Preparation
- **24 WCP probes** are pooled
- Each probe is labeled with a **unique combination** of 5 fluorochromes
- **Cot-1 DNA** (suppression of repetitive sequences) is added
- **Master mix**: probe cocktail + hybridization buffer (formamide, dextran sulfate, SSC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Cot-1 DNA?** Chromosome painting probes contain **repetitive sequences (Alu, LINEs)** that would hybridize non-specifically to all chromosomes. **Cot-1 DNA** competes for these repeats, blocking non-specific binding. This is called **Chromosome In Situ Suppression (CISS)**.

### Phase 3: Denaturation & Hybridization
- **Slide denaturation**: 70% formamide / 2× SSC at **70°C for 2 min**
- **Probe denaturation**: 75°C for 5 min
- **Co-hybridization**: 37°C, **16–48 hours** in a humidified chamber
- During hybridization, probes bind their **complementary chromosomal DNA** in situ

### Phase 4: Post-Hybridization Washes
- **Stringent washes**: 50% formamide / 2× SSC at 42°C (3 × 5 min) → removes weakly bound probes
- **Final wash**: 2× SSC at 42°C
- **Counterstain**: **DAPI (4',6-diamidino-2-phenylindole)** in antifade mounting medium → stains all DNA blue for chromosome identification by banding pattern

### Phase 5: Image Acquisition
- **Sequential image capture** through 5+ filter sets (one per fluorophore)
- For each field of view, **5 monochrome images** are captured (one per fluorophore)
- DAPI image is captured separately (often first, for banding reference)
- **Pixel registration** is critical — all 5 images must be perfectly aligned

### Phase 6: Image Analysis & Pseudocolor Assignment
- The software performs **pixel-by-pixel spectral unmixing**
- Each pixel's intensity across the 5 channels is compared to a **reference library** of known fluorochrome signatures
- The chromosome is assigned a **pseudocolor** based on its dominant code
- A **classified karyotype** is generated

---

## 📊 The Mathematical Engine: Spectral Classification

The classification algorithm uses a **distance metric** in 5-dimensional fluorophore space. For each pixel $p$, the algorithm computes:

$$d_p = \min_{c \in \{1, 2, \ldots, 24\}} \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{5} \left( I_{p,i} - R_{c,i} \right)^2}$$

Where:
- $d_p$ = spectral distance from pixel $p$ to the nearest chromosome reference
- $I_{p,i}$ = measured intensity of pixel $p$ in fluorochrome channel $i$
- $R_{c,i}$ = reference intensity for chromosome $c$ in fluorochrome channel $i$
- The pixel is **assigned to chromosome $c$** with the minimum distance

> [!NOTE]
> **Thresholding:** Pixels with $d_p > T$ (a software-defined threshold) are classified as **"unclassified"** — useful for detecting centromeres, heterochromatin, or hybridization artifacts.

---

| # | Concept | Formula / Value | Meaning / Application |

| 4 | **Beer-Lambert Law (signal attenuation)** | $A = \varepsilon c l$ | Fluorochrome signal loss through optical path |
| 5 | **Fluorescence quantum yield** | $\Phi_F = \frac{k_r}{k_r + k_{nr}}$ | Efficiency of fluorochrome emission |

| 14 | **Hypotonic solution** | $0.075 \, M \, KCl$, $37°C$, $15 \, min$ | Swells nuclei for spreading chromosomes |
| 15 | **Spectral classification distance** | $d_p = \min_c \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{5} (I_{p,i} - R_{c,i})^2}$ | Pixel assignment to chromosome |

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🧪 Derivation 1: Combinatorial Labeling Capacity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Frequency:** This is one of the highest-yield KTU questions. You will be asked to compute the multiplexing capacity for a given $n$, or to justify why 5 fluorophores suffice for 24 chromosomes.

### Problem Setup
You are a molecular cytogeneticist tasked with designing an M-FISH panel. You have $n$ distinct fluorophores available. Each chromosome painting probe must be labeled with a **unique combination** of these fluorophores. How many chromosomes can you distinguish?

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** For each of the $n$ fluorophores, a probe can either:
- **Include** the fluorophore (binary state $= 1$), OR
- **Exclude** the fluorochrome (binary state $= 0$)

This gives $2$ possible states per fluorophore.

**Step 2:** For $n$ fluorophores, the total number of unique binary combinations is:

$$N_{\text{total}} = \underbrace{2 \times 2 \times \cdots \times 2}_{n \text{ times}} = 2^n$$

**Step 3:** However, the combination where **all fluorophores are absent** (binary code $000\ldots0$) corresponds to an **unlabeled probe** — it produces no detectable signal and cannot be assigned to any chromosome. We must **exclude this degenerate case**:

$$N_{\text{usable}} = 2^n - 1$$

**Step 4:** Verification for the standard M-FISH case ($n = 5$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{usable}} &= 2^5 - 1 \\
&= 32 - 1 \\
&= 31
\end{aligned}
$$

Since 31 ≥ 24 (the number of human chromosomes: 22 autosomes + X + Y), **5 fluorophores are sufficient**.

**Step 5:** Generalization: What if we need to identify $K$ chromosomes? The minimum number of fluorophores required is:

$$n_{\min} = \lceil \log_2(K + 1) \rceil$$

For $K = 24$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\min} &= \lceil \log_2(24 + 1) \rceil \\
&= \lceil \log_2(25) \rceil \\
&= \lceil 4.644 \rceil \\
&= 5
\end{aligned}
$$

This confirms that 5 fluorophores is the **theoretical minimum** for human karyotyping.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** Using 6 or 7 fluorophores gives $2^6 - 1 = 63$ or $2^7 - 1 = 127$ codes — useful for **cross-species comparative painting** (e.g., human + mouse = 48 chromosomes).

---

## 📐 Derivation 2: Spectral Distance Classification Algorithm

### Problem Setup
After image acquisition, each pixel $p$ in the metaphase spread has been measured across $n$ fluorophore channels, producing an intensity vector:

$$\vec{I}_p = (I_{p,1}, I_{p,2}, \ldots, I_{p,n})$$

Each chromosome $c$ has a known reference signature vector:

$$\vec{R}_c = (R_{c,1}, R_{c,2}, \ldots, R_{c,n})$$

We need a decision rule to assign pixel $p$ to one of the 24 chromosomes.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Define the **spectral residual vector** as the difference between measured and reference intensities:

$$\vec{\delta}_{p,c} = \vec{I}_p - \vec{R}_c = (I_{p,1} - R_{c,1}, I_{p,2} - R_{c,2}, \ldots, I_{p,n} - R_{c,n})$$

**Step 2:** Compute the **Euclidean norm** (L2 distance) of this residual:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{p,c} &= \| \vec{\delta}_{p,c} \|_2 \\
&= \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( I_{p,i} - R_{c,i} \right)^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Find the chromosome with the **minimum spectral distance**:

$$c^* = \arg\min_{c \in \{1, 2, \ldots, 24\}} d_{p,c}$$

**Step 4:** Apply a **threshold test**. If the minimum distance exceeds a threshold $T$, classify the pixel as unclassified (artifact, centromere, etc.):

$$\text{Class}(p) = \begin{cases} c^* & \text{if } d_{p,c^*} \leq T \\ \text{Unclassified} & \text{if } d_{p,c^*} > T \end{cases}$$

**Step 5:** Numerical example (simplified, 2 fluorophores for clarity):

Suppose $n = 2$ (FITC and Cy3). Consider two chromosomes with reference signatures:

$$\vec{R}_1 = (100, 20) \quad \text{(Chr 1 — mostly FITC)}$$

$$\vec{R}_2 = (30, 100) \quad \text{(Chr 2 — mostly Cy3)}$$

A measured pixel has $\vec{I}_p = (95, 25)$. Compute distances:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{p,1} &= \sqrt{(95-100)^2 + (25-20)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{25 + 25} \\
&= \sqrt{50} \\
&\approx 7.07
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{p,2} &= \sqrt{(95-30)^2 + (25-100)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{4225 + 5625} \\
&= \sqrt{9850} \\
&\approx 99.25
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $d_{p,1} < d_{p,2}$, pixel $p$ is assigned to **Chromosome 1**. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This algorithm is the same one used in clinical M-FISH software** (e.g., Applied Spectral Imaging's SKY). Students should be able to describe it conceptually in a 7-mark KTU question.

---

## 🧬 Derivation 3: Fluorescence Intensity and the Beer-Lambert Law

### Problem Setup
A fluorochrome in the M-FISH probe mixture absorbs excitation light and emits fluorescence. The **emission intensity** at the detector depends on the concentration of the fluorochrome, the optical path, and the quantum yield.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The **absorbance** $A$ of the fluorochrome solution follows the **Beer-Lambert Law**:

$$A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon$ = **molar extinction coefficient** ($M^{-1} cm^{-1}$)
- $c$ = fluorochrome concentration (M)
- $l$ = optical path length (cm)

**Step 2:** The **transmittance** $T$ (fraction of light passing through) is:

$$T = 10^{-A} = 10^{-\varepsilon c l}$$

**Step 3:** The **fluorescence emission intensity** $I_F$ is proportional to the **absorbed light**:

$$I_F = I_0 \cdot (1 - T) \cdot \Phi_F$$

Where:
- $I_0$ = incident excitation intensity
- $\Phi_F$ = **quantum yield** (probability of emission after absorption)
- $(1 - T)$ = fraction of light absorbed

**Step 4:** Substituting:

$$I_F = I_0 \cdot \left(1 - 10^{-\varepsilon c l}\right) \cdot \Phi_F$$

**Step 5:** For dilute solutions ($\varepsilon c l \ll 1$), use the Taylor approximation $10^{-x} \approx 1 - x \ln(10)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_F &\approx I_0 \cdot \varepsilon c l \ln(10) \cdot \Phi_F \\
&= I_0 \cdot \Phi_F \cdot \varepsilon \cdot \ln(10) \cdot c \cdot l
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key insight:** $I_F$ is **linearly proportional to concentration $c$** in the dilute limit — this is the regime where M-FISH quantification is most reliable.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall (KTU 2024 Examiner Note):** At high fluorochrome concentrations (e.g., very thick probe deposits), $I_F$ **saturates** and becomes non-linear. This causes **misclassification** in M-FISH. Always use freshly prepared, dilute probe mixes.

---

## 📈 Derivation 4: Resolution Limit of M-FISH

### Problem Setup
What is the smallest chromosomal region that M-FISH can resolve? This depends on the **microscope's optical resolution** (Rayleigh criterion) and the **probe size**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The **Rayleigh resolution limit** of a fluorescence microscope is:

$$R = \frac{0.61 \cdot \lambda}{NA}$$

Where:
- $\lambda$ = emission wavelength
- $NA$ = numerical aperture of the objective

**Step 2:** For a typical M-FISH setup ($\lambda = 570$ nm, $NA = 1.3$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{0.61 \times 570 \text{ nm}}{1.3} \\
&= \frac{347.7}{1.3} \text{ nm} \\
&\approx 267.5 \text{ nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** This corresponds to a chromosomal region of approximately **2–5 Mb** (since 1 Mb of DNA ≈ 0.34 µm in a metaphase chromosome).

**Step 4:** Comparison with other techniques:

| Technique | Resolution | Type |
|---|---|---|
| G-banding | 5–10 Mb | Banding pattern |
| Conventional FISH | 100 kb – 1 Mb | Single-locus probe |
| **M-FISH (whole chromosome paint)** | **2–5 Mb** | **Whole chromosome** |
| Fiber-FISH | 1–500 kb | Extended DNA fibers |
| CGH / array-CGH | 50–100 kb | Copy number |
| NGS / WGS | Single base pair | Sequencing |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** M-FISH is **best for whole-chromosome identification**, not for sub-megabase structural variants. For those, use **fiber-FISH, array-CGH, or sequencing**.
